text: Research on scientific document summarization increasingly leverages structural signals from citations and rhetorical roles. Miller et al. (2017) proposed a citation graph algorithm that ranks sentences using PageRank variants in biomedical articles. Radev and Erkan (2004) studied centroid-based summarization with lexical cohesion features across news datasets. Narayan et al. (2018) trained a neural extractive model with policy gradients on CNN/DailyMail, and Collins et al. (2018) introduced discourse-aware sentence embeddings for structuring scientific abstracts and evaluating cross-domain generalization robustness. This paper investigates learning to generate section-aware summaries with contrastive objectives and sparse attention.

text: Fairness in recommender systems has evolved from user-centric accuracy to multi-stakeholder considerations spanning consumers, providers, and platforms. Yao and Huang (2017) formulated individual and group fairness for collaborative filtering and reported disparate impacts under ranking losses. Ekstrand et al. (2018) examined exposure disparities in movie recommendation datasets and proposed metrics for provider-side equity. Beutel et al. (2019) studied fairness-aware ranking with pairwise regularization, Burke (2017) discussed stakeholder objectives in recommenders, and Mehrotra et al. (2018) evaluated diversification strategies for balancing satisfaction. We study exposure-aware re-ranking under uncertainty with budgeted interventions.

text: Multimodal emotion recognition seeks to infer affect by integrating heterogeneous signals across time. Poria et al. (2017) introduced a multimodal framework combining text, audio, and visual cues using features for sentiment detection. Zadeh et al. (2018) released CMU-MOSEI dataset and studied tensor fusion to capture cross-modal dynamics in longer videos. Majumder et al. (2019) modeled conversational emotion with states, Khorram et al. (2019) analyzed robustness under missing modalities, and Tsai et al. (2019) proposed factorized multimodal transformers. We investigate alignment-free adapters that handle modality dropout and asynchronous cues.

text: Program synthesis from natural language has drawn attention due to applications in data transformation and education. Yin and Neubig (2018) presented a neural model for mapping utterances to abstract syntax trees in semantic parsing. Chen et al. (2018) built a sketch learning system to automatically generate code with high-level guidance. Kalyan et al. (2018) explored DSL induction for data wrangling, Rabinovich et al. (2017) proposed abstract syntax networks for code generation, and Lachaux et al. (2020) studied translation of languages. We target low-resource semantic parsing with retrieval-augmented pretraining.

text: Federated learning enables privacy-minded training by aggregating updates from many clients. McMahan et al. (2017) introduced federated averaging to train models across devices without centralizing raw data. Geyer et al. (2018) studied client-level differential privacy in production systems and reported accuracy trade-offs under clipping and noise. Konečný et al. (2016) analyzed communication-efficient SGD via update compression, Li et al. (2020) examined heterogeneity with FedProx, and Truex et al. (2019) surveyed privacy attacks against distributed learning. Our work develops adaptive noise scheduling under client drift and participation volatility.

text: Research on cross-lingual abstractive summarization has expanded as multilingual corpora and models have matured. Early systems relied on pipeline translation followed by monolingual summarization with compounding errors. Recent approaches leverage multilingual encoders to share representations and reduce cascading failures. Prior work also examines how training objectives shape faithfulness and compression in cross-lingual settings. Smith et al. (2018) present a dual-encoder model for cross-lingual summarization using shared subword units across languages. Lee and Park (2019) explore multilingual pointer-generators trained with aligned headlines from news portals. Brown et al. (2020) investigate curriculum schedules for machine translation pretraining before summarization, and Nguyen (2021) proposes a coverage penalty tuned on ROUGE to mitigate content omissions in long outputs. Zhao et al. (2022) study domain shifts in cross-lingual news, focusing on lead bias patterns. These threads form a diverse backdrop but leave open questions about aligning content fidelity with target-language style. We address this gap by modeling source salience under translation noise with agreement-regularized attention. Our experiments on XL-Sum and WikiLingua demonstrate consistent gains and controlled abstraction.

text: Algorithmic fairness in recommender systems raises questions about user welfare, provider equity, and platform objectives. Exposure in ranked lists directly translates to opportunities and can instantiate systemic disparities. A growing body of work formalizes these trade-offs using constrained optimization and simulation-based audits. Offline evaluation practices are being revisited to capture long-term dynamics beyond one-shot accuracy. We focus on multi-stakeholder recommendation where exposure, satisfaction, and catalog health must be balanced. Yao and Huang (2017) define fairness in recommender rankings through disparate exposure metrics for protected groups. Biega et al. (2018) introduce amortized fairness to control cumulative attention allocated to items over time. Mehrotra et al. (2020) analyze user-side utility trade-offs when interventions alter ranking positions, and Singh and Joachims (2019) study policy optimization under exposure constraints for search. Ekstrand et al. (2022) examine evaluation protocols and dataset practices in fairness research for recommenders in production contexts. Despite progress, aligning interventions with product goals and stakeholder preferences remains challenging in practice. Our work provides a learning-to-rank approach with counterfactual exposure control and interpretable budgets.

text: Neural program synthesis has advanced rapidly with the rise of large-scale pretraining and retrieval-augmented decoding. Tasks vary from toy DSLs to real-world libraries where specifications are partial and noisy. Evaluation increasingly emphasizes functional correctness under unit tests rather than exact match alone. We situate our work at the intersection of constrained decoding and specification augmentation for repository-scale generation. Balog et al. (2017) propose DeepCoder to learn to predict code components from input-output examples. Devlin et al. (2019) pretrain BERT and show improvements on code-related classification through transfer. Austin et al. (2021) study program synthesis with transformers trained on paired specifications, and Chen et al. (2021) release Codex for general-purpose code generation. Le et al. (2022) study execution-guided decoding for semantic parsing and report gains on compositional benchmarks using lightweight executors during beam search inference. While these developments are promising, their integration with repository context and constraints remains underexplored. We propose a planner-executor that conditions on file graphs and dependency manifests to reduce hallucinated imports. Experiments on HumanEval, MBPP, and RepoBench show consistent functional gains and fewer runtime errors.

text: Predicting molecular properties from structure benefits from graph neural architectures that respect chemical inductive biases. Early methods treated SMILES strings with sequence models that struggle with invariances and locality. Message passing frameworks translate local chemical interactions into node and edge updates suitable for downstream tasks. Pretraining on large unlabeled corpora has also emerged as a promising direction for data-scarce endpoints. Duvenaud et al. (2015) propose molecular graph convolutions to learn fingerprints end-to-end. Gilmer et al. (2017) generalize message passing to unify neural graph models for quantum chemistry. Kearnes et al. (2016) investigate weave modules that interleave atom and pair features, and Yang et al. (2019) introduce D-MPNN focusing on directed bond updates. Rong et al. (2020) present Grover with self-supervised objectives on large compound corpora using motif prediction and context recovery for improved downstream accuracy performance. These contributions highlight varied design choices but leave open how to systematically integrate reaction knowledge into learned representations. We study template-aware message passing that conditions on inferred reaction centers and electron flow hints. Our evaluation on MoleculeNet and proprietary assays indicates stronger generalization, especially under scaffold splits.

text: Low-resource named entity recognition suffers from sparse mentions, label imbalance, and domain shift between annotation and deployment. Augmentation strategies attempt to expand coverage without costly labeling while preserving entity boundaries and context. Beyond surface edits, contextualized generators can tailor variants that maintain ontology constraints. We review approaches spanning lexical substitution, back-translation, and semi-supervised training. Fadaee et al. (2017) perform data augmentation for NMT by replacing rare words with suitable synonyms in context. Wei and Zou (2019) propose EDA with simple operations to expand limited classification datasets. Dai and Adel (2020) investigate contextual word replacements for sequence labeling tasks, and Ding et al. (2021) generate mention variants to improve NER robustness. Xie et al. (2020) present back-translation as noise for semi-supervised learning with notable gains on text classification and tagging benchmarks. These techniques inform our setting but do not address span-level constraints that protect type consistency. We introduce mention-conditioned paraphrasing with constrained decoding and automatic type validation. Results on CoNLL03, WikiGold, and BioNLP show improvements in F1 and robustness across domains.

text: This section reviews prior research on controllable text simplification for low-resource domains. Early rule-based systems relied on handcrafted lexicons and syntactic transformations to reduce complexity. Statistical methods introduced phrase tables and language models to approximate content preservation under length constraints. Neural approaches shifted the focus to encoder–decoder architectures with attention and copy mechanisms. Control tokens and style embeddings have been used to steer simplification level and lexical choice. Domain adaptation remains challenging when source and target distributions differ markedly. Researchers have addressed limited supervision using weak labels and pseudo-parallel corpora. Smith et al. (2019) presented a pointer-generator model with explicit length control for news simplification and controllable paraphrases for readers. Gao and Huang (2020) collected a large pseudo-parallel corpus by back-translating encyclopedic text with constrained decoding. Nisioi et al. (2017) trained on Simple Wikipedia with reinforcement learning to reward readability scores. Martin et al. (2022) proposed controllable sentence splitting using semantic role labeling for technical manuals with improved terminology handling and fewer deletions in long sequences. Despite progress, few studies examine calibration of control strength against domain shift. Our work targets scientific protocols, where precision and readability must be balanced. We introduce a curriculum over control values that aligns with difficulty-aware sampling. The method integrates uncertainty estimates to decide when to trust synthetic pairs. We evaluate on biomedical and materials science datasets with expert-annotated references. Metrics include SARI, BLEU, factuality checks, and human judgments of adequacy. The following subsections detail datasets, baselines, and implementation choices.

text: Urban traffic forecasting has become central to intelligent transportation systems and congestion mitigation. Traditional time-series models capture periodicity but struggle with network-level spatial dependencies. Graph neural networks address topology-aware learning by encoding road segments as nodes and flows as features. Recent methods combine temporal convolutions with dynamic adjacency estimation for robustness to incidents. External context such as weather and events can refine predictions through learned gating mechanisms. Transfer across cities requires domain adaptation to heterogeneous sensor layouts. We study cross-city generalization when labeled data is scarce at the target. We propose a meta-learning approach that builds structure-agnostic temporal modules with fast adaptation. Our framework unifies episodic training with graph coarsening to reduce overfitting to dense regions. Yu et al. (2018) introduced STGCN with temporal gated convolutions on fixed road graphs and benchmarked on the METR-LA dataset. Li et al. (2018) proposed DCRNN to model directed diffusion with sequence-to-sequence learning. Wu et al. (2019) designed Graph WaveNet using adaptive adjacency and dilated causal convolutions for long horizons with improved stability under sparse sensors and noisy measurements conditions. Bai et al. (2020) learned spatiotemporal attention over residual blocks to enhance responsiveness during peak traffic. Unlike prior single-city benchmarks, we evaluate on five metropolitan networks with differing coverage. The method estimates uncertainty to flag low-confidence intervals for downstream routing. We report accuracy under sensor failures using synthetic dropout patterns. The analysis includes ablations on adaptation steps, coarsening levels, and context encoders. We release code, datasets, and scripts to encourage reproducibility.

(2019) designed Graph WaveNet using adaptive adjacency and dilated causal convolutions for long horizons with improved stability under sparse sensors and noisy measurements conditions. Bai et al. (2020) learned spatiotemporal attention over residual blocks to enhance responsiveness during peak traffic.

text: Extracting biomedical relations from literature is essential for curated knowledge graphs and clinical decision support. Early pattern-based systems relied on dependency paths and trigger lexicons. Distant supervision introduced scalable labeling but suffered from noisy alignments to knowledge bases. Neural models with attention improved robustness to varied syntax and terminology. Pretrained language models further advanced performance through domain-specific corpora and continual pretraining. Peng et al. (2019) created BioBERT by pretraining BERT on PubMed abstracts and PMC full text and reporting gains on named entity recognition and question answering. Beltagy et al. (2019) developed SciBERT with a scientific vocabulary to strengthen domain representations broadly. Yoon et al. (2020) introduced ClinicalBERT fine-tuned on clinical notes for hospital outcomes prediction in downstream clinical tasks. Lee et al. (2020) released PubMedBERT trained from scratch on biomedical corpora achieving strong relation extraction scores. Nevertheless, entity typing errors and implicit relations remain challenging on full-text articles. We propose a retrieval-augmented encoder that conditions on sentence-level evidence and discourse cues. The architecture integrates cross-sentence attention with section-aware embeddings. A calibrator mitigates label noise by down-weighting ambiguous training instances. We curate new test sets for drug–gene and variant–disease relations with expert validation. Evaluation includes precision–recall curves and error categories for negation and speculation. We analyze generalization across journals and publication years. Ablations isolate contributions from retrieval, calibration, and section encoding. Our code and models will be released under a permissive license.

text: Risk prediction on tabular data underpins decisions in finance, healthcare, and public policy. Gradient-boosted trees and deep tabular models offer strong accuracy across heterogeneous features. Yet opacity of complex predictors hinders trust, monitoring, and compliance. Global feature importance can mislead when interactions and correlations dominate outcomes. Local explanation methods quantify case-level contributions for actionable review. Post-hoc techniques risk mismatches with the underlying model behavior. Inherently interpretable models trade expressivity for transparency in many settings. We study faithful, test-time efficient explanations integrated into training objectives. Our formulation penalizes non-monotone effects over regulated attributes and encourages sparse attributions. The approach separates stable signals from spurious correlations using invariance constraints across environments. We position our method within prior advances in explainability for structured data. Lundberg and Lee (2017) unified additive feature attribution methods under SHAP values for local explanations using game-theoretic principles to allocate contributions consistently across feature coalitions. Ribeiro et al. (2016) introduced LIME to fit sparse linear models around individual predictions and demonstrated faithfulness on diverse classifiers and tabular benchmarks with human-subject evaluations. Zeiler and Fergus (2014) analyzed convolutional networks with deconvolutional visualizations to interpret filters highlighting feature saliency in widely used recognition datasets and settings. In contrast to proxy explanations, our technique guarantees consistency with the predictive gradients. We assess fidelity on semi-synthetic benchmarks with known ground-truth mechanisms. User studies with analysts measure comprehensibility and decision latency under time pressure. Implementation and datasets are released for public scrutiny.

text: Generating natural language summaries for source code aids maintenance and onboarding in large repositories. Earlier techniques built on information retrieval with identifier matching and template filling. Neural encoder–decoder models capture structural cues from abstract syntax trees and data flow graphs. Pretraining on massive code corpora provides strong initializations for downstream summarization. Low-resource projects still lack sufficient examples to adapt to domain-specific naming and idioms. Prompt-based learning with large language models offers an alternative path when labels are scarce. Yet prompt sensitivity and domain mismatch can produce brittle outputs and hallucinated descriptions. We investigate few-shot adaptation with demonstration selection grounded in static analysis features. Alon et al. (2019) proposed code2seq to learn from AST paths for method name prediction and demonstrated benefits on multiple benchmarks beyond Java. LeClair and McMillan (2019) released a large Java dataset for code summarization with standardized splits including API usage annotations and method-level comments to facilitate learning. Ahmad et al. (2020) built Transformer-based models with copy mechanisms and structural encoders for improved summaries with evaluations spanning cross-project transfer and robustness to identifier obfuscation and noise. The approach augments prompts with retrieved code exemplars and call graph contexts. We design an uncertainty-aware reranker to filter unreliable generations during inference. Automatic metrics are complemented by human ratings of correctness, fluency, and usefulness. Experiments cover Java, Python, and Go repositories with varying comment quality. Ablations examine exemplar count, retrieval depth, and analysis feature sets. We compare against instruction-tuned baselines and supervised fine-tuning under equal token budgets. Code and evaluation tools are released to support replicability.

text: This section reviews literature on explainable recommendation and user-facing justification. Early research emphasized rule-based explanations that map item attributes to human-readable rationales. Herlocker et al. (2000) surveyed explanation interfaces and reported improved user trust with transparent criteria. Later studies incorporated probabilistic models to trade off accuracy and interpretability in ranking tasks. Recent neural approaches seek to ground explanations in textual evidence extracted from reviews or descriptions. Lei et al. (2016) proposed rationalizing predictors by learning extractive snippets that support predictions, which inspired applications in recommendation. In collaborative filtering, attention layers are often interpreted as soft rationales despite known caveats. Ma et al. (2019) analyzed attention weights and cautioned against overinterpreting them in sequence models. Work on counterfactual reasoning reframes explanations as minimal changes that alter the recommended item. Chen et al. (2018) model user-item aspects with attentive pooling. Wang and Li (2019) leverage graph embeddings for explainable ranking. Zhang et al. (2020) align reviews with factors using reinforcement learning. Xu et al. (2021) study counterfactual explanations. Although these methods target different explanation forms, they share a need for faithful alignment between model behavior and presented justifications. Faithfulness metrics compare explanation components to model sensitivities or adversarial perturbations under constrained interventions. Wu et al. (2022) measured faithfulness via deletion tests and gradient-based overlap with selected tokens. Beyond faithfulness, usefulness captures how explanations support user goals during decision making. Kunkel et al. (2019) operationalized usefulness with task success and perceived control in interactive studies. For recommender systems, textual rationales in reviews offer a natural medium for explanation generation. Ni et al. (2019) compiled large review corpora and showed that pretrained encoders improve rationale relevance in multi-task setups. Knowledge-aware models integrate item attributes or knowledge graphs to improve transparency and handle cold-start scenarios. Chen et al. (2020) combined path reasoning over knowledge graphs with sequence decoders to produce human-readable paths. Our work targets faithfulness and usefulness jointly by constraining the explanation generator with causal tests derived from model gradients. We evaluate on two e-commerce datasets with human and automatic metrics aligned to the above criteria. We release code and annotation guidelines to facilitate reproducibility and future user-centered evaluations.

text: This section reviews domain adaptation methods for biomedical named entity recognition. Biomedical text differs from general news in vocabulary, ambiguity, and annotation conventions. Early systems relied on dictionaries and rules curated by domain experts. With the advent of neural sequence labeling, distributed representations improved recall on rare entities. Transfer learning emerged to mitigate limited annotation in specialized subdomains. Pretraining strategies and in-domain language models are central to this progress. Contextual encoders also enable task-specific fine-tuning across heterogeneous corpora. Smith et al. (2018) pretrain models on clinical notes. Habibi et al. (2017) use BiLSTM-CRF with embeddings for gene recognition. Beltagy et al. (2019) introduce SciBERT for scientific text. Lee et al. (2020) release BioBERT trained on PubMed. These resources offer a foundation for adapting taggers to new ontologies with limited supervision. Multi-corpus fine-tuning requires strategies to prevent catastrophic forgetting of previously seen label sets. Ruder et al. (2017) surveyed continual learning and highlighted regularization approaches for sequential tasks. In biomedical NER, sample reweighting balances domain coverage while preserving precision on common terms. Gururangan et al. (2020) demonstrated domain-adaptive pretraining that benefits low-resource specialties. Data augmentation with dictionary projection and span substitution increases mention variety without manual labeling. Weak supervision aggregates noisy sources to expand coverage of entity mentions in emerging topics. Snorkel-style labeling functions provide scalable heuristics for bootstrapping training data in new subfields. Calibration remains important because clinical deployment demands stable precision across document types. Post-hoc normalization maps predicted spans to controlled vocabularies to ensure interoperability. We examine adaptation from literature to clinical text using constrained vocabulary transfer. Our experiments vary the pretraining domain, adaptation schedule, and regularization strength. We report entity-level and span-level metrics across three benchmarks that reflect different subdomains. The findings inform practical recipes for bringing NER models into specialized clinical workflows.

text: Urban traffic forecasting is a canonical benchmark for spatiotemporal learning over graphs. Accurate predictions support routing, congestion mitigation, and safety planning. Sensors produce multivariate time series whose dependencies vary over space and time. Graph neural networks provide a natural inductive bias for such relational structure. Early baselines relied on ARIMA and Kalman filters with fixed spatial neighborhoods. Deep sequence models improved long-horizon stability by combining temporal gating with spatial mixing. Diffusion processes and spectral filters have both been used to capture network dynamics. Li et al. (2018) propose DCRNN for forecasting on sensor networks. Yu et al. (2018) design STGCN using graph convolutions with temporal blocks. Wu et al. (2019) present Graph WaveNet with adjacency. Bai et al. (2020) introduce ASTGCN. Despite strong results, most models assume a static graph that does not reflect incident-driven changes. Adaptive adjacency learning attempts to overcome this limitation with data-driven connectivity. Dynamic graphs also raise identifiability concerns when edge weights shift with exogenous factors. External covariates such as weather and events have been integrated through feature fusion. Meta-learning frameworks adjust parameters to new cities without retraining from scratch. Transfer across regions is challenging due to sensor placement and policy differences. Robust training reduces sensitivity to missing data and sensor faults in deployment. OOD generalization remains underexplored when traffic patterns change seasonally or during crises. Evaluation protocols vary widely in horizon, metrics, and normalization, complicating fair comparison. We focus on uncertainty-aware forecasting that separates data noise from model uncertainty. Our method augments graph diffusion with heteroscedastic outputs calibrated via proper scoring rules. The approach estimates predictive intervals that adapt to congestion regimes and sparse sensors. Experiments on three metropolitan datasets demonstrate gains in coverage and mean error. We release code and processed graphs to facilitate standardized evaluation.

text: Toxicity detection systems raise fairness concerns due to disparate error rates across demographic groups. Bias may arise from data collection, annotation guidelines, or modeling choices. The literature examines both representational and allocative harms in text classifiers. Early studies measured unintended associations between identity terms and negative labels. Mitigation techniques include data balancing, adversarial learning, and calibrated thresholds. Lexical overlap with identity mentions is a persistent confound in toxicity datasets. Context sensitivity remains difficult when reclaimed slurs or quoted content appear. Bolukbasi et al. (2016) document gender biases in word embeddings. Caliskan et al. (2017) quantify stereotypes with WEAT tests. Park et al. (2018) study identity terms in toxicity classification. Davidson et al. (2019) analyze hate speech annotation disagreements. These findings motivate dataset construction practices that minimize spurious correlations and label ambiguity. Counterfactual data augmentation modifies identity terms to assess causal effects on predictions. Garg et al. (2019) proposed template-based swaps to evaluate demographic parity under controlled contexts. Learning objectives can also incorporate fairness regularizers that penalize group-dependent errors. Adversarial representations aim to remove protected attribute information from intermediate layers. Post-hoc calibration improves precision at fixed recall for affected subpopulations. Evaluation should consider both group metrics and individual counterfactual consistency. Further, fairness must be balanced with robustness to adversarial manipulation of toxic content. For operational use, practitioners need interpretable diagnostics that explain disparate impacts. We synthesize prior strategies into a training pipeline with targeted reweighting and counterfactual checks. The pipeline includes auditing dashboards that surface group-level metrics during development. Experiments span three public datasets with standardized demographic lexicons and context-sensitive labels. Results indicate improved worst-group F1 without regressing overall accuracy. We release tools and checklists to support equitable deployment of toxicity detectors.

text: Self-supervised learning has transformed speech processing by leveraging unlabeled audio. These methods learn representations that transfer to recognition, segmentation, and speaker tasks. Predictive coding and masked modeling are two influential paradigms. The design of pretext tasks shapes the granularity and invariances of the learned features. Contrastive losses encourage discrimination of positive samples from temporally near negatives. Masked prediction objectives promote context aggregation without explicit negatives. Unit discovery and quantization further enable discrete targets for prediction heads. Schneider et al. (2019) propose CPC for speech to capture long-range dependencies. Baevski et al. (2020) introduce wav2vec 2.0 with contrastive objectives. Hsu et al. (2021) present HuBERT using masked prediction. Chung et al. (2021) release w2v-BERT hybrids. Pretraining quality correlates with the amount and diversity of unlabeled audio. Fine-tuning strategies influence how much structure from pretraining carries over to downstream tasks. Layer-wise learning rates and feature normalization can stabilize adaptation on small corpora. For robust ASR, multilingual pretraining improves coverage of phonotactics and prosody. Cross-lingual adaptation still requires careful handling of script and tokenization mismatches. Robustness to channel noise and reverberation is often enhanced by augmentations during pretraining. Semi-supervised schedules mix labeled and unlabeled data to optimize sample efficiency. Evaluation must account for error types that vary across languages and speakers. We study feature reuse across layers to interpret where phonetic and lexical cues emerge. Our approach probes frozen encoders with lightweight classifiers on curated phoneme benchmarks. We also analyze stability under additive noise and time warping. Experiments cover read and conversational speech from three public corpora. Results show improved phone discrimination and competitive word error under low-resource regimes. We release checkpoints and scripts for reproducible pretraining and probing.

text: We study domain-adaptive retrieval for specialized question answering in scientific corpora. Prior work highlights the distributional shift between web-scale pretraining and expert subfields that degrades effectiveness. We propose a retrieval framework that integrates late-interaction signals with domain-conditioned contrastive objectives. This section reviews advances in neural reranking, scalable matching, and supervision strategies for scarce labels. Neural cross-encoders report strong reranking accuracy by modeling query–document interactions exhaustively (Nogueira and Cho, 2019). ColBERT adopts late interaction to preserve token-level evidence while enabling sublinear retrieval over large indexes (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). Synthetic query generation and pseudo labels expand training coverage in domain-scarce settings (Ma et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2022). Pretraining on citation graphs has been recently considered elsewhere. Few studies interrogate how these ingredients affect calibration and selective prediction in expert settings. Our formulation unifies candidate generation and adaptation through a lightweight curriculum and efficient indexing. We compare with strong baselines across biomedical, legal, and materials datasets using consistent evaluation suites.

Synthetic query generation and pseudo labels expand training coverage in domain-scarce settings (Ma et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2022). Pretraining on citation graphs has been recently considered elsewhere.

text: We investigate factual consistency in abstractive summarization under resource constraints. Prior research has shown that neural summarizers often hallucinate entities and figures that do not appear in the source. Pointer-generator networks mixed copying with generation to improve coverage and fluency in news summarization (See et al., 2017). BERTSum leveraged pretrained encoders to strengthen sentence selection and abstraction for longer documents (Liu and Lapata, 2019). Constraint-based decoding added entity and number controls to mitigate factual drift during generation (Cao et al., 2020; Narayan et al., 2021). Human preference learning has been reported in instruction tuning for summarization. Despite progress, few works examine domain-conditioned consistency across scientific and financial data. We address this gap with a constrained decoding objective linked to evidence extraction. Our method introduces compact alignment heads trained with weakly labeled rationales. The approach is modular and can be attached to pretrained summarizers without architecture changes. We report results on diverse corpora with careful human evaluation of factuality.

text: We consider multimodal emotion recognition from synchronized audio, video, and text streams. A central difficulty is aligning heterogeneous temporal signals without losing complementary cues. Our study targets robust learning under missing modalities and label noise. This review organizes research in model fusion, cross-modal alignment, and pretraining for affective computing. Prior taxonomies emphasize the distinction between early and late fusion with varying inductive biases. Representation learning for spontaneous reactions in the wild presents additional robustness issues. Early fusion concatenates acoustic, visual, and lexical embeddings for downstream classifiers with modest capacity (Poria et al., 2017). Transformers with cross-modal attention align speech prosody with facial motion trajectories for robust cues (Tsai et al., 2019). Self-supervised objectives on unlabeled video and audio have been used to pretrain encoders before supervised fine-tuning (Albanie et al., 2018; Hsu et al., 2021). Annotation reliability remains a challenge across corpora. We build on these observations to design a scalable, noise-aware fusion strategy with calibrated outputs.

Annotation reliability remains a challenge across corpora.

text: Predicting molecular properties from structure is a core task in computational chemistry and drug discovery. Graph neural networks provide an appealing inductive bias by modeling atoms and bonds directly. We study how architectural choices and data regimes interact to affect generalization across scaffolds. This review organizes prior work around message passing, expressivity, and representation learning. Sparse attention mechanisms on bonds have also been proposed to capture long-range effects. Message passing neural networks aggregate local neighborhoods to encode molecular graphs with learned edge updates (Gilmer et al., 2017). Graph isomorphism networks increase expressivity by injecting sum aggregation and multi-layer perceptrons at nodes (Xu et al., 2019). Pretraining on unlabeled molecules with context prediction and motif objectives has been investigated for transfer (Hu et al., 2020; Rong et al., 2020). Dataset curation practices differ markedly across benchmarks. Despite these developments, out-of-distribution performance remains sensitive to scaffold splits and label noise. Our approach introduces calibration-aware training with scaffold-level regularization and principled validation protocols.

Dataset curation practices differ markedly across benchmarks.

text: We examine fairness-aware learning objectives for top-k recommendation under implicit feedback. The problem spans consumer harms, provider exposure, and societal externalities in large marketplaces. Our contribution is a modular objective that trades off relevance and exposure disparity. Exposure-aware ranking models allocate visibility to items under provider constraints and position bias (Singh and Joachims, 2018). Counterfactual risk minimization addresses selection bias in logged feedback using inverse propensity scoring estimators (Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015). Graph-based recommenders integrate social and item relations through message passing to capture multifaceted signals (Ying et al., 2018; He et al., 2020). User studies sometimes report mixed perceptions of unfairness in platforms. Existing evaluations often conflate user utility and provider visibility, obscuring causal impacts. We disentangle these effects by optimizing calibrated scores and counterfactual exposure baselines. Our method retains efficiency with batched estimators and sparse constraints suited to production settings. We demonstrate improvements across streaming playlists and local business search datasets with fairness metrics.

Counterfactual risk minimization addresses selection bias in logged feedback using inverse propensity scoring estimators (Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015). Graph-based recommenders integrate social and item relations through message passing to capture multifaceted signals (Ying et al., 2018; He et al., 2020). User studies sometimes report mixed perceptions of unfairness in platforms.

text: Recommender systems research increasingly examines fairness, accountability, and transparency as integral objectives beyond accuracy. Prior work defines user and item level harms that arise from exposure imbalances, underrepresented content, and biased feedback loops. In commercial platforms, designers must coordinate multiple stakeholders whose utilities can diverge across ranking positions and time. Our study targets provider fairness in music recommendation and evaluates constrained reranking under realistic catalog shifts. We focus on interventions that are compatible with large-scale candidate generation while preserving relevance signals learned from implicit data. This section reviews research on exposure fairness, multi-stakeholder optimization, and evaluation protocols relevant to our approach. We first summarize foundational studies that characterize disparity mechanisms and propose mitigation algorithms. Kumar et al. (2019) analyzed exposure disparity in top-N recommendation using implicit feedback logs and quantified rank-based inequality across users. Li and Steck (2021) introduced calibrated reranking that preserves score distributions while satisfying group fairness targets in offline evaluations settings. Burke et al. (2018) outlined multi-stakeholder objectives that balance provider exposure, consumer satisfaction, and platform revenue in recommender ecosystems designs. Ekstrand et al. (2022) examined social consequences of algorithmic curation, reporting changes in diversity exposure and inclusion across controlled studies. Sonboli et al. (2020) developed popularity debiasing through item reweighting and stratified sampling, reducing exposure skew without retraining collaborative models. These studies collectively motivate algorithmic levers that can adjust allocation without discarding valuable predictive structure. Building on this motivation, we consider budgeted constraints that trade small utility losses for measurable improvements in provider reach. Recent advances in stochastic ranking offer efficient projections that satisfy linear constraints under streaming traffic. We adopt these projections to operate on top of score-based slates produced by a baseline matrix factorization model. To assess real-world feasibility, we simulate catalog churn and popularity drifts using public listening logs with temporal splits. Our evaluation emphasizes short-horizon and long-horizon exposure parity along with calibration of relevance at multiple cutoffs. The analysis compares reranking with feature-augmented training to highlight the cost of post hoc control versus representation learning. We report sensitivity to constraint strength and catalog skew to guide practitioners deploying fairness safeguards in production. Finally, we release code and configuration templates enabling replication and extension on alternative domains. The next section formalizes the allocation objectives and introduces our optimization scheme with convergence guarantees.

Li and Steck (2021) introduced calibrated reranking that preserves score distributions while satisfying group fairness targets in offline evaluations settings. Burke et al. (2018) outlined multi-stakeholder objectives that balance provider exposure, consumer satisfaction, and platform revenue in recommender ecosystems designs. Ekstrand et al. (2022) examined social consequences of algorithmic curation, reporting changes in diversity exposure and inclusion across controlled studies. Sonboli et al. (2020) developed popularity debiasing through item reweighting and stratified sampling, reducing exposure skew without retraining collaborative models.

text: Transformer models dominate NLP benchmarks yet their decision processes remain difficult to interpret in operational settings. Interpretability is vital for error analysis, trust calibration, and responsible deployment in domains with high stakes. Two major paradigms have emerged, focusing on mechanistic understanding of internal circuits and post hoc attribution of outputs. In sequence modeling, attention distributions and neuron activations provide accessible signals for exploratory analysis. Nonetheless, aligning these signals with faithful causal explanations requires clear assumptions and robust evaluation designs. Our work studies token influence pathways in encoder layers using interventions that alter computational routes while preserving outputs. We formalize influence as changes in path capacities under limited rewiring budgets to capture redundancy in attention graphs. The review below positions our approach relative to prior analyses of attention structure and explanation adequacy. Clark et al. (2019) investigated attention heads with probing tasks and reported interpretable patterns associated with syntactic dependencies and coreference. Voita et al. (2019) identified redundant heads using learned importance metrics and showed minimal performance drop after structured head pruning. Michel et al. (2019) analyzed sensitivity to head removal at inference time and quantified task robustness under aggressive pruning regimes. Vig and Belinkov (2019) visualized attention using attribution maps that highlight token interactions and correlate with model salience scores estimates. Jain and Wallace (2019) questioned attention as explanation by demonstrating weak alignment with gradient-based importance and counterfactual perturbations in evaluations. These investigations chart a landscape of head utility, salience visualization, and the limits of attention as an explanatory device. Building on this landscape, we design targeted ablations that respect residual mixing to isolate path-level contributions. We complement ablations with counterfactual routing that preserves activations but redirects messages through alternate heads. This yields measurable causal effects that distinguish representational redundancy from indispensability across layers and tasks. Our metrics emphasize faithfulness by tying explanations to observed performance changes under controlled manipulations. We evaluate on translation, parsing, and sentiment analysis to probe differences between syntactic and semantic pathways. Results demonstrate that a small subset of heads accounts for most effective paths under constrained routing. We release analysis code and interactive visualizations to facilitate reproducibility and practical adoption. The subsequent section details our formalism and experimental setup.

text: Multimodal emotion recognition seeks robust models that integrate heterogeneous signals while remaining reliable across domains and speakers. Despite progress, systems struggle with temporal misalignment, noise, and covariate shift that degrade generalization. Effective fusion requires architectures that exchange information without collapsing modality-specific nuances. Our work investigates modular adapters that connect self-supervised encoders and enable adaptive cross-modal influence. We prioritize transparency by tracing contributions from each modality through interpretable routing variables. This section situates our design within prior fusion, conversational modeling, and representation learning studies. We emphasize methods that address interaction structure and long-range dependencies in dialogue settings. The literature includes a variety of tensor, graph, and transformer approaches with different inductive biases. We summarize representative techniques that inform our adapter choices. Poria et al. (2017) integrated text, audio, and video features with attention mechanisms to improve utterance-level emotion classification in dialogues. Zadeh et al. (2018) proposed tensor fusion that models unimodal, bimodal, and trimodal interactions for sentiment and emotion tasks jointly. Majumder et al. (2019) leveraged contextual representations in conversational graphs to propagate affective cues across speaker turns and long-range dependencies. Tsai et al. (2019) introduced cross-modal transformers that learn directional alignments between modalities using guided co-attention and factorized bilinear pooling. Hazarika et al. (2020) constructed memory networks capturing persona dynamics and commonsense signals to refine predictions on challenging conversational benchmarks. Together, these lines of work indicate the importance of structured interactions and context propagation for affective reasoning. Building from these insights, we propose lightweight adapters that learn asymmetric influence while preserving unimodal competence. The adapters mediate message passing and gate contributions at multiple resolutions within a shared temporal backbone. We further incorporate robust normalization to tolerate noisy segments and missing modalities during inference. Our experiments evaluate adapters on benchmarks with speaker changes, intensity variation, and spontaneous speech. Results show improved accuracy and calibration relative to strong baselines under covariate shift. We release pretrained checkpoints and ablation scripts to support reproducibility and analysis. The following section formalizes the adapter architecture and training objectives.

(2020) constructed memory networks capturing persona dynamics and commonsense signals to refine predictions on challenging conversational benchmarks.

text: Low-resource machine translation benefits substantially from data augmentation that expands coverage and regularizes model behavior. Augmenting parallel corpora with synthetic examples can reduce overfitting and expose models to broader linguistic patterns. Among available techniques, back-translation and lexical perturbations are widely adopted due to simplicity and scalability. Despite widespread use, selecting augmentation schemes that align with domain and vocabulary constraints remains challenging. We study a unified framework that composes multiple augmentations under explicit risk control. Our review highlights influential methods that inform the design of our pipeline. Sennrich et al. (2016) popularized back-translation by generating synthetic sources, enabling effective use of abundant monolingual target data during training. Edunov et al. (2018) scaled back-translation with diverse sampling and showed stronger performance with large synthetic corpora on WMT tasks. Fadaee et al. (2017) introduced rare word substitution to encourage coverage of infrequent phenomena by modifying sentences with targeted replacements. Poncelas et al. (2018) examined data selection strategies that filter synthetic examples based on domain similarity and perceived translation quality. Gao et al. (2019) explored lexical augmentation using paraphrases and noise schemes to increase variability and resilience in neural translation. These contributions underscore the central role of synthetic data fidelity, diversity, and filtering in effective augmentation. We synthesize these insights into a risk-aware scheduler that balances exploration and reliability across training epochs. The scheduler weighs candidate transformations using held-out validation signals and simple accept-reject tests. It also accounts for domain mismatch by prioritizing examples close to in-domain features measured with language models. We implement the approach in an encoder-decoder toolkit with minimal changes to existing training scripts. Our experiments cover four language pairs with limited bitext and varying morphological complexity. We report BLEU, chrF, and calibration metrics to assess accuracy and uncertainty under shift. Results demonstrate consistent gains over strong baselines using single augmentation strategies. Analysis reveals that combining back-translation with lexical noise widens coverage without excessive degradation of sequence-level likelihoods. Ablations further show the value of adaptive filtering when synthetic examples are noisy or off-domain. The next section details the algorithm and experimental settings.

(2018) scaled back-translation with diverse sampling and showed stronger performance with large synthetic corpora on WMT tasks. Fadaee et al. (2017) introduced rare word substitution to encourage coverage of infrequent phenomena by modifying sentences with targeted replacements. Poncelas et al. (2018) examined data selection strategies that filter synthetic examples based on domain similarity and perceived translation quality. Gao et al. (2019) explored lexical augmentation using paraphrases and noise schemes to increase variability and resilience in neural translation.

(2017) introduced rare word substitution to encourage coverage of infrequent phenomena by modifying sentences with targeted replacements. Poncelas et al. (2019) explored lexical augmentation using paraphrases and noise schemes to increase variability and resilience in neural translation.

text: Molecular property prediction is central to discovery workflows that screen vast chemical spaces with computational models. Graph neural networks have become the dominant paradigm for representing molecules as atoms and bonds with learnable interactions. Despite strong results, models can overfit under limited labeled data and struggle to capture long-range effects. We investigate scalable pretraining and pooling strategies that improve sample efficiency and interpretability. Our approach emphasizes physicochemical inductive biases and principled evaluation on curated benchmarks. We provide an overview of representative architectures and datasets that inform our design choices. The following studies establish the foundations for modern graph learning in chemistry. Gilmer et al. (2017) introduced message passing neural networks that propagate learned messages along bonds to predict molecular properties accurately. Kearnes et al. (2016) developed graph convolutional models for molecules using atom and bond features within differentiable fingerprint architectures designs. Wu et al. (2018) compiled MoleculeNet benchmarks and standardized datasets to evaluate algorithms across diverse targets and data regimes consistently. Yang et al. (2019) presented directed message passing variants that exploit edge directionality to enhance feature flow and reactivity modeling. Hu et al. (2020) proposed pre-training strategies on large unlabeled molecular graphs to improve downstream property prediction performance across tasks. Collectively, these works shaped objectives, datasets, and operators that we adapt in a unified architecture. Building on this foundation, we introduce distance-aware positional encodings that augment bond messages with geometric context. We also propose constrained pooling that preserves substructure evidence while producing fixed-size graph summaries. The model integrates pretraining with supervised fine-tuning using auxiliary tasks aligned with quantum-chemical trends. We evaluate across MoleculeNet benchmarks and additional medicinal chemistry datasets with scaffold splits. Metrics include ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, and regression errors under standardized data partitions. Our results demonstrate improved performance in low-label regimes and better calibration of uncertainty estimates. Case studies highlight mechanistic interpretations tied to functional groups and ring systems. We release code, trained weights, and hyperparameter sweeps for reproducibility. Subsequent sections detail architecture, training procedures, and evaluation protocols.

text: This paper examines personalization strategies for conversational recommendation in dynamic domains. Prior work has established that dialogue policies benefit from user modeling and long-term feedback in multi-turn settings. We review approaches that integrate reinforcement learning with latent preference inference to motivate our design. Recent neural architectures emphasize grounding recommendations in item knowledge graphs and session context. Smith et al. (2019) present a hierarchical policy that interleaves probing questions with targeted suggestions in movie domains. Lee and Park (2020) introduce a memory-augmented encoder that tracks item attributes and user confirmations. Zhao et al. (2021) analyze critique-based updates using differentiable set encoders for apparel recommendation. Nguyen and Chen (2018) explore topic transitions in chit-chat dialogue with variational subgoals. Ramanathan et al. (2022) study slot carryover under sparse supervision in voice-based shopping for new users online. Building on these observations, we propose a modular controller that separates preference elicitation from constraint handling. We release code and data to support reproducibility and further research.

text: We investigate cross-lingual transfer for abstractive summarization under limited supervision. Previous research demonstrates that multilingual encoders can share useful semantic representations across languages. Yet the mechanisms by which summarization models learn alignment remain poorly understood. To situate our approach, we first survey label-scarce training objectives for multilingual summarization. Liu et al. (2020) introduce mBART, a multilingual denoising autoencoder that yields strong gains on cross-lingual generation. Zhang et al. (2021) present XSumML, fine-tuning mBART with mix-language summaries and document pairs. Shi and Knight (2022) study sentence compression signals for guiding abstractive content selection across scripts. Hasan et al. (2019) examine low-resource headline generation with byte-level vocabularies to reduce tokenization mismatch. Garcia et al. (2023) curate distant supervision from bilingual news to bootstrap summaries without human annotations. Our framework leverages dual-view agreement to encourage consistent summaries across pivoted translations. We evaluate on three news corpora spanning five language families and provide an analysis of error types.

text: Temporal knowledge graph reasoning aims to infer future or missing facts conditioned on historical events. The challenge arises from irregular timestamps, sparse supervision, and non-stationary relation dynamics. Existing methods trade off expressiveness against scalability when incorporating temporal signals. We focus on autoregressive models that leverage learned history to predict the next plausible edges. Jiang et al. (2016) propose discrete-time embeddings with relation-specific decay for event forecasting. Xu and Li (2019) model intervals using recurrent translation operators trained on time-stamped triples. Goel et al. (2020) design diachronic entity representations that capture evolving aliases and roles. Sadeghian et al. (2021) explore rule-guided reasoning with temporal constraints mined from frequent patterns. Lacroix and Hamilton (2022) examine time-conditioned scoring functions for autoregressive link prediction over streaming graphs with scalable memory usage in practice deployments. Our method introduces a contrastive forecasting loss that balances long-range dependencies with short-term recency. We validate performance on three benchmarks with ablation studies on time discretization and memory size.

text: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw user data. While privacy is often emphasized, considerable attention is now directed to fairness under non-iid client distributions. Disparities in error rates can emerge from skewed participation, heterogeneous hardware, and domain shift. We study objectives that trade off global accuracy with group- and client-level equity. Mohri et al. (2019) formulate agnostic federated learning to optimize for worst-case mixtures over client distributions. Li et al. (2020) propose FedProx to stabilize updates with proximal regularization under heterogeneity. Andrew et al. (2021) present fairness constraints using adaptive reweighting of client losses. Wu and He (2022) investigate participation-aware schedulers to balance representation across demographics. Kairouz et al. (2021) survey systems and algorithms for production-scale federated learning with attention to deployment constraints in mobile and edge settings. We define a min-max objective that adapts per-client weights through a calibrated gradient tracking scheme. Experiments on vision and speech tasks demonstrate improved worst-group accuracy without sacrificing overall performance.

Wu and He (2022) investigate participation-aware schedulers to balance representation across demographics. Kairouz et al. (2021) survey systems and algorithms for production-scale federated learning with attention to deployment constraints in mobile and edge settings.

text: Automatic program repair seeks to suggest minimal edits that resolve failing tests without introducing regressions. Statistical and neural techniques have broadened the search space beyond hand-written rules, yet robustness remains elusive. Large pretrained models help capture code regularities but struggle with domain-specific constraints and context length. We propose a retrieval-guided decoder that conditions on structurally similar patches drawn from project histories. Gupta et al. (2017) apply neural machine translation to map buggy code to fixes on Java benchmarks. Chen et al. (2019) develop sequence-to-sequence editors with abstracted identifiers for better generalization. Tufano et al. (2019) mine small commit diffs to train lightweight patch generators. Berabi et al. (2021) incorporate repair templates into transformer decoders for constrained decoding. Drain et al. (2021) leverage context from test failures and static analysis alerts to guide patch ranking in continuous integration pipelines. Our approach complements these directions by retrieving task-adaptive exemplars and enforcing syntax-aware constraints during decoding. We conduct studies on Defects4J and QuixBugs with human evaluation of patch readability and maintainability.

text: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw data across institutions. Healthcare applications motivate rigorous privacy and reliability guarantees due to regulatory and ethical constraints. Prior work has investigated communication efficiency, robustness to heterogeneity, and personalization in clinical networks. We focus on cross-hospital prediction tasks where patient distributions and device availability vary significantly. Existing surveys synthesize algorithmic advances and deployment challenges for medical federations. Our study extends this perspective by measuring calibration and auditing in a realistic hospital federation. We also examine operational factors such as participation incentives and compliance logging in multi-party settings. Smith et al. (2019) introduce secure aggregation protocols for cross-device training in large fleets. Zhao and Patel (2020) study gradient compression techniques under constrained uplink bandwidth. Li et al. (2021) propose personalization layers that adapt global models to clinic-specific data. Nguyen et al. (2018) analyze graph-based cohort formation to stabilize updates across irregular participation. Kim and Rogers (2022) explore audit trails for clinical accountability in decentralized learning. These themes motivate our architecture that pairs secure aggregation with on-device uncertainty estimation. We evaluate on de-identified intensive care records spanning multiple health systems and admission types. Our metrics include predictive accuracy, calibration error, communication volume, and fairness across demographic strata. We report ablations that isolate the impact of aggregation choice and participation variability on convergence. We implement a minimal auditing layer that records cryptographic commitments and model provenance. The evaluation protocol aligns with institutional review processes and data governance requirements. We release reproducible scripts and simulated trace data to support further assessment by practitioners. The results illustrate trade-offs between privacy, utility, and oversight that guide future deployments.

Nguyen et al. (2018) analyze graph-based cohort formation to stabilize updates across irregular participation. Kim and Rogers (2022) explore audit trails for clinical accountability in decentralized learning.

text: Multimodal sentiment analysis aims to infer affective states from synchronized audio, visual, and textual streams. The problem is challenging due to cross-modal noise, temporal misalignment, and individual expressivity differences. Recent models use attention and transformers to align and fuse signals with varying sampling rates. Pretraining on large video corpora has improved representations for downstream emotion recognition tasks. We investigate a lightweight fusion strategy that adapts to missing modalities at inference time. Our approach builds on advances in temporal alignment and robust aggregation for social media videos. We emphasize evaluation protocols that reflect real-world variability in recording environments and subject populations. We also consider label uncertainty stemming from annotator disagreement on fine-grained emotions. Barrett et al. (2019) introduce a temporal attention model for video-based sentiment cues. Wu and Liang (2020) collect a large-scale dataset of aligned speech and captions from review videos. Kossaifi et al. (2021) present a facial action coding benchmark that standardizes expression labels. Poria et al. (2018) design a hierarchical fusion network to aggregate audio, text, and visual streams. Chung et al. (2022) release multimodal pretraining objectives. Building on these insights, we propose a masking strategy that conditions fusion on modality reliability scores. The method scales with sequence length and supports training under partial supervision. We conduct experiments on benchmark datasets with controlled modality dropouts and corrupted channels. Our results show competitive accuracy with reduced compute relative to strong baselines. We analyze sensitivity to alignment errors and the effect of curriculum masking schedules. An ablation study quantifies the contribution of reliability estimation versus architectural capacity. Code and pretrained weights are provided to facilitate replication and further analysis.

Chung et al. (2022) release multimodal pretraining objectives.

text: Program synthesis from natural language seeks to generate executable code that fulfills user intent. The task combines representation learning, semantic constraints, and execution feedback to refine outputs. Contemporary systems rely on pretrained language models and domain-specific constraints to ensure correctness. We study constrained decoding with verifiers that balance coverage and search efficiency. Our focus is data-efficient training and faithful evaluation for table and string manipulation tasks. We also investigate the role of execution traces for guiding token-level decisions during inference. Austin et al. (2021) curate execution-based evaluation for code generation tasks. Chen et al. (2021) pretrain large language models on permissive code corpora. Le et al. (2016) propose robust search-based synthesis guided by counterexamples. Polozov and Gulwani (2015) introduce FlashFill to learn string transformations from examples. Alon et al. (2019) mine path-based representations for structural code context. Yin and Neubig (2017) map utterances to ASTs with decoders. Motivated by these directions, we integrate verification-guided decoding with constrained beam search. We incorporate lightweight type checks and input-output consistency tests during hypothesis expansion. Our method reduces semantic errors while preserving lexical diversity in generated programs. We evaluate on standardized benchmarks with both functional and syntactic metrics. Results indicate gains in exact match, test pass rate, and constraint satisfaction. We quantify verifier overhead and report trade-offs between coverage and runtime. Error analysis reveals common failure modes in entity resolution and variable scoping. We release datasets, prompts, and scripts to support reproducible experimentation.

(2019) mine path-based representations for structural code context. Yin and Neubig (2017) map utterances to ASTs with decoders.

text: Recommender systems mediate access to information and opportunity across many domains. Concerns about bias and unfair treatment have motivated rigorous evaluation of ranking outcomes. Research has explored user, item provider, and societal perspectives on equitable recommendations. This work addresses distributional exposure disparities with constrained ranking optimization. We study applications in job platforms and media streaming services with heterogeneous suppliers. The setting involves implicit feedback, position bias, and strategic responses by content producers. We ground our methodology in prior work on fairness-aware training and long-term dynamics. Ekstrand et al. (2018) document user-facing harms emerging from exposure bias in recommenders. Beutel et al. (2019) propose pairwise regularizers to equalize ranking quality across groups. Burke (2017) formulates multi-sided fairness objectives in composite systems. Sonboli et al. (2021) study provider fairness metrics in curated marketplaces. Wu et al. (2022) analyze long-term feedback loops with agent-based simulations. Yao and Huang (2017) highlight disparate exposure across sensitive attributes. Inspired by these findings, we define constraints that bound exposure disparities under click models. We optimize a differentiable surrogate that links utility and exposure within top-k positions. The training procedure interleaves policy learning with dual updates for constraint satisfaction. Experiments use semi-synthetic logs with realistic position bias and provider supply curves. We compare against re-ranking and post-processing baselines under matched utility targets. Results show consistent reduction in disparity with modest utility loss across scenarios. We discuss implications for platform governance and transparent reporting in deployed systems.

text: Data augmentation has become a standard tool to improve generalization in text processing tasks. Unlike images, language augmentation must preserve semantics and label consistency under transformations. Techniques range from token perturbations to paraphrasing and back-translation using sequence models. The choice of augmentation interacts with model scale, regularization, and domain shift. We investigate a policy that selects augmentations based on estimated invariances for each task. Our approach learns to predict utility of a transformation from small pilot experiments. We focus on low-resource classification and translation with limited parallel or labeled data. We also analyze compositional effects when multiple augmentations are applied jointly. Xie et al. (2019) introduce back-translation to improve semi-supervised sequence modeling. Kobayashi (2018) perturbs word embeddings to generate substitute-based samples. Wei and Zou (2019) propose simple token-level operations for low-resource text classification. Fadaee et al. (2017) craft rare word substitutions to enrich translation corpora. Sennrich et al. (2016) augment parallel data with monolingual targets for neural machine translation. Nguyen et al. (2020) evaluate transfer under noisy augmentation. Building on these insights, we frame augmentation choice as a contextual bandit problem. The policy observes dataset features and selects a transformation with exploration bonuses. We measure gains in accuracy and robustness under shifts in topic and style. An ablation isolates the effect of adaptive selection from the diversity of operations. Results show consistent improvements over fixed recipes across multiple language families. We release code and tuning logs to support practical deployment in low-resource settings.

(2016) augment parallel data with monolingual targets for neural machine translation. Nguyen et al. (2020) evaluate transfer under noisy augmentation.

text: This section reviews research on multimodal representation learning for medical imaging. Our focus is on models that integrate images and free text from electronic health records. Prior surveys emphasize the importance of supervision granularity and dataset scale. Early CNN based pipelines paired embeddings using simple averaging strategies. Subsequent work introduced attention mechanisms to condition visual features on textual cues. Weakly supervised learning became common due to limited annotation budgets. Recent studies also explore self-supervised objectives derived from in-report cooccurrence patterns. We organize the discussion by alignment, fusion, and generalization. Alignment approaches target shared spaces where visual and textual semantics meet. Zhou et al. (2019) trained a cross-modal encoder on paired radiology reports and chest X-rays using contrastive objectives. Pereira et al. (2020) introduced a transformer that aligns report sections with image regions without supervision. Nguyen and Patel (2021) studied domain shifts in hospital scanners and proposed calibration layers for robust feature fusion. Datasets varied widely. Fusion methods combine modalities at intermediate layers to preserve uncertainty. Khanna et al. (2022) use gated cross-attention to recalibrate image patches with clinical phrases. Joint training with entity linking further improves localization of pathologies in long reports. Generalization beyond a single site remains difficult due to scanner heterogeneity. Distributionally robust optimization has been tested on small cohorts with mixed outcomes. We adopt a two-stage strategy that pretrains on public corpora and adapts to site specifics. The proposed benchmark emphasizes cross-site validation and standardized preprocessing. Our method complements retrieval style inference with calibrated uncertainty estimates. The contributions include a taxonomy of objectives, an evaluation suite, and an open model.

(2020) introduced a transformer that aligns report sections with image regions without supervision. Nguyen and Patel (2021) studied domain shifts in hospital scanners and proposed calibration layers for robust feature fusion. Datasets varied widely.

(2020) introduced a transformer that aligns report sections with image regions without supervision. Nguyen and Patel (2021) studied domain shifts in hospital scanners and proposed calibration layers for robust feature fusion. Datasets varied widely.

text: This introduction surveys methods for bias measurement and mitigation in natural language processing. We focus on demographic disparities that arise in prediction and generation tasks. Definitions of fairness vary across legal, philosophical, and statistical traditions. In NLP, group fairness metrics are typically computed from labeled corpora or simulated labels. Recent work emphasizes the role of data collection protocols and annotation schemas. Model auditing often reveals compounding effects between pretraining and finetuning stages. Benchmarks also diverge in whether they target representational or allocative harms. We describe evidence from vision and language systems that motivates our approach. Evidence accumulates that spurious correlations persist even after adversarial training. Large models can obscure disparate error profiles under aggregate accuracy. Buolamwini and Gebru (2018) documented large error disparities in commercial face classifiers across skin tones and gender. Zhao et al. (2017) analyzed occupational word embeddings and reported systematic associations that reproduce social stereotypes. Blodgett et al. (2020) surveyed fairness in language technologies across multiple linguistic varieties and highlighted measurement gaps. Data sources differed quite widely. These findings suggest that measurement must consider both task context and demographic coverage. Causality inspired metrics estimate counterfactual sensitivity to protected attributes. Dataset interventions include reweighting, counterfactual augmentation, and targeted relabeling. Mitigation techniques trade off parity with task utility under distribution shift. We adopt a modular framework that separates data, model, and metric interventions. Our experiments probe subgroup performance across granular attribute intersections. We also release protocols for reproducible auditing and transparent reporting. The resulting toolkit aims to support practitioners in evaluation driven model design.

text: Software engineering research increasingly leverages large language models for code understanding and generation. The literature spans representation learning, synthesis, and static analysis. Pretraining objectives exploit code structure and natural language descriptions. Data quality and licensing impose practical limits on model deployment. Code-specific tokenizers improve identifiability of naming conventions and syntax. Fine-tuning often relies on execution based rewards or surrogate metrics. Benchmarks emphasize functional correctness and style constraints. We review prior work to situate our evaluation. Representation learning aligns code and comments for retrieval and summarization. Chen et al. (2021) introduced CodeBERT to learn bimodal representations from source and documentation. Austin et al. (2021) presented Program Synthesis with large language models under few-shot prompting. Ahmad et al. (2021) proposed CoderEval to benchmark code generation on multilingual datasets with unit tests. Reported improvements varied by language and task granularity. Evaluation setups differed. Recent approaches integrate symbolic reasoning to enforce constraints during decoding. Execution-guided decoding filters candidates using intermediate interpreter feedback. Static analysis can supply type hints that narrow the search space. We study data curation strategies that target coverage of library APIs. Our dataset enables paired evaluation under masked span and synthesis settings. The methods compared include prompting, finetuning, and reinforcement posttraining. We report results on pass rates, robustness to obfuscation, and generalization. The findings inform best practices for reliable code generation at scale.

(2021) presented Program Synthesis with large language models under few-shot prompting. Ahmad et al. (2021) proposed CoderEval to benchmark code generation on multilingual datasets with unit tests. Reported improvements varied by language and task granularity. Evaluation setups differed.

(2021) presented Program Synthesis with large language models under few-shot prompting. Ahmad et al. (2021) proposed CoderEval to benchmark code generation on multilingual datasets with unit tests. Reported improvements varied by language and task granularity. Evaluation setups differed.

text: Recommender systems increasingly adopt graph neural networks to capture higher-order interactions. User behavior can be modeled as edges over heterogeneous item graphs. Graph convolutions propagate signals through neighborhoods to learn representations. Industrial deployments face constraints in scalability and latency. Sampling strategies and caching policies determine throughput on dense graphs. Loss functions often combine ranking surrogates with regularization for debiasing. We summarize prior models and training practices relevant to large platforms. Ying et al. (2018) proposed PinSage to scale graph convolutions for recommendation on web-scale item graphs. He et al. (2020) introduced LightGCN removing nonlinearities to improve collaborative filtering. Wu et al. (2019) surveyed graph neural network architectures and training tricks for diverse tasks. Hyperparameters and datasets were not consistent. Reporting standards varied across studies substantially. Subsequent work integrates knowledge graphs to enrich item attributes. Session-based settings rely on temporal graphs with recurrent message passing. Negative sampling affects both convergence and bias in offline evaluation. We analyze sensitivity to sampling rate and propagation depth. Our method decouples encoding from retrieval to meet latency budgets. A hybrid index supports incremental updates from streaming events. We evaluate on public and proprietary datasets with matched protocols. Metrics include NDCG, recall at cutoffs, and calibration error. Ablations quantify the contribution of depth, sampling, and feature dropout. The results inform practical configurations for production scale recommenders.
span_text: Ying et al. (2018) proposed PinSage to scale graph convolutions for recommendation on web-scale item graphs. He et al. (2020) introduced LightGCN removing nonlinearities to improve collaborative filtering. Wu et al. (2019) surveyed graph neural network architectures and training tricks for diverse tasks. Hyperparameters and datasets were not consistent. Reporting standards varied across studies substantially.
