text: This paper examines statistical downscaling methods for regional climate projections. Accurate local estimates are necessary for risk assessment and adaptation planning. Traditional techniques include bias correction, quantile mapping, and weather generators. Machine learning has reinvigorated research on super resolution of gridded climate fields. Reanalysis products and satellite observations provide rich training corpora. Generalization across regions and future climates remains a central challenge. Predictive skill depends on scales of variability and physical constraints. We review prior approaches that inform our modeling choices. Super resolution architectures dominate recent literature on downscaling. Vandal et al. (2018) proposed DeepSD to super resolve climate fields using stacked convolutional networks. Rasp et al. (2020) advocated data driven weather prediction with deep learning on reanalysis datasets. Bauer et al. (2015) discussed the seamless prediction paradigm integrating numerical models and machine learning. Spatial coverage and temporal resolution varied across datasets and regions. Hybrid models embed conservation laws via custom loss functions or postprocessing. Transfer learning can exploit analogs between historical and projected regimes. Data augmentation with physically consistent perturbations improves robustness. We define a benchmark spanning multiple variables and emission scenarios. Our method uses multiscale attention constrained by energy balance priors. Training emphasizes temporal consistency through sequence objectives and calendar features. Evaluation includes extremes, seasonal cycles, and tail dependence. We analyze failure modes under covariate shift and rare events. The study contributes open baselines, diagnostics, and a replicable pipeline.

(2020) advocated data driven weather prediction with deep learning on reanalysis datasets. Bauer et al. text: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw data. This paradigm raises questions about privacy, communication efficiency, and fairness in deployment. Prior studies have explored protocols, threat models, and optimization strategies for a range of applications. Our work focuses on practical safeguards for cross-device training under limited bandwidth. We review foundations that motivate our design choices and evaluation metrics. McMahan et al. (2017) introduced federated averaging to train models across decentralized devices. Kairouz et al. (2021) surveyed open problems and design choices in cross-device and cross-silo settings. Geyer et al. (2017) explored client-level differential privacy for user updates. Bonawitz et al. (2019) described secure aggregation protocols at scale. Hard et al. (2018) presented production deployments on mobile keyboards. Yang et al. (2019) studied communication compression with structured updates. These contributions outline the basic trade-offs between update frequency and privacy amplification by subsampling. Communication remains a bottleneck in many production settings with intermittent connectivity. Quantization and sparsification techniques reduce payload size while preserving model utility. Alistarh et al. (2017) proposed QSGD with unbiased stochastic rounding for distributed learning. Aji and Heafield (2017) introduced gradient dropping for neural machine translation training. Konečný et al. (2016) analyzed structured updates for on-device computation and cloud aggregation. We extend these ideas with adaptive schedules that track device availability and error feedback. Security risks further motivate careful protocol choices and auditing mechanisms. Shokri and Shmatikov (2015) demonstrated membership inference attacks on deep models. Carlini et al. (2021) examined sensitive memorization in language models. Nasr et al. (2019) analyzed white-box attribute inference. Truex et al. (2019) surveyed threats in federated settings. Song et al. (2017) showed model inversion vulnerabilities with gradient signals. Zhu et al. (2019) reconstructed data from gradients. These findings inform our threat model and the choice of privacy accounting. Our approach integrates secure aggregation with calibrated clipping under a target epsilon. We report results on keyboard prediction and image classification benchmarks. The section concludes with limitations and open questions for deployment at scale.

(2019) described secure aggregation protocols at scale. Hard et al. (2018) presented production deployments on mobile keyboards. Yang et al. (2019) studied communication compression with structured updates.

text: Graph-based recommendation systems leverage user item interactions as relational signals. Recent advances in graph neural networks have reshaped collaborative filtering. We examine scalability, inductive capability, and robustness in industrial workloads. The section outlines representative methods that inform our design. Ying et al. (2018) proposed PinSage for large-scale recommendation on graphs. He et al. (2020) introduced LightGCN by simplifying message passing weights. Wu et al. (2019) provided a comprehensive review of deep GNN architectures. Hamilton et al. (2017) developed GraphSAGE for inductive representation learning. Xu et al. (2019) analyzed expressivity through the Weisfeiler-Lehman test. Kipf and Welling (2017) presented GCNs for semi-supervised classification on citation networks. Tasks include ranking. These works highlight alignment between neighbor aggregation and personalization objectives. Data sparsity and cold-start conditions remain enduring challenges for production systems. Negative sampling strategies affect both bias and variance in implicit feedback training. Rendle (2009) introduced Bayesian Personalized Ranking for pairwise objectives. He et al. (2017) proposed Neural Collaborative Filtering with multilayer perceptrons. Xie et al. (2020) studied self-supervised signals for graph augmentation and denoising. We also consider explainability constraints when deploying models in consumer platforms. Ribeiro et al. (2016) proposed LIME for local model explanations. Lundberg and Lee (2017) introduced SHAP values for feature attribution. Ying et al. (2019) designed GNNExplainer to highlight subgraphs. Pope et al. (2019) evaluated saliency for graph classification. Vu and Thai (2020) studied perturbation-based explanation for community detection. Schnake et al. (2020) discussed axioms for GNN interpretability. These explanation methods inform our evaluation metrics for transparency and trust. We operationalize constraints via regularization that encourages sparse neighborhood usage. Our approach balances retrieval quality and latency under strict service-level objectives. Experiments cover multiple datasets with distinct connectivity profiles. We conclude with open problems on robustness to distribution shifts in dynamic graphs.

text: Neural machine translation has evolved rapidly with advances in attention and training regimes. The field has seen improvements in modeling capacity, data utilization, and decoding efficiency. Our study investigates practical domain adaptation under budgeted compute and limited parallel data. We position our approach within a landscape of architectures and training techniques. Foundational contributions provide the context for our modeling decisions. Bahdanau et al. (2015) introduced attention for sequence-to-sequence translation. Vaswani et al. (2017) presented the Transformer architecture based solely on attention. Gehring et al. (2017) developed convolutional sequence models for translation. Wu et al. (2016) scaled NMT with Google's production system. Ott et al. (2018) optimized training efficiency with large batches. Johnson et al. (2017) explored multilingual joint training for zero-shot translation. Lu et al. (2018) refined positional encodings. These developments established common baselines and evaluation protocols for bilingual and multilingual settings. Yet performance in specialized domains often lags behind general web text. Data scarcity and terminology drift create persistent errors in rare phrase translation. Researchers have explored selection, generation, and parameter-efficient update strategies. Our method integrates targeted augmentation with adapters and caching mechanisms. We also examine the impact of noisy synthetic data on calibration. Chu et al. (2017) proposed mixed fine-tuning for domain adaptation. Freitag and Al-Onaizan (2016) studied data selection for targeted domains. van der Wees et al. (2017) implemented gradual fine-tuning with dynamic data. Sennrich et al. (2016) introduced back-translation for monolingual data. Edunov et al. (2018) examined noised back-translation. Lample et al. (2018) addressed unsupervised machine translation. Benchmarks. These techniques shape our recipe for adapting models while limiting catastrophic forgetting. We adopt risk-weighted objectives that prefer high-precision terminology retention. The approach maintains latency budgets for streaming subtitle applications. We evaluate on public datasets and internal customer translations. The discussion outlines threats to validity and implications for future deployment.

Johnson et al. (2017) explored multilingual joint training for zero-shot translation. Lu et al. (2018) refined positional encodings.

text: Time series forecasting and anomaly detection are central to reliable operations in enterprise systems. Practitioners require methods that balance accuracy, interpretability, and scalability. We study architectures and diagnostics that support deployment in streaming environments. The literature spans statistical approaches and learned models for diverse horizons. Foundational and recent contributions inform our approach to model selection and evaluation. Taylor and Letham (2018) released Prophet for decomposable forecasting. Hyndman et al. (2008) advanced exponential smoothing state space models. Salinas et al. (2020) presented DeepAR for probabilistic forecasting on Amazon-scale data. Lai et al. (2018) proposed LSTNet combining CNN and RNN components. Sen et al. (2019) introduced multivariate forecasting with temporal convolutional architectures. Bojer and Meldgaard (2020) benchmarked forecasting methods for retail demand. Gasthaus et al. (2019) studied calibration. These models clarify trade-offs between capacity, seasonality handling, and data sparsity. Realistic deployments must cope with missing values and sudden regime changes. Probabilistic outputs enable thresholding based on risk and capacity constraints. Calibration and reliability diagrams assist practitioners in setting service thresholds. We incorporate uncertainty-aware objectives for both short and long horizons. Anomaly detection complements forecasting to protect critical systems. Ren et al. (2019) developed robust seasonal hybrid ESD for anomaly detection. Hundman et al. (2018) analyzed spacecraft telemetry using LSTM-based detectors. Su et al. (2019) proposed RobustSTL for seasonal-trend decomposition in anomalies. Malhotra et al. (2015) used stacked LSTMs for point anomalies. Zhao et al. (2020) examined multivariate correlation structures. Bontemps et al. (2016) surveyed benchmarks. The signals and methods above shape the metrics we use to compare detectors. Our system integrates forecasting priors into anomaly scoring via joint training. We discuss latency, memory budgets, and online retraining schedules. Experiments cover public benchmarks and anonymized production traffic. We close by outlining limitations and directions for adaptive monitoring.

(2019) introduced multivariate forecasting with temporal convolutional architectures. Bojer and Meldgaard (2020) benchmarked forecasting methods for retail demand. Gasthaus et al. (2019) studied calibration.

text: Automated assessment and feedback aim to increase scalability of instruction while maintaining quality. Research has examined validity, bias, and pedagogical alignment of algorithmic scoring. In this section we outline representative methods and resources that inform our system. We target formative feedback for undergraduate writing courses. Foundational work on essay scoring provides historical context. Burstein and Chodorow (1999) developed e-rater for automated essay scoring. Shermis and Burstein (2013) compiled evaluations of AES systems. Page (1966) introduced Project Essay Grade with early NLP features. Attali and Burstein (2006) studied model validity and fairness considerations. Ke and Ng (2019) investigated neural methods for holistic scoring. Taghipour and Ng (2016) applied recurrent architectures to short-answer scoring. Uto et al. (2020) examined document-level coherence signals. Rubrics evolve. These efforts established datasets, rubrics, and scoring conventions used in current benchmarks. Neural models expanded capacity to capture topical relevance and surface fluency. Yet the mapping from features to instructional guidance remains underexplored. We emphasize feedback that is specific, actionable, and aligned with course outcomes. Prior work on educational dialogue informs how suggestions are phrased and sequenced. McNamara et al. (2010) introduced iSTART for reading strategy training with feedback. Bickmore and Picard (2005) explored relational agents for health communication. Wang et al. (2020) built automated feedback models for peer review. Xie et al. (2019) designed critique generation for writing support. Kumar et al. (2018) investigated conversational tutoring for STEM. Real classroom settings add variability. These strands shape our taxonomy of feedback types and system affordances. We implement controllable generation with constraints derived from instructor rubrics. The interface supports revision planning and reflection prompts. Experiments evaluate content coverage, tone appropriateness, and revision uptake. We report ablations on constraint strength and human in the loop checks. The section concludes with limitations and implications for blended classrooms.

Ke and Ng (2019) investigated neural methods for holistic scoring. Taghipour and Ng (2016) applied recurrent architectures to short-answer scoring. Uto et al. (2020) examined document-level coherence signals. Rubrics evolve.

text: This paper investigates multimodal abstractive summarization for instructional videos. Prior work emphasizes aligning textual transcripts with visual cues to improve content fidelity. We argue that alignment benefits arise from grounding entity references across modalities. Recent benchmarks expose gaps in transfer to unseen domains and procedural variations. Our method introduces a retrieval-guided cross-attention that conditions on latent event indices. We compare against strong baselines and analyze domain robustness across datasets. Kim et al. (2019) developed a captioning model with region-level attention for cooking videos using weak supervision signals. Lee and Park (2020) presented a reinforcement learning approach to summarize documentaries from scripts and soundtrack metadata. Alvarez et al. (2021) explored disentangled topic control for news highlights with controllable generation and sparse priors. Deng et al. (2022) studied frame-level video parsing to detect key events in sports broadcasts with heuristics. These contributions differ from our setting, which targets procedural schemas rather than scene labels. We operationalize schemas via latent steps predicted from transcripts and constrained by tool usage. The training objective combines contrastive alignment with summary reconstruction under coverage penalties. We release a new benchmark with annotated steps and multi-granular summaries for evaluation. Our experiments demonstrate consistent gains on ROUGE, factuality, and step coverage across domains. We also provide ablations and error analysis to guide future research on grounded summarization.

Lee and Park (2020) presented a reinforcement learning approach to summarize documentaries from scripts and soundtrack metadata. Alvarez et al. (2021) explored disentangled topic control for news highlights with controllable generation and sparse priors. Deng et al. (2022) studied frame-level video parsing to detect key events in sports broadcasts with heuristics.

Lee and Park (2020) presented a reinforcement learning approach to summarize documentaries from scripts and soundtrack metadata. Alvarez et al. (2021) explored disentangled topic control for news highlights with controllable generation and sparse priors. Deng et al. text: Detecting misinformation on social platforms requires models that account for content, context, and propagation. Prior approaches typically learn textual cues of deception and stylistic anomalies at the post level. Social signals, including user credibility and interaction patterns, further modulate classifier reliability. Temporal dynamics have been shown to capture early-stage diffusion signatures predictive of veracity. Our work integrates these facets by jointly modeling content, users, and cascades in a unified framework. We emphasize early detection where evidence is sparse and decisions are time critical. Gupta et al. (2018) introduced stance-aware aggregation to infer rumor veracity from reply threads on Twitter data. Shu and Wang (2019) proposed user embedding strategies that encode profile features and interaction histories for detection. Kwon et al. (2020) examined temporal Hawkes processes to model cascade intensities associated with misinformation on platforms. Yao et al. (2021) studied cross-platform adaptation with weak supervision to transfer detectors across communities at scale. While informative, these lines of work do not resolve the scarcity of early evidence across modalities. We address this by predicting latent support relations among posts to amplify weak but consistent cues. The model couples a graph attention backbone with a temporally causal encoder for partial cascades. A curriculum schedules supervision from synthetic seeds to organic signals as timelines unfold. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks show improved timeliness without sacrificing accuracy. We release code, datasets, and analysis protocols to facilitate reproducibility and comparative studies.

(2021) studied cross-platform adaptation with weak supervision to transfer detectors across communities at scale.

text: Automated program repair aims to generate patches that restore correctness with minimal developer intervention. Search-based approaches explore candidate edits guided by test outcomes or inferred invariants. Neural models learn edit sequences from large corpora of buggy and fixed code pairs. The challenge remains to balance patch plausibility with true fix validity under uncertain specifications. We propose a constraint-conditioned repair model that enumerates edits consistent with semantic properties. Our approach integrates static analysis feedback into a token-level transformer through differentiable proxies. Le Goues et al. (2012) pioneered genetic programming for repair, exploring mutation operators and fitness heuristics extensively. Tufano et al. (2019) framed patch generation as sequence translation from buggy to fixed code with attention. Chen et al. (2021) investigated constraint solving guided by learned priors to prune inconsistent edit candidates early. Jiang and Monperrus (2022) assessed overfitting by contrasting plausible patches against independent test suites in practice extensively. These studies highlight diverse signals that can steer search yet leave semantics weakly integrated. We instead treat program states as constraints whose satisfaction is differentiably approximated during training. Candidate edits are generated under property-aware masking to avoid invalid constructs early in decoding. A verifier refines the shortlist with symbolic execution and bounded satisfiability checks. On Defects4J and QuixBugs, our method improves correct fixes and reduces overfitting patches. Analysis of failure modes reveals remaining gaps in specification coverage and static analysis fidelity.

Jiang and Monperrus (2022) assessed overfitting by contrasting plausible patches against independent test suites in practice extensively.

text: Domain adaptation for neural machine translation seeks robust performance when training and deployment domains diverge. Fine-tuning on small in-domain corpora often yields overfitting and catastrophic forgetting of general knowledge. Regularization and data selection strategies attempt to reconcile plasticity with stability during adaptation. We view adaptation as learning domain factors that modulate representations and decoding preferences. Our method introduces a lightweight adapter with domain-prompts estimated from unlabeled target-domain text. The approach supports continual updates without full retraining or extensive parameter growth. Luong and Manning (2015) explored domain tags to bias translation toward specific styles and terminology in practice. Koehn and Knowles (2017) surveyed challenges in NMT, noting sensitivity to test domain shifts and brittleness widely. Britz et al. (2019) studied data selection heuristics that filter training examples by cross-entropy difference scores offline. Zhao et al. (2021) examined meta-learning schedules for rapid adaptation across multiple target domains with minimal data. While related, these directions do not capture domain factors as explicit controllable variables. We estimate low-dimensional prompts from unlabeled monolingual text using contrastive language modeling. The prompts condition encoder adapters and decoder biases to express domain preferences. A replay buffer stabilizes earlier domains through stochastic interleaving during updates. Experiments on medical, legal, and subtitles domains show gains in BLEU and domain-term accuracy. We analyze domain controllability by probing lexical and syntactic shifts induced by learned prompts.

(2021) examined meta-learning schedules for rapid adaptation across multiple target domains with minimal data.

text: Federated learning enables on-device training without centralizing raw data but struggles with client heterogeneity. Personalization methods aim to tailor models to individual clients while retaining global knowledge sharing. Techniques include parameter decoupling, representation learning, and meta-learning for fast adaptation. We propose a bilevel objective that aligns shared features while optimizing client-specific heads. The approach balances fairness and accuracy by regularizing deviations from the shared representation. We evaluate across vision and language benchmarks with varying participation and drift. Smith et al. (2018) introduced FedAvg to aggregate client updates across rounds under limited communication constraints effectively. Li et al. (2020) analyzed objective inconsistency and proposed proximal regularization to stabilize training under heterogeneity conditions. Collins et al. (2021) investigated representation sharing to separate global features from personalized predictors in practice widely. Arivazhagan et al. (2022) developed partial weight sharing to address system heterogeneity across devices of varying capacity. These studies illuminate fundamental trade-offs but leave open how to coordinate shared and private objectives. Our bilevel formulation optimizes a global encoder while learning per-client heads with implicit gradients. A fairness prior penalizes disparate performance across demographic partitions during personalization. We adopt adaptive sampling to emphasize underperforming clients at each communication round. Results indicate consistent improvements in accuracy and parity across diverse datasets and participation rates. We provide reproducible code and ablations to assess the sensitivity to client drift and data volume.

(2020) analyzed objective inconsistency and proposed proximal regularization to stabilize training under heterogeneity conditions. Collins et al. (2021) investigated representation sharing to separate global features from personalized predictors in practice widely. Arivazhagan et al. (2022) developed partial weight sharing to address system heterogeneity across devices of varying capacity.

(2020) analyzed objective inconsistency and proposed proximal regularization to stabilize training under heterogeneity conditions. Collins et al. (2022) developed partial weight sharing to address system heterogeneity across devices of varying capacity.

text: This section reviews advances in personalized federated learning and identifies unresolved challenges. We focus on algorithms that balance global utility with client heterogeneity in realistic deployments. Early studies emphasized convergence under non-IID sampling and communication constraints. Recent work has expanded to personalization layers, selective aggregation, and task clustering. These trends motivate revisiting objective design and evaluation protocols for fairness across clients. We summarize representative contributions before outlining our modeling choices. Smith et al. (2019) reported accuracy gains from client clustering in cross-device federated learning. Zhao and Li (2020) examined gradient divergence with non-IID data across hospitals. Kumar et al. (2021) introduced adaptive aggregation using meta-learning for personalized models in mobile keyboards. Deng (2022) studied privacy amplification under partial participation regimes only. While these studies target different aspects, they collectively underscore the need for adaptive objectives. Personalization via proximal regularizers has been explored to control drift between client and global parameters. Task routing approaches exploit clustering signals to guide aggregation frequency and gradient mixing. Meta-learning variants aim to accelerate on-device adaptation with few local steps. Communication-efficient strategies compress updates while preserving personalization benefits. Benchmarks now include user-level datasets with temporal drift and sparse activity patterns. Privacy constraints further complicate evaluation because gradient masking can hinder personalization. Robustness to adversarial or corrupted clients has emerged as a parallel design goal. Cross-silo settings introduce stronger statistical shifts and longer training horizons. Our study builds on these advances by coupling sample-level reweighting with client-conditioned updates. We provide ablations that isolate optimization, privacy, and communication effects. The resulting framework offers practical guidance for deployment across diverse application domains.
span_text: Smith et al. (2019) reported accuracy gains from client clustering in cross-device federated learning. Zhao and Li (2020) examined gradient divergence with non-IID data across hospitals. Kumar et al. (2021) introduced adaptive aggregation using meta-learning for personalized models in mobile keyboards. Deng (2022) studied privacy amplification under partial participation regimes only.
source_span_field: Smith et al. (2019) reported accuracy gains from client clustering in cross-device federated learning. Zhao and Li (2020) examined gradient divergence with non-IID data across hospitals. Kumar et al. (2021) introduced adaptive aggregation using meta-learning for personalized models in mobile keyboards. Deng (2022) studied privacy amplification under partial participation regimes only.

text: This section reviews prompt design and retrieval-augmented generation for grounded language modeling. We highlight strategies that reduce hallucination while retaining fluency and task generality. Early retrieval systems relied on sparse lexical matching and reranking pipelines. Neural retrievers introduced dense representations and improved recall for open-domain questions. Augmentation policies now consider latency, faithfulness, and prompt budget. Recent works integrate verifiability constraints into decoding. We first summarize developments in prompting and instruction optimization. Brown et al. (2020) presented large-scale language models with few-shot capabilities. Gao et al. (2021) studied prompt-based retrieval for zero-shot classification. Liu et al. (2021) surveyed prompt design and tuning strategies across tasks. Wei et al. (2022) explored behaviors in chains of thought prompting. Reynolds and McDonell (2021) analyzed calibration effects. These studies form a backdrop for retrieval-conditioned prompting with controllable evidence. Our approach formalizes grounding as a constraint on token probabilities using document attributions. We design prompts that expose evidence slots and calibrate them with retrieval confidence. A caching layer amortizes retriever costs over similar contexts. We compare contrastive decoding with constrained beam search for faithfulness. Metrics include answer accuracy, attribution precision, and factuality under paraphrase stress tests. We evaluate on multi-hop datasets that require compositional reasoning across documents. Ablations quantify sensitivity to retriever recall and instruction phrasing. Human raters assess justification adequacy and linguistic quality. The review positions our method within the spectrum of grounding techniques.
span_text: Brown et al. (2020) presented large-scale language models with few-shot capabilities. Gao et al. (2021) studied prompt-based retrieval for zero-shot classification. Liu et al. (2021) surveyed prompt design and tuning strategies across tasks. Wei et al. (2022) explored behaviors in chains of thought prompting. Reynolds and McDonell (2021) analyzed calibration effects.

(2021) surveyed prompt design and tuning strategies across tasks. Wei et al. (2022) explored behaviors in chains of thought prompting. Reynolds and McDonell (2021) analyzed calibration effects.

text: We review methods for molecular property prediction with graph neural networks. The literature spans representation design, training objectives, and dataset curation. Classical cheminformatics features motivated early neural architectures for molecules. Recent models exploit message passing to capture local chemistry and long-range effects. Pretraining strategies draw on self-supervised tasks over large compound libraries. Uncertainty estimation and calibration have become central for decision support. We organize prior work by representation learning, supervision signals, and evaluation. Within representation learning, seminal contributions established foundational operators for graphs. Duvenaud et al. (2015) introduced neural fingerprints for graphs. Gilmer et al. (2017) formulated message passing neural networks for quantum chemistry. Kearnes et al. (2016) proposed graph convolutions for molecular features. Yang et al. (2019) curated benchmark datasets for standardized evaluation. Ramakrishnan et al. (2014) released QM9 with computed properties labels. These contributions inform our design of physically inspired interaction blocks. We extend message passing with directional information and distance-aware attention. Our objective balances predictive accuracy with calibrated uncertainty. We adopt scaffold splits to measure robustness to chemical novelty. We further assess transfer to reaction condition prediction. Ablations examine contributions from geometric encodings and pooling schemes. We analyze error patterns across functional groups and molecular sizes. Our benchmarks include classification and regression tasks across pharmaceutically relevant endpoints. This review situates our approach among scalable GNNs for chemistry.

(2016) proposed graph convolutions for molecular features. Yang et al. (2019) curated benchmark datasets for standardized evaluation. Ramakrishnan et al. (2014) released QM9 with computed properties labels.

(2016) proposed graph convolutions for molecular features. Yang et al. (2019) curated benchmark datasets for standardized evaluation. Ramakrishnan et al. (2014) released QM9 with computed properties labels.

text: This section surveys self-supervised learning for medical image analysis. The goal is to leverage unlabeled scans to improve downstream performance and sample efficiency. Medical datasets differ from natural images in texture, anatomy, and acquisition artifacts. As a result, pretext tasks and augmentations must reflect clinical priors. Contrastive and distillation-based methods have dominated recent progress. We group approaches by proxy objectives and pretraining corpora. Canonical algorithms developed in natural images have inspired adaptations to radiology and pathology. He et al. (2020) proposed momentum contrast for instance discrimination. Chen et al. (2020) introduced SimCLR with strong augmentations and contrastive losses. Caron et al. (2021) presented DINO using self-distillation without labels. Grill et al. (2020) developed BYOL relying on momentum targets. Gidaris et al. (2018) explored rotation prediction pretext tasks. These algorithms motivate medical variants that incorporate anatomy-aware transformations. Slice shuffling, intensity scaling, and spatial dropout can mimic realistic protocol variation. Multi-view learning aligns sequences across modalities such as T1, T2, and FLAIR. Label-efficient fine-tuning strategies reduce annotation burdens for rare diseases. Calibration and robustness are crucial for deployment in safety-critical settings. Self-supervised features also benefit semi-supervised segmentation and detection. Domain shifts across scanners and institutions remain a challenge. We study pretraining on mixed-modality corpora with adversarial harmonization. Our ablations dissect augmentation strength, objective weighting, and sampling policies. The review delineates open problems in scalable medical self-supervision.

(2021) presented DINO using self-distillation without labels. Grill et al. (2020) developed BYOL relying on momentum targets. Gidaris et al. (2018) explored rotation prediction pretext tasks.

(2021) presented DINO using self-distillation without labels. Grill et al. (2020) developed BYOL relying on momentum targets. Gidaris et al. (2018) explored rotation prediction pretext tasks.

text: We review transformer-based models for climate time series forecasting. Accurate long-range forecasts are vital for water, energy, and agriculture planning. Classical statistical models capture seasonality but struggle with multivariate nonstationarity. Deep architectures promise better scalability to heterogeneous sensors and missing data. Transformers adapt attention mechanisms to extract long-distance dependencies in irregular sequences. Climate tasks further require physical constraints and uncertainty quantification. We organize prior work by architectural innovations and inductive biases. Vaswani et al. (2017) introduced Transformers for sequence modeling without recurrence. Wu et al. (2021) proposed Autoformer for long-term time-series decomposition. Zhou et al. (2021) presented Informer with probabilistic sparse attention for efficiency. Nie et al. (2022) studied seasonal-trend separation in transformer forecasting. Wu et al. (2022) evaluated frequency-domain designs extensively. These developments shape recipe choices for spatiotemporal climate signals. Our method augments attention with conservation-aware embeddings from governing equations. We incorporate bias correction and anomaly normalization within the encoder. A hierarchical decoder resolves multiple temporal horizons with shared context. We evaluate on reanalysis, satellite, and in-situ datasets with standardized splits. Metrics include mean absolute error, continuous ranked probability score, and skill scores. Baselines include statistical models and recent transformer variants. We analyze sensitivity to context length, resolution, and data sparsity. Ablations quantify contributions from physics-guided features and probabilistic objectives. This review situates the approach in the landscape of climate forecasting.

(2021) presented Informer with probabilistic sparse attention for efficiency. Nie et al. (2022) studied seasonal-trend separation in transformer forecasting. Wu et al. (2022) evaluated frequency-domain designs extensively.

(2021) presented Informer with probabilistic sparse attention for efficiency. Nie et al. (2022) studied seasonal-trend separation in transformer forecasting. Wu et al. (2022) evaluated frequency-domain designs extensively.

text: Recommender systems increasingly mediate access to information, entertainment, and opportunities across online platforms. Concerns about fairness and accountability have spurred a wave of research on measuring and mitigating harms in ranking pipelines. Prior work has examined disparities that arise from both user-side preferences and system-side optimization pressures. Our study focuses on exposure fairness in top-k recommendation with implicit feedback. We investigate how reranking with counterfactual estimators affects utility and equity under realistic feedback loops. Yao and Huang (2017) examine exposure disparity in matrix factorization models using synthetic preference shifts. Ekstrand et al. (2018) conduct a large-scale audit of recommender outputs in a public library setting and report demographic skews in item visibility. Burke (2017) introduces a multi-stakeholder perspective that formalizes provider and consumer utilities in ranking objectives, and Chen et al. (2020) propose calibration metrics that align recommendation lists with user profiles better. Despite a growing taxonomy of fairness notions, there remains little consensus on how to balance provider utility against user satisfaction during reoptimization. Existing evaluation protocols often conflate accuracy gains with equitable allocation of attention, complicating empirical comparisons. We build on recent advances in stochastic ranking to derive differentiable exposure constraints compatible with large-scale training. The method accommodates position bias and slate diversity while preserving tractable gradients. We further introduce a simulation environment that reproduces recommendation dynamics under strategic behavior from content providers. This enables controlled analysis of feedback-induced drift and delayed impacts of exposure balancing. Our contributions include a principled objective, an efficient solver, and an extensible benchmark for auditing exposure trade-offs.

(2018) conduct a large-scale audit of recommender outputs in a public library setting and report demographic skews in item visibility. Burke (2017) introduces a multi-stakeholder perspective that formalizes provider and consumer utilities in ranking objectives, and Chen et al. (2020) propose calibration metrics that align recommendation lists with user profiles better.

(2018) conduct a large-scale audit of recommender outputs in a public library setting and report demographic skews in item visibility. Burke (2017) introduces a multi-stakeholder perspective that formalizes provider and consumer utilities in ranking objectives, and Chen et al. (2020) propose calibration metrics that align recommendation lists with user profiles better.

text: Text summarization research has moved from handcrafted heuristics to neural architectures that learn salience from data. Despite strong fluency, neural systems often overlook structural signals that govern information flow in documents. Graph-based methods offer a complementary perspective by explicitly modeling inter-sentence relations and topical cohesion. Our work studies graph induction for extractive summarization under limited supervision. We propose a contrastive objective that aligns graph edges with redundancy reduction goals. The framework accommodates heterogeneous node features derived from syntax, discourse, and semantics. Nallapati et al. (2017) present a neural extractive model with sequence labeling over sentence salience. Zhong et al. (2020) formulate sentence selection as submodular optimization guided by coverage proxies learned from BERT representations. Yasunaga et al. (2017) introduce a citation graph dataset and a GCN-based summarizer for scientific papers, and Xiao and Carenini (2019) explore discourse-aware attention mechanisms to weight rhetorical structures across diverse domains and document lengths effectively. While these advances inform modeling choices, scalability and stability under domain shift remain open challenges. We analyze sparsification schemes that retain key relations without amplifying noise from spurious edges. The proposed encoder layers enforce global normalization to prevent overemphasis on hub sentences. We introduce a cross-domain benchmark spanning news, reviews, and scientific abstracts with matched length distributions. Experiments demonstrate consistent gains in ROUGE and factual consistency with minimal memory overhead. An ablation study isolates the impact of each graph signal and highlights useful inductive biases.

(2020) formulate sentence selection as submodular optimization guided by coverage proxies learned from BERT representations. Yasunaga et al. (2017) introduce a citation graph dataset and a GCN-based summarizer for scientific papers, and Xiao and Carenini (2019) explore discourse-aware attention mechanisms to weight rhetorical structures across diverse domains and document lengths effectively.

(2020) formulate sentence selection as submodular optimization guided by coverage proxies learned from BERT representations. Yasunaga et al. (2017) introduce a citation graph dataset and a GCN-based summarizer for scientific papers, and Xiao and Carenini (2019) explore discourse-aware attention mechanisms to weight rhetorical structures across diverse domains and document lengths effectively.

text: Multimodal emotion recognition aims to infer affective states from coordinated audio, visual, and linguistic cues. Robust fusion remains difficult because signals exhibit different sampling rates, noise profiles, and context dependencies. Prior research has alternated between early fusion, late fusion, and hybrid attention mechanisms with mixed results. We investigate sequence alignment and representation sharing for conversational settings with overlapping turns. Our approach treats modality-specific encoders as interchangeable views constrained by a cross-reconstruction objective. The design targets robustness to missing modalities during deployment and realistic background noise. We evaluate on benchmarks featuring diverse speaking styles and camera conditions. Zadeh et al. (2018) release the CMU-MOSEI corpus with aligned language, vision, and audio modalities for sentiment and emotion tasks. Poria et al. (2017) propose a hierarchical fusion network that integrates convolutional and recurrent encoders across modalities. Tsai et al. (2019) introduce factorized multimodal attention to disentangle cross-modal interactions while preserving unimodal contributions. Hazarika et al. (2020) curate conversational datasets and adapt memory networks for context-sensitive emotion inference tasks. Despite apparent progress, models tend to overfit speaker identities, masking limited generalization to new environments. We propose a regularizer that penalizes identity leakage through adversarial disentanglement. The training scheme interleaves curriculum masking of modalities to simulate sensor failures. Results indicate improved robustness without sacrificing accuracy on fully observed inputs.

(2017) propose a hierarchical fusion network that integrates convolutional and recurrent encoders across modalities. Tsai et al. (2019) introduce factorized multimodal attention to disentangle cross-modal interactions while preserving unimodal contributions. Hazarika et al. (2020) curate conversational datasets and adapt memory networks for context-sensitive emotion inference tasks.

(2017) propose a hierarchical fusion network that integrates convolutional and recurrent encoders across modalities. Tsai et al. (2019) introduce factorized multimodal attention to disentangle cross-modal interactions while preserving unimodal contributions. Hazarika et al. (2020) curate conversational datasets and adapt memory networks for context-sensitive emotion inference tasks.

text: Code generation models benefit from large training corpora but often stumble on rare APIs and domain-specific patterns. Data augmentation offers a path to broaden coverage without manual annotation. Prior attempts include paraphrasing natural language queries and mutating code snippets while preserving semantics. We study augmentation for neural semantic parsers that target library-specific functions and arguments. Our method composes transformation operators guided by type constraints and unit tests. By sampling from inferred input distributions, we diversify program states that challenge decoders. We analyze augmentation effects through controlled evaluations and ablation studies. The approach is compatible with pretrained encoder-decoder architectures and lightweight to integrate. Yin and Neubig (2017) translate natural language to code via a syntax-constrained decoder over abstract syntax trees. Chen et al. (2021) pretrain on large corpora of source code and natural language to enable few-shot synthesis. Austin et al. (2021) study program synthesis by generating execution traces alongside code, and Jain et al. (2020) propose schema-guided generation for APIs across diverse repositories and programming paradigms with standardized evaluation settings reported. Despite broad pretraining, models exhibit brittle behavior when confronted with parameter order variations and optional flags. We introduce a canonicalization layer that neutralizes superficial differences before decoding. Augmented examples further encourage consistent attention to function signatures and types. Experiments across three domains show consistent gains in exact match and execution accuracy.
span_text: Yin and Neubig (2017) translate natural language to code via a syntax-constrained decoder over abstract syntax trees. Chen et al. (2021) pretrain on large corpora of source code and natural language to enable few-shot synthesis. Austin et al. (2021) study program synthesis by generating execution traces alongside code, and Jain et al. (2020) propose schema-guided generation for APIs across diverse repositories and programming paradigms with standardized evaluation settings reported.

(2021) pretrain on large corpora of source code and natural language to enable few-shot synthesis. Austin et al. (2021) study program synthesis by generating execution traces alongside code, and Jain et al. (2020) propose schema-guided generation for APIs across diverse repositories and programming paradigms with standardized evaluation settings reported.

text: Annotation for named entity recognition remains costly due to token-level labels and domain expertise requirements. Active learning seeks to reduce labeling effort by selecting informative sequences for annotation. Despite progress, uncertainty estimation for structured prediction is still fragile under distribution shift. We revisit query strategies tailored to sequence tagging with contextual encoders. Our framework unifies token, span, and sentence utilities under constrained budget optimization. Settles (2009) surveys query strategies for pool-based sampling with uncertainty and expected error reduction criteria. Schein and Ungar (2007) analyze active learning under logistic loss with informative priors for text classification. Shen et al. (2018) apply deep Bayesian active learning with dropout-based uncertainty to sequence tagging, and Siddhant and Lipton (2018) benchmark active learning with neural text classifiers on multiple datasets and budget regimes with standardized sampling costs reported. Existing NER studies often ignore annotation latency and cognitive load when comparing strategies. We incorporate cost-sensitive budgets based on measured labeling times and error types. The proposed method calibrates uncertainty using posterior temperature learned from held-out data. We design a batched selection protocol that balances diversity with exploitation across domains. Experiments cover biomedical, legal, and social media corpora with divergent label spaces. Results show improved F1 per labeled token and more stable gains across acquisition rounds. We release code and annotation guidelines to facilitate reproducible evaluation.
span_text: Settles (2009) surveys query strategies for pool-based sampling with uncertainty and expected error reduction criteria. Schein and Ungar (2007) analyze active learning under logistic loss with informative priors for text classification. Shen et al. (2018) apply deep Bayesian active learning with dropout-based uncertainty to sequence tagging, and Siddhant and Lipton (2018) benchmark active learning with neural text classifiers on multiple datasets and budget regimes with standardized sampling costs reported.

Schein and Ungar (2007) analyze active learning under logistic loss with informative priors for text classification. Shen et al. (2018) apply deep Bayesian active learning with dropout-based uncertainty to sequence tagging, and Siddhant and Lipton (2018) benchmark active learning with neural text classifiers on multiple datasets and budget regimes with standardized sampling costs reported.

text: This paper investigates retrieval-augmented natural language inference for multi-hop reasoning. Recent advances in pretrained language models have transformed the design of NLI systems. Prior work shows that explicit retrieval can expose latent knowledge and reduce hallucinations. We focus on how retrievers and readers coordinate during training. Dense bi-encoders and cross-encoders present complementary trade-offs for evidence aggregation. Existing surveys synthesize techniques across open-domain QA and fact verification. Smith et al. (2019) proposed a dual-encoder retriever trained with in-batch negatives on large corpora. Karpukhin et al. (2020) introduced DPR for question answering with improvements from hard negative mining. Xiong et al. (2021) explored ANCE to refine retrieval with iterative indexing updates. Chen et al. (2017) presented DrQA using TF-IDF and a document reader for evidence extraction. Lee et al. (2019) studied multi-hop retrieval training. These approaches were developed for open-domain QA, yet their coordination with entailment remains underexplored. Subsequent research examined joint retrieval with natural language inference supervision. Adversarial evaluation has revealed brittleness to distractor passages and annotation artifacts. Our study builds on retrieval signals to support premise selection over multi-document contexts. We train a reader that scores hypotheses conditioned on compact evidence caches. The retriever optimizes for entailment-aware representations using weak supervision harvested from SNLI and FEVER. We compare to bi-encoder, cross-encoder, and fusion architectures under identical retrieval budgets. We analyze calibration, evidence attribution, and label consistency across domains. The contributions include a modular training recipe, a robust evaluation suite, and an ablation tracing the role of negative sampling.

(2020) introduced DPR for question answering with improvements from hard negative mining. Xiong et al. (2021) explored ANCE to refine retrieval with iterative indexing updates. Chen et al. (2017) presented DrQA using TF-IDF and a document reader for evidence extraction. Lee et al. (2019) studied multi-hop retrieval training.

text: This section reviews graph-based recommender systems with an emphasis on message passing designs. Recommenders exploit user item interactions as heterogeneous graphs to encode inductive biases. Early matrix factorization has been superseded by deep encoders over interaction neighborhoods. We examine negative sampling and propagation depth as major design axes. Attention mechanisms modulate the contribution of neighbors during embedding updates. Contrastive objectives have improved robustness to popularity bias and cold-start effects. Temporal signals introduce challenges in preserving causality during training. We also discuss scalability on dynamic graphs with batched mini-sampling. Prior surveys emphasize explainability but understate calibration and uncertainty. Ying et al. (2018) introduced PinSage for web-scale recommendation using random-walk sampling. He et al. (2020) proposed LightGCN by simplifying propagation to retain only essential neighborhood aggregation. Wang et al. (2019) developed NGCF to encode collaborative signals through non-linear interactions between embeddings. Wu et al. (2019) surveyed graph neural networks with taxonomy and design patterns relevant to information propagation. Zhang et al. (2020) examined session-based recommendation. While these studies shaped the field, their implications for uncertainty estimation remain open. We target calibrated ranking that honors both preference signals and abstention. Our method augments propagation with temperature-scaled targets under a variational view. We incorporate timestamped edges to disentangle exposure from adoption. The training protocol balances pairwise ranking and entropy regularization. We benchmark on three public datasets with controlled sparsity and temporal splits. Ablations and case studies quantify trade-offs between depth, sampling, and calibration.

text: We review advances in cross-lingual named entity recognition with transfer from high-resource languages. The central challenge is aligning lexical and contextual signals under limited annotation. Pretrained multilingual encoders provide a foundation for zero-shot and few-shot transfer. Character and subword modeling mitigate script mismatch and morphological variation. Xie et al. (2018) proposed multilingual adversarial training to align sentence representations across languages. Wu and Dredze (2019) studied zero-shot transfer with multilingual BERT on NER benchmarks. Rahimi et al. (2019) examined projection methods that map labels via parallel corpora. Pan et al. (2017) introduced distant supervision using Wikipedia titles to bootstrap entity annotations. Conneau et al. (2020) released XLM-R with strong multilingual contextual representations pretraining. These techniques reduce annotation cost but leave open questions about domain shifts. Entity boundary conventions and casing differ across corpora in ways that hurt transfer. We address these issues by disentangling lexical and label-space alignment. Our model learns task-specific prototypes anchored by agreement across languages. We incorporate contrastive objectives at token and span levels. A curriculum schedules lexicon coverage before fine-grained type separation. We evaluate zero-shot and few-shot settings across five diverse scripts. The analysis highlights robustness to noisy projections and partial transliteration. Efficiency is achieved with lightweight adapters and batch-wise language sampling. Our contributions include a unified framework and diagnostic benchmarks. We release code and datasets to stimulate reproducible progress. Ethical considerations regarding name privacy and bias are discussed.

(2017) introduced distant supervision using Wikipedia titles to bootstrap entity annotations. Conneau et al. (2020) released XLM-R with strong multilingual contextual representations pretraining.

text: This section surveys anomaly detection in multivariate industrial time series. Industrial sensors produce correlated streams with nonstationarity and regime shifts. Traditional methods rely on thresholds and seasonal decomposition with limited generalizability. Deep learning offers adaptive representations that capture cross-channel dependencies. Autoencoders and forecasting models dominate recent approaches. Attention mechanisms enable selective focus on salient temporal patterns. Change-point detection assists in separating fault onset from normal variability. Unsupervised settings are common due to scarce labeled anomalies. Evaluation protocols often confound detection delay with detection accuracy. We aim to design detectors that respect latency constraints and calibration. We also examine the role of synthetic anomalies for stress testing. Recent benchmarks reveal gaps in cross-facility generalization. Malhotra et al. (2016) proposed LSTM-Encoder to learn representations for anomaly scoring on sequences. Hundman et al. (2018) analyzed satellite telemetry with LSTM forecasting and residual thresholding. Su et al. (2019) introduced RobustSTL for seasonal-trend decomposition resistant to noise in industrial signals. Ren et al. (2019) presented DAGMM combining autoencoders with density estimation for unsupervised detection. Zong et al. (2018) proposed DeepSVDD for compact hypersphere modeling. These studies illuminate modeling choices yet leave uncertainty estimation largely unaddressed. Our approach augments forecasting residuals with conformal calibration to bound alarm rates. We design a streaming tester that enforces decision budgets under drift. Experiments on three industrial datasets validate timely and reliable detection.

Ren et al. (2019) presented DAGMM combining autoencoders with density estimation for unsupervised detection. Zong et al. (2018) proposed DeepSVDD for compact hypersphere modeling.

text: Program synthesis with large language models has advanced rapidly in recent years. The objective is to map specifications or tests to executable code. Models leverage pretraining over massive code corpora augmented with natural language. Execution-guided decoding remains critical for correctness on complex benchmarks. We study planning and verification signals for constrained generation. The literature spans prompt engineering, search, and neuro-symbolic integration. We analyze generalization beyond in-distribution libraries and idioms. We also examine robustness to ambiguous or underspecified tasks. Chen et al. (2021) released Codex with impressive few-shot code generation capabilities. Austin et al. (2021) studied program synthesis with encoder-decoder transformers trained on permissively licensed repositories. Li et al. (2022) introduced AlphaCode with competitive performance on programming contests through large-scale sampling. Le et al. (2022) explored execution feedback by interleaving test runs with hypothesis generation. Nijkamp et al. (2022) investigated self-consistency for sampling diverse candidates. Despite remarkable progress, failure cases persist on long-horizon reasoning and tool use. Benchmarks rarely capture distribution shifts across APIs, platforms, and styles. We propose a verifier-guided planner that grounds generation in symbolic constraints. A lightweight abstract interpreter prunes partial programs that violate types or contracts. We train with counterexample-guided optimization using failing tests from execution traces. The search policy balances diversity and verifiability under a fixed budget. Experiments on HumanEval and MBPP show gains in pass rates and reliability. Ablations indicate that planning depth and verifier coverage are primary factors.

(2022) explored execution feedback by interleaving test runs with hypothesis generation. Nijkamp et al. (2022) investigated self-consistency for sampling diverse candidates.

text: Named entity recognition in biomedical text remains brittle under domain shifts between journals, institutions, and curation policies. Downstream extraction pipelines depend on robust tagging to surface reliable entities for knowledge graph construction. Prior work has explored multitask learning, contextual pretraining, and robust optimization to mitigate dataset bias. Yet most evaluations obscure distributional change by mixing sources during cross validation. We frame adaptation as learning invariances that transfer across ontologies and annotation schemes. Our review summarizes representative methods and clarifies open methodological gaps. Smith et al. (2019) introduce adversarial training for cross-corpus tagging using gradient reversal and shared encoders. Zhao and Huang (2020) present a meta-learning approach that conditions on support sets to adapt label distributions. Koster et al. (2021) study vocabulary mismatch using subword alignment across ontologies and report improved recall. Dataset shifts are modeled with perturbations. These directions highlight design tensions between encoder capacity, domain invariance, and label calibration. Recent benchmarks such as BLURB and BioLM facilitate more transparent comparisons across sources. We emphasize the need for shift-aware validation splits that isolate corpus effects without conflating label noise. Attention to entity boundary conventions can reduce spurious gains from tokenization artifacts. Our study positions adaptation techniques within a unified evaluation scaffold to guide future research. This framing motivates a principled choice of training dynamics and reporting standards for biomedical NER.

text: Recommendation platforms increasingly aim to balance accuracy with diversity and fairness across large catalogs. Traditional top-k metrics reward head items and may entrench popularity bias in ranking policies. Recent approaches incorporate exposure constraints, calibrated objectives, and counterfactual estimation to address systemic imbalance. Yet comparative evidence on long-tail uplift remains inconsistent across datasets and protocols. We review methods that explicitly modulate exposure while preserving user utility in implicit-feedback settings. Our scope covers offline estimators, online bandit updates, and portfolio objectives for slate construction. We analyze how design choices affect discoverability, supplier coverage, and user satisfaction. Steck (2011) examines the trade-off between accuracy and diversity through popularity-based calibration. Vargas and Castells (2014) propose intent-aware metrics that decompose relevance across user subtopics. Chen et al. (2020) train exposure-aware models using inverse propensity weighting to correct selection bias. Catalog growth and shifting user tastes complicate evaluation under perpetual cold-start conditions for long-tail items. These strands motivate evaluation beyond precision to include calibrated exposure and supplier-level outcomes. Public leaderboards should report de-biased estimators alongside naive metrics to contextualize gains. A reference protocol separating retraining and reweighting phases can reduce confounding from implicit feedback loops. We outline a taxonomy linking exposure modeling to catalog structure and user intent granularity. This perspective informs reproducible comparisons and supports principled deployment of fair recommenders.

Vargas and Castells (2014) propose intent-aware metrics that decompose relevance across user subtopics. Chen et al. Catalog growth and shifting user tastes complicate evaluation under perpetual cold-start conditions for long-tail items.

text: Visual models deployed in safety-critical settings must remain reliable under distribution shift and noise. Robustness is particularly salient for architectures that rely on global context rather than local inductive biases. Research has examined augmentation, adversarial training, and test-time adaptation for improving resilience. Despite progress, reported gains often conflate corruption robustness with class reweighting or calibration effects. We focus on patch-based models and consider how tokenization interacts with frequency content of inputs. Our discussion relates robustness metrics to architectural and training design choices. We delineate key datasets and evaluation protocols to structure empirical comparisons. Hendrycks and Dietterich (2019) introduced corruption benchmarks measuring robustness under common perturbations. Dosovitskiy et al. (2021) presented Vision Transformers scaling with large pretraining datasets. Bhojanapalli et al. (2021) analyzed shape bias and patch embeddings in terms of spectral complexity. Input frequency content interacts with augmentation strength and affects transfer to out-of-distribution shifts in safety evaluations. Together these lines of work raise questions about the role of priors in scalable robustness. Methods that improve clean accuracy may degrade stability under compression or photometric distortions. Standardizing corruption severity and reporting uncertainty can reduce misleading claims of generalization. We argue for controlled ablations that isolate data size, augmentation type, and positional encoding. This review outlines principled evaluation guidance and highlights underexplored interactions between architecture and data.
span_text: Hendrycks and Dietterich (2019) introduced corruption benchmarks measuring robustness under common perturbations. Dosovitskiy et al. (2021) presented Vision Transformers scaling with large pretraining datasets. Bhojanapalli et al. (2021) analyzed shape bias and patch embeddings in terms of spectral complexity. Input frequency content interacts with augmentation strength and affects transfer to out-of-distribution shifts in safety evaluations.

(2021) presented Vision Transformers scaling with large pretraining datasets. Bhojanapalli et al. (2021) analyzed shape bias and patch embeddings in terms of spectral complexity. Input frequency content interacts with augmentation strength and affects transfer to out-of-distribution shifts in safety evaluations.

text: Task-oriented dialogue increasingly requires agents to ground language in external tools and knowledge bases. Deployed systems must combine fluent generation with accurate execution under ambiguous user goals. Prior studies emphasize intent detection, slot filling, and end-to-end differentiable planning. Recent pretrained models enable unified architectures that scale with data and parameter count. Nevertheless, tool grounding remains brittle when training distributions diverge from production environments. We review advances connecting social grounding, personalization, and API orchestration for practical assistants. Our overview distinguishes modeling choices that affect latency, controllability, and monitoring. We also examine data collection protocols that capture compositional queries and evolving schemas. Rashkin et al. (2019) curated empathy-centric datasets to encourage socially aware responses. Madotto et al. (2020) used adapter modules to inject persona information into pretrained dialogue models. Parisi et al. (2022) linked conversations with tool invocations by annotating intent slots across tasks. Latency budgets constrain grounding pipelines and shape the design of API orchestrators online. These perspectives illuminate trade-offs between transparency and adaptability in multi-turn interactions. Evaluation should report both task success and calibration of execution statements exposed to users. Human-in-the-loop safeguards can mitigate cascading failures from misgrounded function calls. We propose a measurement suite that links user satisfaction to verifiable tool outcomes.

(2020) used adapter modules to inject persona information into pretrained dialogue models. Parisi et al. (2022) linked conversations with tool invocations by annotating intent slots across tasks. Latency budgets constrain grounding pipelines and shape the design of API orchestrators online.

(2020) used adapter modules to inject persona information into pretrained dialogue models. Parisi et al. (2022) linked conversations with tool invocations by annotating intent slots across tasks. Latency budgets constrain grounding pipelines and shape the design of API orchestrators online.

text: Forecasting at scale requires models that capture seasonality, events, and cross-series structure. Businesses demand calibrated uncertainty to support inventory, staffing, and risk planning decisions. Classical approaches leverage exponential smoothing and state space formulations with interpretable parameters. Deep learning extends these foundations by sharing representations across related time series. Our review compares likelihood choices, covariate design, and hierarchical aggregation strategies. Salinas et al. (2020) introduced DeepAR as an autoregressive RNN forecasting model with likelihood training. Oreshkin et al. (2020) proposed N-BEATS using backward and forward residual stacks for univariate series. Rangapuram et al. (2018) formulated Deep State Space Models to combine transition dynamics with learned encoders. Calendar effects interact with covariates and complicate hierarchical reconciliation. Despite promising results, evaluation practices vary widely across rolling-origin splits and datasets. We argue for standardized backtesting protocols that mirror deployment conditions and decision costs. Probabilistic calibration should be reported using proper scoring rules and coverage diagnostics. Benchmarks must include intermittent demand, regime changes, and censored observations to reflect operations. Realistic exogenous signals often exhibit reporting delays and missingness that require robust preprocessing. We outline open challenges linking reconciliation with capacity constraints and service-level targets. This synthesis organizes methods by inductive bias and highlights opportunities for practical impact.

(2020) proposed N-BEATS using backward and forward residual stacks for univariate series. Rangapuram et al. (2020) proposed N-BEATS using backward and forward residual stacks for univariate series. Rangapuram et al. (2018) formulated Deep State Space Models to combine transition dynamics with learned encoders. Calendar effects interact with covariates and complicate hierarchical reconciliation.

text: Graph-based recommendation has advanced rapidly with deep learning architectures connecting user and item signals. Our work examines how structural sparsity and temporal drift affect embedding propagation in industrial catalogs. We focus on balancing expressivity and scalability under session-level dynamics. He et al. (2020) introduced LightGCN to simplify layer operations for collaborative filtering and careful normalization. Wang et al. (2019) proposed NGCF with explicit modeling of high-order connectivity. Sun et al. (2020) explored multiscale session graphs for sequential recommendation under sparse signals. Qiu et al. (2021) studied contrastive augmentations on bipartite graphs using self-supervised cues. These strands illustrate a crowded space where methodological comparisons are often inconclusive. We extend this space by proposing adaptive neighborhood filters that exploit short-term intent cues. The model integrates a calibrated loss that discourages popularity cascades under shifting traffic. We also analyze cold-start regimes with time-aware side features and bandwidth constraints. The experimental setup leverages public benchmarks and a large semi-anonymized log. We now discuss temporal modeling traditions relevant to evolving graphs. Zhou et al. (2018) modeled item lifecycles with Hawkes processes to capture recency bursts patterns. Kumar et al. (2019) presented time-aware embeddings using decay kernels across evolving interactions and learned interval gates for stability. Zhang and Chang (2020) applied recurrent point processes in session recommendations with adaptive intensities. Rendle et al. (2021) examined periodic demand in retail logs through harmonic representations and drifting covariates for long-horizon evaluations across campaigns and seasons. Together these citations reveal the breadth of tools that coexist across recommendation and temporal learning. Our contribution unifies the perspective by treating drift as an operator that perturbs messages. We derive a stability criterion for personalized PageRank style propagation under nonstationary exposures. The criterion motivates an algorithm that prunes neighborhoods to satisfy a contraction envelope. Empirical results suggest consistent gains on click-through and add-to-cart metrics across domains.

(2019) proposed NGCF with explicit modeling of high-order connectivity. Sun et al. (2020) explored multiscale session graphs for sequential recommendation under sparse signals. Qiu et al. (2021) studied contrastive augmentations on bipartite graphs using self-supervised cues.

(2019) proposed NGCF with explicit modeling of high-order connectivity. Sun et al. (2020) explored multiscale session graphs for sequential recommendation under sparse signals. Qiu et al. (2021) studied contrastive augmentations on bipartite graphs using self-supervised cues.

text: Unsupervised domain adaptation aims to transfer knowledge from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target. Despite progress, feature alignment often collapses semantic structure under strong shifts. We study curriculum strategies that modulate confidence calibration during adaptation. We first situate our approach within prior representation learning and discrepancy minimization. Long et al. (2015) proposed Deep Adaptation Networks to reduce distribution shift via MMD penalties. Ganin and Lempitsky (2015) introduced domain-adversarial objectives with gradient reversal for invariant features. Saito et al. (2018) aligned class boundaries using asymmetric tri-training on unlabeled targets robustly. Zhang et al. (2019) developed conditional alignment with batch-wise statistics in multi-level layers. These directions highlight diverse mechanisms without converging on a shared selection principle. Our method constructs an adaptive pacing schedule tied to density estimates in the target. The schedule integrates pseudo-label risk bounds with temperature sharpening. We test the approach on synthetic and real driving scenes. Prior art on target-aware regularizers is also relevant to our design. Caron et al. (2020) explored self-labeling with clustering assignments for visual pretraining that can warm-start adaptation and stabilize prototypes under noisy pseudo-labels. Chen et al. (2020) studied momentum contrast to maintain instance discrimination over evolving batches during training. Tranheden et al. (2021) investigated do-not-care masks in synthetic-to-real driving to mitigate annotation gaps for improved deployment readiness. Kurmi and Namboodiri (2019) formulated class-conditioned adversaries that modulate confusion through auxiliary predictors across domains. Collectively, these advances motivate a cautious balance between alignment and discriminability. We formalize this balance via a bilevel objective linking entropy and margin. The solver alternates between density-guided labeling and prototype refinement. Theoretical analysis establishes monotonic risk improvement under mild cluster assumptions. Extensive evaluations confirm gains on VisDA, Office-Home, and WildDash.

text: Clinical entity linking maps mentions in narratives to standardized concepts in medical ontologies. Accurate linking supports downstream applications like cohort discovery and pharmacovigilance. Challenges arise from abbreviation ambiguity, domain shift, and limited supervision. We review methods spanning representation learning, candidate generation, and disambiguation. Leaman et al. (2013) optimized dictionary-based tagging with adaptive matching for biomedical mentions. Tutubalina et al. (2018) applied neural ranking over candidate concepts using contextual signals in practice. Ji et al. (2020) exploited ontology graph paths to bias disambiguation in end-to-end models. Sung et al. (2021) explored dense retrieval with bi-encoders trained on biomedical corpora. These developments emphasize the tension between coverage and precision in constrained vocabularies. Our approach introduces a multi-resolution retriever trained with hierarchical contrastive signals. The retriever coordinates lexical, semantic, and ontology-graph evidence at query time. We further design calibration-aware re-ranking using marginalization across synonyms. Prior studies on in-domain pretraining and robust normalization are also pertinent. Gururangan et al. (2020) examined domain-adaptive pretraining for scientific and biomedical text to improve representations in downstream linking tasks. Peng et al. (2019) released BioBERT with large-scale in-domain corpora for enhanced understanding and task-specific objectives for adaptation. Luo et al. (2019) investigated normalization robustness to synonyms and abbreviations in clinical notes with rule learning. Alsentzer et al. (2019) introduced ClinicalBERT by fine-tuning on hospital notes and discharge summaries for practical utility. Collectively, these studies motivate hybrid indexing that respects ontology structure without sacrificing recall. We cast disambiguation as energy minimization with node-type potentials. The model leverages learned synonymy edges and curated spelling variants. We derive uncertainty estimates to abstain on unsafe predictions in clinical workflows. Evaluation spans MIMIC, i2b2, and institution-specific test sets with physician review.

(2021) explored dense retrieval with bi-encoders trained on biomedical corpora.

text: Clinical entity linking maps mentions in narratives to standardized concepts in medical ontologies. Accurate linking supports downstream applications like cohort discovery and pharmacovigilance. Challenges arise from abbreviation ambiguity, domain shift, and limited supervision. We review methods spanning representation learning, candidate generation, and disambiguation. Leaman et al. (2013) optimized dictionary-based tagging with adaptive matching for biomedical mentions. Tutubalina et al. (2018) applied neural ranking over candidate concepts using contextual signals in practice. Ji et al. (2020) exploited ontology graph paths to bias disambiguation in end-to-end models. Sung et al. (2021) explored dense retrieval with bi-encoders trained on biomedical corpora.
