text: Credit risk modeling increasingly uses machine learning to assess default probabilities. Regulatory environments require transparency and fairness alongside accuracy. This work studies post-hoc and intrinsic explainability under distribution shift in lending portfolios. We provide a structured survey before presenting our approach. The literature on scorecard modeling and interpretable learning is extensive and fragmented. Hand et al. (2007) discussed classifier technology in credit scoring with pragmatic evaluation criteria. Rudin (2019) argued for interpretable models over black boxes in high-stakes decisions. Lundberg and Lee (2017) presented SHAP values as unified measures of feature contribution in practice today. Chen et al. (2020) studied monotonic gradient boosting to respect ordered financial covariates. These trajectories illustrate varied assumptions about monotonicity and causal sufficiency. Our method imposes monotone partial dependence while preserving calibration and stability. We integrate counterfactual constraints to limit explanation gaming. Benchmarking employs real application data with outcomes over multiple vintages. Bauernfeind et al. (2020) evaluated explanation stability across time for consumer lending portfolios with drift diagnostics under macroeconomic shifts. Kusner et al. (2017) formalized counterfactual fairness with constraints on latent causal variables and measurable implications for recourse. Barocas et al. (2019) surveyed fairness trade-offs and legal considerations in automated decision systems in regulated financial services. Sundararajan et al. (2017) introduced integrated gradients for attributions along paths in differentiable models and deployments. The synthesis motivates a principled recipe tying explanation faithfulness to compliance artifacts. We derive risk bounds connecting shapley-based attributions to constrained logistic utilities. The algorithm alternates convex updates with isotonic projections. We report ablation studies separating monotonicity, calibration, and counterfactual components. Results demonstrate improved area under ROC and tighter fairness margins across subgroups.

Chen et al. (2020) studied monotonic gradient boosting to respect ordered financial covariates.

text: Adaptive learning systems tailor content to learner proficiency and engagement signals. Designing such systems involves modeling knowledge, predicting performance, and allocating practice. We address the challenge of mixing short diagnostic sequences with longer mastery modules. The following review situates our contribution among prior approaches to adaptivity and assessment. Corbett and Anderson (1995) introduced knowledge tracing to model latent skill acquisition over time. Pavlik et al. (2009) developed spacing models that schedule practice across expanding intervals. Piech et al. (2015) applied recurrent networks to student response sequences for performance prediction online. Rafferty et al. (2016) studied POMDP formulations for tutoring actions in intelligent systems. These works collectively underscore data sparsity and nonstationary behavior in classrooms. Our method treats adaptation as constrained exploration with curricular guardrails. We embed concept graphs into a latent space aligned with cognitive difficulty. The policy leverages risk-sensitive objectives to curb forgetting. We evaluate the approach in a simulated tutor and two live courses. Research on student modeling and content sequencing offers further context for our setup. van de Sande (2013) analyzed proficiency estimation in Bayesian models with hierarchical priors for large-scale deployments in schools. Lindsey et al. (2014) examined retention outcomes when practice schedules adapt to error rates over semesters. Koedinger et al. (2015) investigated the assistance dilemma by contrasting instruction with discovery in classroom settings daily. Lopes et al. (2019) proposed bandit-based tutors that balance exploration with mastery progress tracking under content constraints and varying attention. The synthesis suggests a gap in coupling mastery tracking with exploration bonuses. We propose a dual-state learner model that separates fluency and recall. The algorithm optimizes a mixed objective over accuracy, latency, and coverage. Results show improvements in learning gains and reduced time-to-mastery with stable effort.

(2009) developed spacing models that schedule practice across expanding intervals. Piech et al. (2015) applied recurrent networks to student response sequences for performance prediction online. Rafferty et al. (2016) studied POMDP formulations for tutoring actions in intelligent systems.

text: Federated learning enables collaborative model training under data locality constraints while exposing new tradeoffs among privacy, communication, and utility. Prior work has clarified these tradeoffs through analyses of client heterogeneity, partial participation, and encryption mechanisms in realistic systems. McMahan et al. (2017) introduced federated averaging for training models across devices without centralizing raw data. Kairouz et al. (2021) surveyed systems and theory for privacy and communication efficiency in large-scale deployments. Bonawitz et al. (2019) described secure aggregation protocols, and Li et al. (2020) discussed personalization under heterogeneous client data and fairness considerations, tradeoffs. Despite these advances, practical deployments still struggle to balance update sparsification with robustness to client drift in nonstationary environments. We focus on establishing a principled objective that unifies personalization and secure aggregation through a decoupled proximal regularizer. Our contributions include a convergence analysis under bounded variance, a communication-adaptive optimizer, and an open-source simulator for dynamic client populations.

text: Explainability is central to clinical adoption of deep learning for imaging-based diagnosis and triage. Regulatory requirements and clinician trust motivate methods that provide faithful, calibrated rationales aligned with medical knowledge. Recent studies have investigated both gradient-based saliency and concept-based explanations, showing divergent behavior under distribution shift. Selvaraju et al. (2017) proposed Grad-CAM to highlight salient image regions for classification models in vision. Tjoa and Guan (2020) reviewed interpretability in medical imaging, Lundberg and Lee (2017) introduced SHAP for feature attributions, and Ghassemi et al. (2021) examined pitfalls in clinical machine learning and dataset shift risks in deployment contexts and governance concerns. Yet little work quantifies agreement between radiologists and algorithmic rationales at case-level granularity. We introduce a benchmark with lesion-level annotations and a protocol for evaluating causal faithfulness via counterfactual edits. Our analysis connects attribution stability with calibration and outlines reporting standards for safety-critical deployments.

(2021) examined pitfalls in clinical machine learning and dataset shift risks in deployment contexts and governance concerns.

text: Learning from user–item interaction graphs has become a dominant paradigm for modern recommender systems. Graph neural networks capture high-order connectivity and mitigate sparsity by propagating preferences along edges. Side information such as content and temporal context can regularize representations and improve cold-start performance. Several architectures operationalize these ideas with simplified propagation and loss-aware sampling for large catalogs. Ying et al. (2018) presented PinSage for web-scale item graph embedding using random walks and convolutions. He et al. (2020) developed LightGCN by simplifying propagation, Wu et al. (2019) analyzed graph convolution for recommendation, and Sun et al. (2020) introduced Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering with attention to scalability, cold-start users, and session dynamics in practice. Building on these insights, we design a session-aware message passing scheme with confidence-weighted neighbor selection. We report consistent gains on five benchmarks and release reproducible pipelines to facilitate evaluation under realistic sparsity.

(2020) introduced Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering with attention to scalability, cold-start users, and session dynamics in practice.

text: Automated program repair aims to reduce maintenance costs by generating patches that restore intended behavior. Recent neural approaches learn to propose edits from bug–fix pairs or from failing tests accompanied by execution traces. Large language models treating code as a first-class modality have expanded the search space while enabling natural-language guidance. Long et al. (2016) introduced DeepFix for C program error repair using sequence models trained on compiler feedback. Tufano et al. (2019) generated patches for Java methods with neural machine translation and code abstractions. Chen et al. (2021) released Codex for code generation tasks, and Xia et al. (2022) studied test-aware repair with execution feedback. We target realistic defect classes in multi-file projects and incorporate static analysis to enforce syntax and type invariants during decoding. Our evaluation quantifies repair correctness with semantic equivalence checks and measures generalization to unseen projects.

(2022) studied test-aware repair with execution feedback.

text: Recommender systems increasingly shape access to information and opportunity. Concerns over fairness and accountability have motivated a surge of research on algorithmic harms. Prior work defines different fairness notions for users, items, and providers. Our study examines provider exposure under ranking constraints in marketplaces. We situate our approach within research on exposure-aware learning to rank. Early methods adjusted scoring functions to equalize outcomes across groups. Subsequent models introduced constraints to control disparities in attention and clicks. Offline evaluation frameworks have attempted to quantify long term effects of interventions. Field deployments report practical challenges in balancing profit and equity. Zhang et al. (2019) study exposure disparities in ranking. Burke (2017) surveys multi-stakeholder fairness. Mehrotra and Celis (2020) optimize listings for supplier equity. Ekstrand et al. (2018) evaluate user impacts in live systems. These findings are reported across domains without clarifying methodological compatibility or shared assumptions. Comparative relevance to recommendation diversity is not articulated or measured. Despite broad interest, there is limited consensus on appropriate baselines for fair exposure. Position bias and inventory constraints interact with group definitions in subtle ways. Our work connects constrained optimization with interpretable exposure targets. We formalize provider goals as linear constraints over stochastic rankings. We derive bounds on welfare loss under different constraint choices. We present a calibration procedure that aligns training proxies with exposure outcomes. The method supports dynamic marketplaces with volatile supply and demand. We evaluate on two public datasets and one proprietary catalog. Metrics include provider Gini, minimum share, and disparity ratios. We compare against unconstrained learning to rank and reweighting baselines. We also test a post hoc reordering strategy to assess tradeoffs. Sensitivity analysis examines robustness under misspecified group labels. An ablation isolates the contribution of the calibration step. We release code and a reproducibility checklist to support future work. The findings inform policy discussions about equitable ranking in platforms.

Comparative relevance to recommendation diversity is not articulated or measured.

Comparative relevance to recommendation diversity is not articulated or measured.

text: Text summarization research has shifted from extractive to abstractive techniques over the last decade. As models become more fluent, factual consistency has emerged as a critical failure mode. Evaluating factuality requires both datasets and metrics that capture semantic correctness. Our work proposes a contrastive factuality evaluation using counterfactual perturbations. We focus on news and scientific abstracts to study domain effects. Early evaluation relied on n-gram overlap measures that reward surface similarity. Semantic metrics based on entailment attempt to capture faithfulness beyond token overlap. Recent datasets include human annotations of factual errors and hallucinations. Despite progress, correlations with human judgments are still inconsistent across settings. We aim to bridge metric design and model training through calibrated rewards. We formalize factuality as entailment under document-grounded hypotheses. We also introduce a procedure for generating minimally edited revisions for evaluation. See et al. (2017) introduce pointer-generator summaries. Kryscinski et al. (2019) examine factual errors in neural outputs. Maynez et al. (2020) report weak correlation between factuality and ROUGE. Fabbri et al. (2021) build a multidimensional evaluation benchmark. Links among task definitions and metrics are left unspecified for comparative interpretation. Contextual scope across datasets remains largely implicit. Our method couples an entailment verifier with an uncertainty-aware aggregation rule. The verifier operates on sentence-level claims extracted from summaries. The aggregation rule mitigates overconfident false positives by calibrating scores. We compare against leading automatic metrics using released code and data. Human evaluation uses three-way labels for supported, contradicted, or unverifiable claims. We test transfer across domains with controlled sampling of entities. Robustness analysis examines sensitivity to document length and entity rarity. Training details include model sizes, learning schedules, and inference settings. We report bootstrap intervals for all metric correlations with annotators. We release annotations and task scripts for transparent replication. Ethical considerations address annotator consent and potential misuse of automated scoring. The results indicate consistent gains in aligning metric scores with human judgments.

text: Molecular property prediction underpins virtual screening and materials discovery. Graph neural networks have become a dominant approach for learning from molecular graphs. Choosing architectures and data splits strongly affects reported accuracy. We investigate inductive biases that support generalization across chemical families. Our framework decomposes message passing into aggregation, update, and readout choices. We analyze the interplay between edge features and long-range interactions. Prior studies disagree on the benefits of attention mechanisms in chemistry. The role of pretraining on unlabeled molecules is still unsettled. We revisit evaluation protocols to reduce overestimation from scaffold leakage. We also examine the tradeoff between model size and calibration. We situate our study among foundational contributions on graph learning for molecules. Duvenaud et al. (2015) propose convolutional fingerprints for molecules. Kearnes et al. (2016) design graph convnets tailored to chemistry. Gilmer et al. (2017) unify message passing formulations. Yang et al. (2019) release large-scale benchmarks. The relation among architectural priors, dataset biases, and evaluation splits is left unstated for cross-study synthesis. Comparability of outcomes remains largely ambiguous. Our experiments consider datasets that stress out-of-distribution generalization. We measure performance using ROC-AUC, RMSE, and calibration error. Hyperparameter searches use fixed budgets to ensure fair comparison. We include strong baselines such as random forests on fingerprints. Interpretability is assessed with gradient attributions and counterfactual edits. We report detailed ablations over aggregation and readout choices. We probe the impact of explicit 3D information on conformationally sensitive tasks. Uncertainty estimates are used to filter low-confidence predictions. We study the costs of extra message passing steps on runtime. We release reusable split generators and scripts for benchmarking. Reproducibility is supported with environment snapshots and seed control. Ethical statements discuss responsible use in drug discovery and safety screening. The findings guide principled selections of architectures for molecular prediction.

(2017) unify message passing formulations. Yang et al. (2019) release large-scale benchmarks. The relation among architectural priors, dataset biases, and evaluation splits is left unstated for cross-study synthesis. Comparability of outcomes remains largely ambiguous.

text: Multilingual machine translation aims to share capacity across languages for efficiency and transfer. Large-scale pretraining has amplified interest in unified models for many languages. Yet design choices interact with data imbalance in complex ways. We analyze how routing and scaling decisions affect transfer for low-resource pairs. Our approach examines capacity allocation under fixed compute budgets. We evaluate zero-shot and many-to-one settings with consistent tokenization. The study connects architectural choices with language clustering structures. We also quantify negative interference between distant language families. Our experiments span typologically diverse languages with varying scripts. Training uses temperature-based sampling to modulate exposure to small corpora. We situate the work within prior advances in multilingual translation. Johnson et al. (2017) demonstrate zero-shot translation in a shared model. Arivazhagan et al. (2019) explore language clustering for transfer. Fan et al. (2020) scale multilingual pretraining. Wu and Zhao (2021) investigate tokenization impacts. The narrative across capacity, data balance, and routing strategies is not connected to a unified explanatory account. Comparative implications remain largely undiscussed. Our contribution formalizes capacity routing as a constrained allocation problem. We propose a simple routing prior based on language similarity and corpus size. The prior regularizes expert selection in a sparse mixture-of-experts encoder. We derive conditions under which transfer is guaranteed to improve recall. We present an analysis of sampling temperatures that balances coverage and stability. Evaluation includes FLORES and WMT test sets with BLEU and chrF. Low-resource gains are compared against monolingual and bilingual baselines. We probe tokenization sensitivity by varying subword vocabulary sizes. Ablations remove the routing prior to isolate its effect. We conduct error analysis on morphology and named entities. We report costs measured in training tokens and wall-clock hours. Release materials include code, data recipes, and evaluation scripts. The study offers practical guidance for scalable multilingual translation systems.

(2020) scale multilingual pretraining. Wu and Zhao (2021) investigate tokenization impacts. The narrative across capacity, data balance, and routing strategies is not connected to a unified explanatory account. Comparative implications remain largely undiscussed.

text: Robust reinforcement learning seeks policies that perform under distribution shift. Many benchmarks show high variance when tested outside training conditions. We investigate methods that improve generalization across procedurally generated environments. Our approach integrates data augmentation with risk-sensitive objectives. We measure robustness using held-out levels and perturbed dynamics. Prior work has explored diverse mechanisms for tackling overfitting in RL. Differences in environment design complicate cross-paper conclusions. We formalize robustness as worst-case value over a parametric family of MDPs. The formulation enables principled comparisons of objectives and augmentations. We further analyze the role of observation stochasticity in training. We describe related lines of research spanning evaluation and algorithm design. Zhang et al. (2018) investigate generalization gaps from training seeds. Cobbe et al. (2019) propose Procgen to test robustness. Packer et al. (2018) vary environments to evaluate overfitting. Igl et al. (2019) study Bayesian approaches for uncertainty. The interplay among distribution shifts, randomized levels, and posterior modeling is left unspecified for actionable comparison. Practical links remain. Our contribution is a unified framework that couples risk measures with augmentation. We introduce a controllable shift generator for dynamics and visuals. The generator parameterizes families of tasks with calibrated difficulty. A robust objective encourages solutions that hedge against adverse variations. We study policy regularizers and value constraints derived from the generator. The experiments cover gridworlds and continuous control tasks. We compare against domain randomization and adversarial training baselines. Sample complexity and final performance are reported with confidence intervals. We test transfer to environments unseen during algorithm design. An ablation quantifies the effect of each augmentation component. We analyze failure modes including texture bias and action saturation. We release code and level seeds for reproducibility. The results provide guidance for principled robustness evaluation in RL.

(2018) vary environments to evaluate overfitting. Igl et al. (2019) study Bayesian approaches for uncertainty. The interplay among distribution shifts, randomized levels, and posterior modeling is left unspecified for actionable comparison. Practical links remain.

text: Graph-based recommendation has advanced by leveraging high-order connectivity to better capture user preferences in sparse data. This work examines the robustness of graph neural models under distribution shift in real-time platforms. Zhang et al. (2019) introduced a session-based recommender using gated graph propagation with improved short-term accuracy. He and McAuley (2020) explored contrastive learning for implicit feedback by maximizing agreement between augmented user–item views. Sun et al. (2021) presented multi-behavior modeling with disentangled representations for sequential recommendation. Chen et al. (2022) studied cross-domain adaptation with meta-learning for cold-start. Despite progress, evaluation under dynamic exposure bias and delayed feedback remains underexplored. We present a streaming training and calibration framework that unifies counterfactual estimation with stability-aware message passing.

text: Explainability in NLP is essential for accountable deployment of neural systems in regulated domains. Prior approaches vary in their assumptions about access to model internals and the desired granularity of explanations. Ribeiro et al. (2016) proposed model-agnostic explanations through local perturbations of inputs. Lundberg and Lee (2017) formulated SHAP values to unify additive feature attribution methods. Jain and Wallace (2019) questioned attention as explanation with empirical evidence across tasks. Pruthi et al. (2020) studied input gradient regularization to align saliency maps with human intuition in text classification models. Yet few studies examine temporal stability of explanations under evolving data distributions. We investigate shift-aware explanation protocols that maintain fidelity while supporting decision auditing.

text: Federated learning aims to collaboratively train machine learning models without centralizing raw user data. In practice, non-iid data, resource heterogeneity, and privacy constraints complicate both optimization and evaluation. McMahan et al. (2017) introduced federated averaging to train shared models from decentralized data. Li et al. (2020) proposed FedProx to mitigate client drift by modifying the local objective. Arivazhagan et al. (2019) examined personalization layers to adapt federated models to heterogeneous users. Kairouz et al. (2021) surveyed systems and privacy directions across cross-silo and cross-device settings. Despite advances, guarantees for fairness and robustness under adversarial participation remain limited. We present a benchmarked framework for adaptive aggregation and auditing under constrained communication budgets.

text: Multimodal learning for radiology seeks to ground textual reasoning in medical images to aid clinical decision support. Recent datasets and representation techniques have enabled joint modeling of findings, impressions, and visual evidence at scale. Rajpurkar et al. (2017) released ChestX-ray14 with labels mined from reports to benchmark thoracic disease detection. Johnson et al. (2019) introduced MIMIC-CXR, providing large-scale paired images and free-text radiology narratives. Tiu et al. (2022) leveraged contrastive alignment between images and reports for retrieval and zero-shot classification. Zhou et al. (2021) proposed cross-modal attention pretraining for report generation. Yet consistency between generated rationales and clinically actionable labels is not well characterized. We study calibration-aware retrieval and reporting that align concepts across modalities while preserving uncertainty estimates.

text: Neural program synthesis aims to produce correct executable code from natural language specifications and examples. Despite impressive progress with large language models, grounding and verifiability remain open problems in practical settings. Reed and De Freitas (2016) introduced neural programmer-interpreters to learn end-to-end differentiable machines from traces. Yin and Neubig (2017) modeled code generation with a syntax-aware decoder that respects production rules. Chen et al. (2021) demonstrated large-scale pretraining for code completion and synthesis across multiple programming languages. Le et al. (2022) explored test-time execution feedback to steer decoding. Current approaches rarely quantify failure modes when specifications are ambiguous or underdetermined. We propose verifiable synthesis via counterexample-guided decoding with lightweight static analysis.

text: Recommender systems increasingly leverage graph structure to model interactions between users and items. Graph neural networks provide a principled framework for learning over such relational data in large marketplaces. Despite rapid progress, open questions remain about balancing expressivity, scalability, and debiasing effects. We study representation learning objectives that preserve neighborhood semantics while controlling popularity skew. Prior research has advanced models that propagate signals across user item graphs with varying complexity. Early approaches emphasized deep stacks of nonlinear transformations to capture high-order connectivity patterns. Recent models prioritize lightweight propagation while retaining collaborative signals in sparse settings. He et al. (2020) introduced LightGCN for collaborative filtering with simplified propagation and showed improvements on standard benchmarks. Wu et al. (2019) presented a comprehensive survey of graph neural networks and summarized message passing architectures relevant to recommendation. Sun et al. (2020) proposed Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering to model user item interactions with attentive aggregation using implicit feedback signals. Ding et al. (2018) examined session-based recommendation with graph embeddings and reported gains in item ranking tasks. These developments collectively motivate designs that trade parameter counts for propagation depth in implicit feedback regimes. Beyond modeling, evaluation protocols have shifted toward temporal splits that prevent leakage across training and test. We adopt chronological splits and report metrics that reflect ranking quality under strict exposure constraints. Exposure bias and confounding popularity are mitigated by inverse propensity scoring and controlled negative sampling. Our method integrates calibrated propagation with sample reweighting to disentangle preference from exposure. We contribute a scalable training pipeline that handles billion edge graphs with memory efficient mini-batching. The pipeline coordinates neighbor sampling with cached item representations to stabilize stochastic gradients. Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks demonstrate consistent gains under both offline and semi-online metrics. We release code and reproducible configurations to facilitate transparent comparison and future work.

text: Data augmentation is widely used to improve generalization of neural text classifiers. Recent work explores both rule-based and model-based techniques for generating diverse examples. Despite gains, augmented data can distort label semantics if perturbations are poorly controlled. We investigate selection policies that prioritize label-preserving edits measured by calibration signals. Our framework unifies token-level and sentence-level transformations under a probabilistic budget. The method supports conditional generation from large language models while constraining divergence from the source. We relate our design to established augmentation lines in NLP. Prior studies demonstrate benefits across tasks and scales. Wei and Zou (2019) proposed easy data augmentation operations such as synonym replacement and random insertion for text classification. Kobayashi (2018) introduced contextual augmentation that replaces words using probabilities from a language model to maintain semantics. Xie et al. (2020) presented Unsupervised Data Augmentation leveraging consistency training with unlabeled examples to regularize classifiers. Sennrich et al. (2016) explored back translation for generating synthetic text that expands training corpora in low resource settings. EDA remains competitive today. These approaches inform our strategy for combining lexical edits with consistency regularization under a single scheduler. We operationalize label preservation using a classifier-free guidance score estimated from held-out logits. To avoid distribution shift, we sample candidate outputs within a bounded perplexity neighborhood of the input. We further introduce an acceptance test that filters edits violating entailment constraints relative to the source sentence. The training loop interleaves original and augmented batches according to a fixed curriculum. Experiments on five text classification datasets show reduced overfitting and improved calibration. We release code and data to foster reproducibility.

text: Clinical decision support systems must justify predictions to be adopted in safety-critical environments. Explanations help users probe model behavior and mitigate risks from dataset shift and spurious correlations. In healthcare, interpretability is also mandated by regulation and institutional review processes. We examine attribution methods and inherently interpretable models for bedside risk estimation. Our study focuses on calibration, stability, and human factors in explanation design. Ribeiro et al. (2016) introduced LIME to approximate local decision boundaries with sparse linear explanations around individual predictions. Lundberg and Lee (2017) unified additive feature attribution methods under the SHAP framework based on Shapley values. Caruana et al. (2015) demonstrated that interpretable generalized additive models with pairwise interactions can match black box accuracy on medical risk prediction. Tonekaboni et al. (2019) studied clinician trust in machine learning explanations during decision support in critical care. Trust remains. These lines of work highlight complementary strategies for aligning model reasoning with clinical expectations. We extend this literature by benchmarking explanation stability across shifts induced by hospital-specific coding practices. We introduce a protocol that perturbs inputs along clinically meaningful axes to test monotonic response. The approach incorporates domain constraints into an additive surrogate that enforces safety properties. We compare post hoc methods with constrained models under identical preprocessing and evaluation metrics. Annotated rationales from experts are used to assess faithfulness and cognitive load during simulated triage. We report accuracy, calibration error, and counterfactual sensitivity across three intensive care cohorts. Our human study measures how explanations affect decision latency and agreement with gold standards. Results show that stability-aware explanations can reduce overreliance on unreliable features while preserving utility. The findings yield design recommendations for integrating interpretable models into clinical workflows.

(2019) studied clinician trust in machine learning explanations during decision support in critical care. Trust remains.

text: Neural language models are vulnerable to small perturbations that cause incorrect predictions. This vulnerability poses risks for safety-critical deployments and evaluation integrity. Adversarial training has emerged as a defense that improves local smoothness around training examples. In NLP, constructing discrete attacks and defenses requires bridging continuous optimization and symbolic constraints. We analyze training objectives that align perturbations with semantics and syntactic validity. Our approach decouples perturbation generation from parameter updates through a bi-level formulation. The surrogate loss penalizes inconsistency under paraphrastic and character-level variations. We connect our method to prior adversarial studies in text. We contextualize both embedding-space and token-space strategies within a unified framework. We review relevant attack and defense techniques from the literature. Miyato et al. (2017) applied virtual adversarial training to text classifiers by adding perturbations in embedding space. Jia and Liang (2017) showed that reading comprehension systems can be fooled by distracting sentences appended to paragraphs. Ebrahimi et al. (2018) introduced HotFlip, a character-level attack that uses gradient signals to propose discrete edits. Alzantot et al. (2018) designed a population-based method for crafting semantically similar adversarial examples against neural classifiers. Robustness remains a moving target in NLP. These findings motivate evaluations that stress both lexical invariance and sentence meaning preservation. We incorporate certified smoothing for token substitutions using randomized embeddings. The training procedure alternates between constrained search and robust optimization with early stopping. Experiments show improved worst-case accuracy under diverse attacks on sentiment and NLI tasks. We provide an open-source toolkit for reproducible robust training.

text: Federated learning enables training models using decentralized data while preserving user privacy. On-device prediction for keyboards and input methods benefits from this paradigm due to sensitive content. Real-world deployments must balance accuracy, communication cost, and differential privacy guarantees. We target next-word prediction and emoji suggestion on mobile devices with intermittent connectivity. Our approach emphasizes personalization while respecting federation constraints on compute and bandwidth. We present a method that blends global and local objectives through gradient interpolation. The algorithm uses lightweight adapters to capture user-specific preferences without overfitting. We relate our contributions to prior advances in federated optimization and personalization. Communication efficiency is addressed with structured sparsification and prioritized client sampling. Privacy is preserved with calibrated noise addition and secure aggregation across rounds. We briefly review foundational work and recent developments in the field. McMahan et al. (2017) introduced federated averaging to train models across devices without centralizing raw data. Kairouz et al. (2021) summarized challenges in communication efficiency, personalization, and privacy for large scale federated systems. Li et al. (2020) proposed FedProx to stabilize training under client heterogeneity by modifying the objective with a proximal term. Reddi et al. (2021) developed adaptive federated optimization methods that incorporate server momentum and variance reduction. Personalization strategies remain an active area today. These insights inform our design of adapters that fine-tune predictions to user contexts with minimal drift. We evaluate on large-scale keyboard datasets collected under explicit opt-in policies. Results show improved top-k accuracy and reduced training variance across heterogeneous client populations. We provide ablations that isolate the effects of adapters, sampling policies, and privacy budgets.

(2021) developed adaptive federated optimization methods that incorporate server momentum and variance reduction. Personalization strategies remain an active area today.

text: Research on question answering over semi-structured tables has expanded with advances in neural modeling. The task requires mapping natural language questions to executable programs or evidence chains. Our work examines alignment signals that guide intermediate reasoning over heterogeneous schemas. We focus on generalization across tables unseen during training. This section reviews representative approaches in semantic parsing and retrieval for tabular QA. Early systems emphasized grammar induction with limited schema coverage. Neural methods later adopted encoder decoders with schema linking and copy operations. Pretraining on table and text corpora has become a common strategy. Weak supervision from denotations reduces annotation costs while complicating search. Benchmarks vary widely in complexity, schema size, and execution semantics. We organize related work by modeling paradigm and supervision type. Chen et al. (2020) introduced a neural semantic parser for table questions using latent programs and weak supervision. Wang and Pasupat (2018) studied compositional generalization on synthetic tables with a focus on systematicity in query patterns. Li et al. (2019) presented a retrieval model for table lookup that ranks cell evidence rather than generating logical forms. Zhong et al. (2017) released WikiSQL and trained seq2SQL models with reinforcement learning across a broad set of schemas. Dataset coverage remained an issue. Subsequent efforts combined symbolic executors with neural controllers to handle compositional queries. Pretrained language models were adapted with schema serialization and value linking. Execution guided decoding has been explored to prune spurious programs. Contrastive objectives align question spans with candidate columns and cells. Evidence aggregation frameworks score multi-cell rationales over rows and columns. Cross-domain transfer remains limited by annotation bias and dataset artifacts. We propose a unified encoder that fuses schema graphs with question intents. Our approach introduces curriculum signals derived from execution traces. We evaluate across multiple benchmarks with consistent training protocols. The results demonstrate improved robustness under distribution shifts.

(2017) released WikiSQL and trained seq2SQL models with reinforcement learning across a broad set of schemas. Dataset coverage remained an issue.

text: Sentiment analysis for low-resource languages faces data scarcity and domain heterogeneity. Pretrained multilingual encoders have improved zero-shot transfer but still degrade on colloquial text. We study sentence-level polarity classification under cross-lingual constraints with limited labeled examples. Our goal is to measure robustness to spelling variation, code-switching, and topic drift. This section surveys resources, pretraining strategies, and transfer methods relevant to our setup. Early approaches relied on translation and lexicon projection from high-resource languages. Subsequent work used adversarial training to align latent spaces across languages. Task-adaptive fine-tuning has shown benefits when target data distributions are small. Multi-task training with emotion and subjectivity can help regularize features. Evaluation protocols vary widely across corpora and domains. We highlight studies that shaped current cross-lingual sentiment modeling. Barnes et al. (2018) compiled a multilingual sentiment corpus with consistent annotation and evaluated transfer learning baselines. Ruder et al. (2019) introduced cross-lingual language model pretraining using shared subword vocabularies across dozens of languages. Ponti et al. (2020) analyzed typological factors that affect zero-shot transfer in sentiment and other sequence classification tasks. Loureiro et al. (2022) released a lexical resource linking emotion ratings across languages through cross-lingual embeddings. Domain shifts hinder consistent polarity mapping. Label noise severely complicates cross-lingual alignment. These efforts inform the choice of objectives and data curation in our experiments. We adopt constrained decoding to stabilize predictions under label imbalance. Auxiliary language identification reduces errors on mixed-language posts. Augmentation with back-translated snippets increases coverage of slang variants. Calibration with temperature scaling improves probability estimates for thresholding. We standardize tokenization to avoid over-segmentation in morphologically rich languages. Our analysis separates annotation artifacts from genuine transfer phenomena. The proposed pipeline emphasizes reproducibility and fair comparison across languages. Results demonstrate gains on low-resource settings without sacrificing performance on source data.

(2020) analyzed typological factors that affect zero-shot transfer in sentiment and other sequence classification tasks. Loureiro et al. (2022) released a lexical resource linking emotion ratings across languages through cross-lingual embeddings. Domain shifts hinder consistent polarity mapping. text: Privacy constraints restrict central collection of patient data for predictive modeling. Federated learning offers a framework for collaborative training across institutions. Clinical data are heterogeneous in coding, demographics, and device characteristics. We investigate robust optimization under participation variability and non-IID distributions. This section reviews methods relevant to optimization, privacy, and communication. Classical distributed training assumes reliable clusters and synchronized updates. Healthcare networks violate these assumptions due to policy and infrastructure barriers. Model personalization is often necessary to handle population differences. Auditable training pipelines are essential for regulatory compliance. McMahan et al. (2017) developed federated averaging to train models across clients without centralizing raw data. Kairouz et al. (2021) surveyed advances and open problems in federated optimization, privacy, and systems deployment. Li et al. (2020) proposed FedProx to address heterogeneity by modifying the local objective with a proximal term. Geyer et al. (2017) integrated differential privacy into client updates for user-level protection. Communication costs and stragglers limit scalability in clinical networks with intermittent participation. Label shift further degrades aggregation. These considerations motivate algorithms that adapt to shifting client sets and variable data quality. We incorporate adaptive weighting informed by validation residuals. Our approach separates representation sharing from classifier calibration. We employ secure aggregation to reduce the risk of gradient leakage. Client sampling is scheduled to balance fairness and throughput. We bound update norms to stabilize training under rare events. We evaluate on multi-institutional ICU and oncology cohorts. Metrics include AUROC, calibration error, and decision-curve utility. Ablations quantify the impact of participation rates and personalization strength. We release a reproducible framework with synthetic data for benchmarking. Results show consistent improvements under severe heterogeneity and participation churn.

(2017) integrated differential privacy into client updates for user-level protection. Communication costs and stragglers limit scalability in clinical networks with intermittent participation. text: Learning to repair software bugs has benefited from advances in neural sequence modeling. Generalization across projects remains difficult due to style and library variation. We study patch generation conditioned on failing tests and static analysis signals. The objective is to improve correctness while preserving developer intent. This section overviews key datasets, architectures, and decoding strategies. Early work framed repair as translation from buggy to fixed code. Hybrid methods integrate program analysis to enforce constraints. Recent approaches leverage pretrained code models with structural encoders. Tufano et al. (2019) used sequence-to-sequence models to learn fixes for small Java methods from large commit histories. Gupta et al. (2017) explored deep code search for bug localization using joint embeddings of queries and code. Chen et al. (2019) introduced SequenceR to predict patches by conditioning on buggy context and abstracted tokens. Jiang et al. (2021) investigated copy mechanisms and constrained decoding to improve patch validity. Realistic build systems and tests introduce brittle behaviors during evaluation. Context windows matter. These studies highlight the need for syntax awareness and semantic preservation. We design a graph-augmented encoder that captures control and data dependence. Patch candidates are validated with test-execution and lightweight type checks. Constraint templates reduce the search space for mutations and insertions. Edit representations are trained with curriculum over increasing context size. We sample negative patches to harden discriminators against spurious fixes. Training incorporates bug-bug contrast to refine locality cues. We evaluate on Defects4J and additional large-scale repositories. Metrics include pass rate, plausibility rate, and exact match. Ablations assess the effect of analysis features and decoding constraints. Reproducibility is ensured with version-pinned toolchains and seeds. Our results show higher correct-fix rates with fewer invalid patches.

(2021) investigated copy mechanisms and constrained decoding to improve patch validity. Realistic build systems and tests introduce brittle behaviors during evaluation. Context windows matter.

text: Safety concerns remain central to the deployment of open-domain dialogue agents. Unchecked generation can amplify toxic content and misleading claims. Our study focuses on safety shaping with controllable generation and retrieval conditioning. We target realistic prompts spanning sensitive topics and adversarial intent. This section reviews safety datasets, control methods, and evaluation strategies. Early work relied on blacklists and simple filtering with limited coverage. Recent approaches add classifiers that rerank or block unsafe continuations. Conditional generation uses control tokens, prompts, and attributes to steer outputs. Retrieval augmentation offers grounded context that can constrain model behavior. Evaluation protocols must measure both safety and conversational quality. Gehman et al. (2020) created RealToxicityPrompts to probe toxicity in language models using curated prompts and continuation scoring. Welbl et al. (2021) examined retrieval augmented generation for factuality with analysis of knowledge conflicts. Keskar et al. (2019) trained CTRL with control codes to steer style and content across diverse domains. Dinan et al. (2021) studied adversarial safety in open-domain chat with human-in-the-loop data collection. Prompt filtering and constrained decoding remain unstable across topics, intents, and user behaviors. Coverage gaps persist. These threads indicate challenges in achieving reliable safety while preserving expressivity. We propose a modular controller that coordinates safety filters and retrieval signals. The controller estimates risk and allocates decoding budgets accordingly. A lightweight safety plan is predicted and enforced during generation. We design detectors for sensitive entities and claims based on weak supervision. Human preference data help calibrate penalties for overblocking. We include adversarial training with paraphrased unsafe prompts. Our evaluation uses automatic and human judgments across open and safety-focused datasets. Ablations assess the impact of each control and retrieval component. Results show improved safety with limited loss of dialogue quality.

text: Cross-lingual summarization aims to produce concise outputs in a target language while preserving key information from a source language. Recent advances in multilingual pretraining have improved content selection and fluency, yet alignment between languages remains challenging. Early research relied on pipeline machine translation followed by monolingual summarization, which often propagated errors and duplicated content. Joint models attempt to balance translation faithfulness with salience, but objective design still lacks clear grounding in user goals. Prior surveys emphasize the need for consistent evaluation protocols to compare progress across domains and languages. We review work on modeling choices that affect cross-lingual faithfulness, compression, and discourse structure. For content selection, constraints on entity consistency and event framing have proven important in news and scientific text. Li et al. (2019) proposed pointer-generator adaptations for cross-lingual summarization. Tang and Hu (2020) explored reconstruction losses to preserve content fidelity in transfer settings. Xiao et al. (2021) introduced multilingual pretraining with sentence-level alignment for headline generation. Chen et al. (2022) examined coverage penalties in low-resource news datasets. Rossi and Patel (2021) studied sentence compression heuristics under cross-domain drift in multilingual corpora. Training signals were adjusted by proxy metrics. Despite these advances, few studies isolate the causal impact of alignment objectives on factual consistency. Our study introduces a controlled evaluation suite that disentangles transfer, compression, and grounding with matched datasets. We also present an analysis of error types that links lexical choice to alignment drift and offers actionable guidance for future models.

text: Urban traffic forecasting supports routing, congestion management, and safety-critical planning in modern cities. Spatiotemporal dependencies arise from road topology, signal timing, and human behavior, which complicate predictive modeling. Traditional time series methods capture local patterns but ignore graph structure and exogenous factors. Graph neural networks offer a unified view that models spatial edges and temporal dynamics jointly. Recent work explores how to encode directionality, adapt adjacency, and integrate external signals for robust predictions. Benchmarks typically report metrics under multiple horizons to reflect operational needs. We summarize architectural choices that influence generalization across cities with heterogeneous sensors. Zhang et al. (2018) modeled traffic flow using diffusion convolution on directed graphs. Yu and Li (2018) applied spatiotemporal GCNs with gated temporal units for citywide prediction. Bai et al. (2020) investigated adaptive adjacency matrices learned end-to-end for irregular sensors. Guo et al. (2019) designed attention mechanisms to capture dynamic spatial dependencies. Wang et al. (2021) studied residual dilation for long horizons. External features included weather and holiday indicators. Despite strong performance, many models underperform when sensor layouts change or nodes drop unexpectedly. Our approach focuses on structure-invariant operators that retain accuracy when topology is partially observed. We also release ablations that clarify which components most improve transfer to unseen networks.

Wang et al. (2021) studied residual dilation for long horizons. External features included weather and holiday indicators.

text: Clinical decision support systems increasingly leverage machine learning to assist clinicians with risk stratification and treatment planning. Recent large language models enable flexible reasoning over free-text notes but require careful grounding in clinical evidence. Prior work has emphasized structured EHR features, yet narrative documentation contains key signals about comorbidities and care processes. Integrating text and tabular data can improve recall while maintaining calibration required for bedside use. Nevertheless, methodological rigor and transparency remain uneven across datasets and tasks. The literature highlights data provenance, label quality, and temporal drift as central concerns for deployment. We review resources and benchmarks that shape evaluation practices in clinical prediction and explanation. Johnson et al. (2016) curated MIMIC-III for reproducible intensive care research. Harutyunyan et al. (2019) established benchmarks for mortality and length of stay prediction. Suresh et al. (2018) discussed phenotyping approaches with temporal features. Lehman et al. (2021) evaluated label quality and coding practices in clinical text corpora. McDermott et al. (2021) assessed fairness metrics in health prediction models. Guidelines addressed provenance, leakage, and deidentification among shared datasets. Practices. These works motivate standardized protocols for splitting patient timelines and auditing feature leakage. Building on these insights, our framework couples retrieval-augmented prompting with temporally aligned supervision. We evaluate on intensive care outcomes and provide qualitative analyses of generated rationales to support interpretability.

Suresh et al. (2018) discussed phenotyping approaches with temporal features. Lehman et al. (2021) evaluated label quality and coding practices in clinical text corpora. McDermott et al. (2021) assessed fairness metrics in health prediction models. Guidelines addressed provenance, leakage, and deidentification among shared datasets. Practices.

text: Vision-and-language navigation studies how agents follow natural language to move through photo-realistic 3D environments. This task stresses grounded language understanding, memory, and exploration under partial observability. Early models relied on sequence-to-sequence mappings between instructions and action sequences without scene context. Later systems integrated panoramic perception, depth cues, and pretrained language encoders to improve generalization. Data augmentation and curriculum design have been used to mitigate overfitting to training houses. Evaluation commonly reports success rate, path length, and instruction fidelity, though human studies remain limited. We summarize representative datasets and modeling choices shaping progress on indoor navigation. Anderson et al. (2018) introduced the Room-to-Room benchmark for grounding instructions in 3D environments. Wang et al. (2019) proposed reinforcement learning with speaker-follower models to improve exploration. Ma et al. (2019) incorporated cross-modal attention for trajectory planning. Hong et al. (2020) studied environmental dropouts to reduce overfitting to seen houses. Ku et al. (2020) examined instruction paraphrases with data augmentation. Metrics emphasized success rate and navigation error under constraints. Despite progress, robustness to paraphrase and environmental shifts remains a major open challenge. Our method introduces uncertainty-aware grounding that reconciles local visual cues with global route plans. We demonstrate gains on multiple splits and analyze failure cases to inform future benchmarks.

(2020) studied environmental dropouts to reduce overfitting to seen houses. Ku et al. (2020) examined instruction paraphrases with data augmentation. Metrics emphasized success rate and navigation error under constraints.

text: Automatic program repair aims to generate patches that fix software bugs while preserving intended behavior. Search-based methods and learning-based approaches offer complementary strategies for exploring large patch spaces. Early systems focused on test-suite adequacy, whereas recent work targets semantic correctness and readability. Datasets and oracles play crucial roles in evaluating patch plausibility and overfitting risk. Program representation choices influence generalization across projects and programming languages. We review algorithmic patterns that underlie patch generation and ranking across paradigms. Our survey begins with classical search and then considers neural models trained from code histories. Le Goues et al. (2012) introduced GenProg to search patch spaces with genetic operators. Kim et al. (2013) designed PAR using fix patterns mined from human patches. Martinez and Monperrus (2016) developed ASTOR to explore program transformations across templates. Long and Rinard (2016) proposed Prophet to rank candidate patches with probabilistic models. Tufano et al. (2019) studied neural sequence models that learn to fix single-line bugs. Patch generalization lagged. Despite diverse techniques, common failure modes include missing context, brittle templates, and low-quality test suites. We propose a hybrid framework that learns repair operators while enforcing equivalence constraints with symbolic checks. Empirical results on multiple benchmarks show improvements in correctness and maintainability relative to established baselines.

Martinez and Monperrus (2016) developed ASTOR to explore program transformations across templates. Long and Rinard (2016) proposed Prophet to rank candidate patches with probabilistic models. Tufano et al. (2019) studied neural sequence models that learn to fix single-line bugs. Patch generalization lagged.

text: Clinical decision support systems increasingly rely on explainable machine learning to gain clinician trust. Prior research has examined feature attribution, counterfactual reasoning, and prototype learning as complementary paradigms for transparency. Recent surveys detail domain constraints in healthcare, including safety, accountability, and interpretability norms imposed by regulation. Ribeiro et al. (2016) introduced model-agnostic explanations for complex classifiers. Lundberg and Lee (2017) proposed additive attributions based on cooperative game theory for local interpretability. Holzinger et al. (2019) surveyed human-centered AI practices in medicine alongside Lipton (2018) on the taxonomy of interpretability claims. Doshi-Velez and Kim (2017) discussed rigorous evaluation frameworks for explanations in sensitive applications. Caruana et al. (2015) studied intelligible models. Despite these advances, clinical tasks impose data shift, missingness, and class imbalance that entangle explainer reliability. A separate thread investigates faithfulness metrics that compare explanation stability under input perturbations. Work on causal surrogates argues for interpretable models that are inherently constrained during training. Guidotti et al. (2018) reviewed post-hoc explanation methods across black-box settings. Rajkomar et al. (2018) reported end-to-end deep learning for electronic health records in hospitals. Tonekaboni et al. (2019) analyzed clinician trust in predictive models using survey-based instruments. Ghassemi et al. (2021) highlighted pitfalls in medical AI evaluation and urged reporting that reflects clinical utility. Our study builds a hierarchical explanation interface that reconciles patient-level rationales with cohort-level evidence. We integrate uncertainty-aware attribution summaries to communicate when explanations may be unreliable. We evaluate on sepsis prediction and imaging triage, focusing on outcomes that reflect usability by clinical teams.

text: Multimodal sentiment analysis seeks to infer attitudes by combining language, acoustic, and visual signals. While benchmarks have grown, model design frequently outpaces understanding of cross-modal interactions. Prior studies debate early fusion versus late fusion and their susceptibility to modality noise. Zadeh et al. (2017) presented the CMU-MOSI dataset with aligned opinion segments for multimodal learning. Pham et al. (2019) introduced hierarchical multimodal transformers for context-sensitive sentiment. Tsai et al. (2019) explored factorized bilinear pooling to disentangle modality-specific and shared information. Hazarika et al. (2020) proposed memory networks to capture speaker states in conversational sentiment. Poria et al. (2017) built multimodal graph features for affect. Despite strong accuracy on curated datasets, robustness to missing modalities remains underexplored. Missing data often arises from occlusion, speech overlap, or transcription failures in real interaction. Recent methods attempt conditional imputation and distillation to mitigate degradation under modality dropout. Evaluation protocols vary across alignment granularity and label aggregation procedures, leading to inconsistent claims. Alam et al. (2018) examined sarcasm detection with multimodal cues on social media. Baltrušaitis et al. (2018) surveyed multimodal machine learning with a taxonomy spanning representation, translation, and co-learning. Mittal et al. (2020) studied noise-aware training for audio encoders using curriculum schedules. Kaur et al. (2021) released tools for synchronized annotation across streams in labs. Our framework addresses partial modality availability by learning uncertainty-aware gating. We introduce a dataset simulator that drops channels according to empirically observed failure modes.

(2019) explored factorized bilinear pooling to disentangle modality-specific and shared information. Hazarika et al. (2020) proposed memory networks to capture speaker states in conversational sentiment. Poria et al. (2017) built multimodal graph features for affect.

text: Recommender systems increasingly use graph neural networks to model user item interactions and side information. Graph structures capture high-order connectivity that matrix factorization fails to represent compactly. Recent surveys emphasize scalability, dynamic graphs, and debiasing as core challenges for graph recommenders. Ying et al. (2018) presented PinSage for web-scale recommendation using random-walk neighborhood sampling. Wang et al. (2019) proposed NGCF to encode collaborative signals via message passing in user item graphs. He et al. (2020) developed LightGCN with simplified propagation for stronger performance. Wu et al. (2019) summarized graph neural architectures for recommendation tasks. Sun et al. (2020) introduced scenario ranking with representations across platforms. Despite gains, encoding long-range dependencies without oversmoothing remains a persistent issue. Real systems must handle billions of edges under strict latency budgets for ranking. Negative sampling strategies interact with graph propagation depth in nontrivial ways during training. Counterfactual evaluation with logged bandit data is required to avoid optimistic bias in online metrics. Zou et al. (2019) studied adversarial attacks on recommendations through graph perturbations. Chen et al. (2020) proposed graph contrastive learning to regularize node embeddings under augmentations. Steck (2019) analyzed popularity bias and catalog coverage in implicit feedback. Saito et al. (2020) investigated user cold-start with meta-learning in large catalogs. Bonner et al. (2018) modeled exposure. We propose a latency-aware propagation scheme with calibrated sampling and off-policy evaluation for production constraints.

(2019) summarized graph neural architectures for recommendation tasks. Sun et al. (2020) introduced scenario ranking with representations across platforms.

text: Automatic question generation supports formative assessment and personalized tutoring at scale. Early systems relied on templates derived from dependency parses and named entity patterns. Neural approaches now dominate, using sequence-to-sequence models with attention and pretrained encoders. Du et al. (2017) released a large corpus for sentence-based question generation. Zhao et al. (2018) introduced paragraph-level models with pointer mechanisms for answer-aware generation. Scialom et al. (2019) leveraged reinforcement learning with factual constraints to produce controllable questions. Pan et al. (2020) explored semantic role labeling signals to improve answer focus. Subramanian et al. (2018) studied discourse cues for paragraph question generation tasks. Despite quality gains, educational utility depends on alignment with learning objectives and cognitive difficulty. Item calibration requires reliable estimates of question difficulty and discrimination. Answer-aware encoders sometimes overfit surface patterns, reducing diversity and novelty at scale. Human-in-the-loop pipelines address coverage but introduce substantial annotation overhead for course designers. Reddy et al. (2019) presented a dataset of instructor-authored questions for classroom deployment. Heilman and Smith (2010) described rule-based transformations for wh-question formation from declaratives. Pan et al. (2021) investigated controllable difficulty with syntactic edit operations. Kumar et al. (2022) analyzed distractor quality in multiple-choice generation using psychometric proxies. Li et al. (2020) studied transfer. We present a curriculum-aware generator that conditions on concept graphs and target Bloom levels.

Scialom et al. (2019) leveraged reinforcement learning with factual constraints to produce controllable questions. Pan et al. (2020) explored semantic role labeling signals to improve answer focus. Subramanian et al. (2018) studied discourse cues for paragraph question generation tasks.

text: Anomaly detection in multivariate IoT telemetry is essential for reliability and safety. Modern deployments collect high-frequency streams under tight compute and memory constraints at the edge. Machine learning methods have progressed from statistical baselines to deep representation learning for complex patterns. Malhotra et al. (2015) used LSTM autoencoders to model normal behavior in temporal sequences. Hundman et al. (2018) studied spacecraft telemetry anomalies with telemetry features and alerting pipelines. Su et al. (2019) proposed robust seasonal hybrid ESD for streaming detection in production. Ren et al. (2019) introduced Logsy to capture inter-variable dependencies in system logs. Zong et al. (2018) modeled densities with deep SVDD. Despite accuracy improvements, models often misfire on distribution shifts and maintenance events. Operational false positives cause costly investigations and alert fatigue among responders. Explainable alerts and per-sensor attributions improve triage but remain scarce in deployed systems. Label scarcity motivates self-supervision and synthetic anomaly generation to reduce dependence on incident logs. Blázquez-García et al. (2021) surveyed unsupervised approaches for time series across reconstruction and forecasting paradigms. Lai et al. (2018) proposed LSTNet combining CNN and RNN blocks for long-term dependencies. Xu et al. (2018) built DONUT for unsupervised detection using variational autoencoders. Laptev et al. (2015) reported large-scale real-world deployment characteristics for seasonal metrics in production. We introduce a streaming architecture that fuses reconstruction residuals with change-point signals. Our experiments evaluate alert burden and lead time under bursty incident regimes.

text: Explainable AI has been increasingly adopted in clinical decision support to build trust with practitioners. Prior work shows that explanation form and fidelity can shape perceived reliability of models in high-stakes settings. Smith et al. (2019) analyzed rule-based explanations for diagnostic models using stroke registries. Lee and Kumar (2020) introduced prototype-based auditing to summarize local feature attributions in radiology. Garcia et al. (2021) explored counterfactual reasoning for lab results, Nguyen et al. (2022) reported clinician preferences for contrastive rationales, Patel et al. (2018) examined dashboards for ICU triage, Chen and Rao (2020) studied saliency in EHRs, Zhou et al. (2017) evaluated attention maps. These disparate approaches underscore the challenges of aligning explanation granularity with clinical workflow. Our study evaluates explanation templates alongside counterfactual suggestions on sepsis prediction, focusing on interpretability and utility. We release code and protocols to facilitate replication across three hospital systems.

text: Graph-based recommenders leverage user-item interactions as edges to capture collaborative signals. Recent advances incorporate higher-order neighborhoods to model complex preference patterns. Wang et al. (2019) proposed NGCF to propagate embeddings along the interaction graph. He et al. (2020) presented LightGCN removing nonlinearities and feature transforms for efficiency. Sun et al. (2019) investigated knowledge-graph regularization in cross-domain recommendation, Qiu et al. (2020) studied dropout on graph topology, Koren et al. (2009) analyzed factorization baselines, Rendle (2010) introduced Bayesian personalized ranking, Ying et al. (2018) developed PinSage for web-scale graphs and session modeling tasks. Despite gains on static datasets, reproducibility remains limited by inconsistent evaluation splits and sampling. We revisit neighborhood propagation under a unified negative sampling protocol across six benchmarks. Our experiments reveal a trade-off between depth and calibration, motivating a simple tuning strategy for practical deployments.

text: Parameter-efficient transfer has become a central strategy for scaling multilingual NLP across many languages. Houlsby et al. (2019) introduced adapter layers enabling fast task-switching without full fine-tuning. Pfeiffer et al. (2020) proposed modular adapters for cross-lingual transfer across encoders. Bapna and Firat (2019) explored language-specific adapters in multilingual NMT, Philip et al. (2020) studied routing for adapters, Wang et al. (2021) examined residual sharing for low-resource languages, Ansell et al. (2021) evaluated adapter fusion, Xue et al. (2021) reported behavior on mT5 with parameter-efficient variants. Despite these advances, the interaction between adapter placement and subword segmentation remains underexplored. We investigate insertion strategies across encoder depths while controlling vocabulary granularity and training budget. Our analysis isolates capacity bottlenecks that emerge when morphology mismatches tokenization. The resulting guidelines suggest robust configurations for new languages with limited compute.

text: Data augmentation mitigates overfitting and label scarcity in text classification. Prior methods range from heuristic edits to model-based paraphrasing with variable semantic fidelity. We focus on controllable policies that target minority classes and rare lexical phenomena. Our framework unifies policy learning with uncertainty-aware filtering to preserve label semantics. Wei and Zou (2019) proposed EDA operations that randomly swap, delete, and replace words. Kobayashi (2018) leveraged conditional language models to substitute tokens while preserving context likelihood. Feng et al. (2021) surveyed augmentation libraries, Xie et al. (2020) introduced unsupervised back-translation for low-resource text, Sennrich et al. (2016) established back-translation for MT, Anaby-Tavor et al. (2020) synthesized class-conditional examples with GPT-2, Kumar et al. (2021) explored diversity-promoting constraints on paraphrasers too. We benchmark across four corpora with human evaluation of fluency and consistency.

text: Automatic code generation has progressed rapidly with large language models trained on massive repositories. Yet evaluation remains fragmented across datasets, metrics, and human studies, obscuring real gains. Prior work assesses functional correctness via unit tests while probing security and maintainability. Chen et al. (2021) evaluated Codex on program synthesis with pass@k metrics. Lu et al. (2021) compiled CodeXGLUE to standardize tasks and metrics for code intelligence. Hendrycks et al. (2021) proposed APPS for problem-solving, Austin et al. (2021) studied execution-guided decoding, Nijkamp et al. (2022) explored infilling for program repair, Li et al. (2022) constructed MBPP for problems, Lahiri et al. (2022) analyzed docstring evaluation, Le et al. (2023) reported tests. Across these resources, definitions of success vary widely, complicating cross-paper comparison for practitioners. We introduce a unified harness with oracle execution and unbiased sampling to reconcile results under consistent conditions.

text: Data-efficient automatic speech recognition has gained renewed attention as labeled audio remains costly in many domains. Prior work demonstrates that self-supervised pretraining substantially reduces labeled requirements when combined with lightweight finetuning. Despite rapid progress, comparisons are hindered by inconsistent evaluation data and recipe details across studies. We propose a standardized benchmark and ablation protocol that isolates augmentation, architecture, and label budget. Our study focuses on streaming-capable encoders with constrained footprint for on-device scenarios. We review related efforts that inform the design of our experiments. Baevski et al. (2020) introduced wav2vec 2.0 for self-supervised speech learning. Hsu et al. (2021) investigated scaling laws in masked prediction for audio. Conneau et al. (2021) presented XLS-R for multilingual representation learning. Zhang et al. (2022) studied data augmentation with time warping and reverberation. Chen et al. (2019) explored lightweight streaming encoders too. Existing benchmarks rarely constrain latency and memory, obscuring practical trade-offs. We curate a suite of English and multilingual corpora with unified streaming constraints. To ensure fair comparison, we align tokenizers, decoding policies, and learning rate schedules across models. We report label efficiency curves and compute-normalized scores to highlight real-world efficiency. Our ablation isolates the effects of noise simulation versus synthetic augmentation under identical budgets. The results reveal consistent benefits from modest pretraining when paired with strong beam pruning. We release recipes and artifacts to support reproducible, resource-aware ASR research.

(2019) explored lightweight streaming encoders too.

text: Personalized recommendations are increasingly accompanied by textual rationales to improve user trust and control. Despite the promise, current explanations often overfit item popularity and fail to convey actionable insight. We target model-agnostic, faithful rationales that reflect the decision process rather than surface correlations. Our approach combines sparse feature attribution with structured review selection under budget constraints. We position our work within the broader literature on explainability for collaborative filtering and sequence models. Early neural recommenders offered post hoc saliency maps without faithfulness guarantees, inviting more rigorous evaluation. Recent studies evaluate plausibility and faithfulness using human judgments and counterfactual tests in shared benchmarks. Zhang et al. (2014) proposed explicit factor models for explainable collaborative filtering. Chen and Wang (2017) introduced attention-based opinion extraction from reviews. Li et al. (2019) combined aspect-level sentiment with pointer networks. Wu et al. (2020) built session-based explanations using graph neural networks. Ma et al. (2021) explored counterfactual reasoning for recommendation explainability techniques. These observations motivate our focus on perturbation-based tests aligned with the underlying ranking objective. We introduce a dataset that couples purchase histories with annotated rationales from domain experts. The method selects minimal review snippets that preserve ranking decisions under constrained deletion. We compare with attention rationales, gradient attributions, and concept bottlenecks under identical training settings. Results show improved faithfulness with negligible loss in recommendation quality across three domains. We release code and annotation guidelines to facilitate reproducible progress in explainable recommendation.

(2019) combined aspect-level sentiment with pointer networks. Wu et al. (2020) built session-based explanations using graph neural networks. Ma et al. (2021) explored counterfactual reasoning for recommendation explainability techniques.

Li et al. (2019) combined aspect-level sentiment with pointer networks. Wu et al. (2020) built session-based explanations using graph neural networks. Ma et al. (2021) explored counterfactual reasoning for recommendation explainability techniques.

text: Cross-domain medical image segmentation suffers from scanner shifts and protocol variation that degrade generalization. Collecting labeled data for each target site is rarely feasible in clinical practice. Unsupervised domain adaptation is therefore a practical alternative for reliable deployment. We study a source-free setting where only a trained model and unlabeled target images are available. Our method adapts batch statistics and prototypes while enforcing structural consistency across scales. We contextualize the approach within prior adaptation techniques for visual recognition and dense prediction. Early work examined adversarial objectives and style transfer to align distributions for segmentation tasks. Self-training methods further improved target performance by iteratively refining pseudo labels. Ganin and Lempitsky (2015) proposed domain-adversarial training to learn invariant features. Tzeng et al. (2017) studied adversarial adaptation with feature and pixel losses. Hoffman et al. (2018) developed Cycle-Consistent adaptation for structured predictions. Chen et al. (2019) introduced style transfer to bridge appearance gaps in cross-modality segmentation. Zou et al. (2019) explored noisy self-training. These techniques highlight the tension between stability and plasticity when adapting fine-grained boundaries. Building on these insights, we estimate classwise prototypes from confident regions and propagate constraints spatially. We regularize the adaptation with entropy smoothing to prevent collapse on small organs. Experiments on CT and MRI datasets show consistent gains over competitive alternatives without target labels. Code and preprocessed splits will be released to facilitate evaluation across institutions.

Hoffman et al. (2018) developed Cycle-Consistent adaptation for structured predictions. Chen et al. (2019) introduced style transfer to bridge appearance gaps in cross-modality segmentation. Zou et al. (2019) explored noisy self-training.
