text: Recent progress in large language models has transformed the landscape of code intelligence. Models can now synthesize functions and tests directly from natural language specifications. Yet correctness remains fragile under distribution shifts and long-horizon dependencies. We study constrained decoding and feedback-driven refinement to improve functional accuracy. Our framework integrates type hints, unit tests, and symbolic checks during inference. We situate our contribution within prior work on pretrained models for program synthesis. Early systems relied on statistical machine translation analogies with limited generalization beyond templates. Scaling transformer decoders led to substantial gains on competitive programming benchmarks. Chen et al. (2021) released Codex for natural language to code generation. Austin et al. (2021) investigated program synthesis with pretrained transformers at scale. Li et al. (2022) introduced CodeT5 with task-specific pretraining objectives. Nijkamp et al. (2023) studied executable code generation with self-consistency decoding. Zhang et al. (2023) examined retrieval for grounding libraries. These developments underscore the importance of integrating execution signals into decoding loops. We propose a test-time search that allocates budget adaptively by uncertainty and cost. The approach balances exploration of diverse hypotheses with incremental repair of failing candidates. On HumanEval and MBPP, the method improves pass@k at fixed compute budgets. We discuss security implications and release an evaluation harness for reproducible studies.

(2023) examined retrieval for grounding libraries.

text: Understanding stance in social media supports content moderation and deliberation analytics. The task requires predicting an author's position toward a given target from noisy posts. Challenges include implicit targets, sarcasm, and rapid topic shift across events. We investigate domain-robust stance detection using parameter-efficient adapters with target-aware prompts. The model separates target representations from topical confounders via contrastive regularization. We situate our contribution within prior supervised and weakly supervised approaches to stance modeling. Early pipelines leveraged sentiment lexicons and hand-engineered features for explicit targets in curated datasets. Mohammad et al. (2016) introduced SemEval tasks that established early evaluation standards. Augenstein et al. (2016) explored target-dependent attention for stance classification. Du et al. (2017) examined multi-task learning with sentiment and stance. Ghosh et al. (2019) modeled conversation structure to propagate stance across threads. Zhang et al. (2020) released datasets spanning controversial topics. These efforts highlight the importance of target conditioning and discourse context for difficult cases. Our approach consolidates these ideas by aligning target prompts with retrieved evidence spans. We curate event-split benchmarks where topics do not overlap between training and test. Evaluation covers English Twitter, Reddit, and multilingual protest discussions with matched annotation protocols. Results show stable gains in F1 while reducing annotation effort through few-shot transfer. We provide code, data cards, and error analyses to support responsible deployment.

(2020) released datasets spanning controversial topics.

text: This work examines multimodal summarization with images and long transcripts. Prior research emphasizes aligning visual cues with discourse structure. We focus on extractive guidance for generative summarizers in newsroom settings. Related studies address multimodal fusion, dataset scale, and evaluation robustness. Early fusion treats images as additional tokens appended to text encodings. Late fusion aggregates modality-specific predictions using learned gates. Hybrid strategies learn both cross-attention and modality selection policies. These approaches motivate our exploration of contrastive supervision. Chen et al. (2020) introduced a transformer that pairs regional visual features with sentence positions for news highlights. Li and Zhu (2019) built a large-scale dataset of image-caption-article triples oriented to lifestyle and culture reporting. Park et al. (2021) proposed a dual-encoder retriever for linking frames to paragraphs under weak timestamp supervision. Ng and Chiu (2020) modeled salience with coverage penalties on visual attention and encouraged diversity through orthogonal pooling operations. Ramos et al. (2018) examined cross-modal redundancy in event summaries using topic graphs and reported mixed gains over text-only baselines. Prior image selection heuristics remain underexplored for breaking ties. Beyond architectures, the field also debates annotation consistency. Automatic metrics often fail to capture multimodal faithfulness. Human judgments correlate weakly with reference-based scores on noisy data. We adopt protocols that separate salience, groundedness, and fluency. Training data quality also shapes the behavior of multimodal systems. Weakly aligned screenshots can bias models toward superficial cues. We curate examples that balance narrative and visual density. The proposed method integrates retrieval to constrain the decoder. Retrieved snippets serve as checkpoints for evidence grounding. Our experiments span diverse publishers and event types. We report both reference-based and faithfulness-oriented metrics. An ablation suite isolates the contribution of each component. All code and annotations will be released upon acceptance.

Ng and Chiu (2020) modeled salience with coverage penalties on visual attention and encouraged diversity through orthogonal pooling operations. Ramos et al. (2018) examined cross-modal redundancy in event summaries using topic graphs and reported mixed gains over text-only baselines. Prior image selection heuristics remain underexplored for breaking ties.

text: We investigate fairness-aware learning in sequential recommendation. Our goal is to mitigate exposure disparities while preserving utility. The setting involves bandit feedback under user and item constraints. Prior work has explored calibration, coverage, and diversity controls. We consider demographic parity at the slate level. The literature connects allocation algorithms with stochastic ranking. We build on differentiable surrogates for nondecomposable objectives. This section reviews key developments relevant to our approach. Yao and Huang (2017) formalized fairness in collaborative filtering by measuring disparate exposure across user groups using ranking-based objectives carefully. Singh and Joachims (2018) proposed constrained ranking optimization that treats provider fairness as a budgeted constraint without explicit user-side personalization. Biega et al. (2018) introduced amortized fairness for rankings by bounding cumulative exposure over time to protect underrepresented items effectively. Patro et al. (2020) studied counterfactual interventions in recommenders using pseudo-demographics and assessed disparate impact under controlled perturbations via simulation. Sapiezynski et al. (2019) measured platform exposure bias using audited profiles and reported skews across content categories and creator attributes. Exposure regularization can be achieved with differentiable surrogates for ranked positions. We adopt position-based credit to approximate slate-level constraints. The optimization is cast as weighted bipartite matching during training. We incorporate user intent by conditioning constraints on recent sequences. Calibration is maintained through temperature-sharpened target distributions. To preserve utility, we mix in a margin loss on clicks. Our algorithm computes control variates for variance reduction in gradients. The framework extends to multiple provider attributes without retraining. Offline evaluation uses replay simulation with interleaving to reduce bias. We release a semi-synthetic benchmark with public user sequences. Metrics include exposure parity, coverage, and normalized discounted utility. Ablations isolate the effect of constraint tightness and slate size. The proposed approach attains strong fairness utility tradeoffs across regimes.

Singh and Joachims (2018) proposed constrained ranking optimization that treats provider fairness as a budgeted constraint without explicit user-side personalization. Biega et al. (2018) introduced amortized fairness for rankings by bounding cumulative exposure over time to protect underrepresented items effectively. Patro et al. (2020) studied counterfactual interventions in recommenders using pseudo-demographics and assessed disparate impact under controlled perturbations via simulation. Sapiezynski et al. (2019) measured platform exposure bias using audited profiles and reported skews across content categories and creator attributes.

text: This paper studies constrained code generation using large language models. We aim to enforce API usage and control side effects during synthesis. The constraints are expressed as temporal logic over program traces. Existing work investigates prompting, fine-tuning, and verifier-guided decoding. We situate our method at the intersection of program analysis and decoding control. Prior surveys have cataloged failure modes in code completion scenarios. Our focus is on post hoc verifiability and preemption of unsafe calls. The following section reviews relevant advances in neural program synthesis. Chen et al. (2021) released CodeXGLUE with standardized tasks for code intelligence, covering translation, refinement, and clone detection benchmarks widely. Austin et al. (2021) evaluated large language models for code by measuring pass rates on unit tests across open-source repositories. Le et al. (2022) introduced Codex-Edit for instruction-based code modifications using constrained decoding to enforce locality during transformations in practice. Jain et al. (2022) presented execution-guided decoding that prunes candidates by partial interpretation and maintains a beam under runtime feedback. Wang et al. (2023) studied self-repair in code generation using learned critics that propose edits after detecting failing unit tests. These studies underscore the importance of evaluation design and feedback channels. Our formulation unifies decoding with verifiable constraints over program semantics. We compile specifications into automata that guide token acceptance. The automata interface exposes admissible next actions to the generator. Constraint satisfaction is checked incrementally while decoding proceeds. Violations trigger backtracking using a bounded repair search. The approach reduces invalid API patterns on multiple libraries. We integrate type checking signals as soft scoring features. A sandbox executor supplies partial traces during beam expansion. Datasets include synthesis from docstrings and from natural language. We report pass rates and constraint satisfaction statistics. Ablations evaluate automata size, beam width, and repair budget. Reproducible code and specifications are provided in the repository.

text: We address data augmentation for low-resource neural machine translation. Our method targets lexical diversity while controlling alignment fidelity. The approach inserts paraphrastic variants using bilingual constraints. Prior work has explored back-translation, noising, and representation mixing. We aim to synthesize pairs that preserve adequacy beyond surface changes. The challenge lies in avoiding semantic drift during augmentation. We operate in settings with limited parallel data and noisy monolingual corpora. The following review situates our contributions in existing research. Sennrich et al. (2016) popularized back-translation by generating source sentences from target monolingual data to expand parallel corpora quite effectively. Edunov et al. (2018) investigated sampling strategies for back-translation and showed improvements from diverse synthetic sources in high-resource settings too. Fadaee et al. (2017) proposed rare word data augmentation by replacing words with low-frequency synonyms to enrich lexical coverage substantially. Nguyen et al. (2020) analyzed round-trip consistency as a signal for filtering noisy synthetic pairs produced by iterative back-translation pipelines. Poncelas et al. (2018) examined data diversification techniques including choice perturbations across pivot languages and reported gains on distant pairs. These threads highlight objectives for effective augmentation under scarcity. Our strategy composes paraphrase lattices constrained by bilingual lexicons. Candidate substitutions are filtered by alignment confidence and language model scores. We ensure tokenization invariance by applying detokenization checks post substitution. The generator samples paths that satisfy adequacy thresholds. A discriminator penalizes semantically drifting pairs during training. We integrate augmented data with curriculum scheduling based on rarity. Experiments span four low-resource language families with morphological variation. We compare against back-translation, constrained replacement, and round-trip filters. Metrics include BLEU, chrF, and human adequacy judgments. Gains persist across transformer sizes and regularization regimes. Ablations isolate lexicon coverage, lattice width, and discriminator weight. We release code and lexicons to facilitate replication.

text: We study grounding in conversational search where users iteratively refine intents. Our framework links dialogue state tracking with evidence retrieval. The problem involves maintaining entailment between responses and cited sources. We address hallucination by constraining generation to retrieved spans. Prior work spans conversational QA, citation control, and interactive IR. We include learning-to-rank components in the retriever reader pipeline. The discourse model predicts follow-up needs and answer types. This section situates our approach within the broader literature. Rastogi et al. (2020) built the MultiWOZ-style task-oriented dialogues dataset for schema-guided systems with numerous domains and labeled state transitions. Chen et al. (2020) proposed ReCoRD-style cloze reading comprehension where commonsense reasoning is evaluated through entity-centric evidence selection under pressure. Qin et al. (2021) introduced pipeline for open-domain conversational QA that combines dense retrievers with response generators trained on dialogues. Glass et al. (2022) proposed attribution-aware decoding for faithful generation by aligning token probabilities with supporting passages retrieved at runtime. Kulshreshtha et al. (2023) evaluated hallucination in conversational agents using contradiction detection against sources and released benchmark for grounded dialogue. These lines of work highlight supervision signals available for grounded agents. Our system composes retrieval with a discourse state controller. The controller predicts query intents and citation requirements. We train a rewriter to contextualize follow-up questions. A verifier judges entailment between the draft reply and sources. If entailment fails, the generator is nudged via constrained decoding. The retriever indexes passages with sentence-level offsets for precise spans. We adopt dense and sparse hybrids to maximize recall. Training uses conversational logs with human-provided annotations. Evaluation considers answer quality, grounding rate, and user preference. We run studies on realistic multi-turn information needs. Ablations test the contribution of the verifier and rewriter. Resources and code will be shared with the community.

text: Recommender systems increasingly mediate access to information, raising urgent questions about fairness and exposure inequality. Yao and Huang (2017) study disparate impact in collaborative filtering using user-group metrics, reporting accuracy trade-offs across demographic segments and platforms. Ekstrand et al. (2018) evaluate item-provider fairness and observe systematic skewed exposure in offline experiments. Biega et al. (2018) propose amortized exposure constraints for rankings, Diaz et al. (2020) analyze position bias in multi-stakeholder settings, Mehrotra et al. (2018) examine counterfactual logging for fair learning. We build on these insights to formalize a controllable exposure objective for slate generation and validate it online.

text: Domain adaptation remains a central challenge for neural machine translation due to mismatches between training and deployment data. Luong and Manning (2015) introduce fine-tuning for sequence-to-sequence models with gains on in-domain test sets and specialized vocabularies. Chu et al. (2017) study multi-domain training using domain tags and data sampling to balance corpora. Aharoni and Goldberg (2020) explore instance weighting with gradient regularization, Britz et al. (2017) examine model capacity under subword segmentation, van der Wees et al. (2017) propose dynamic data selection across epochs. We pursue a calibration-driven approach that decouples domain preference from lexical choice and report results on noisy business communications.

text: Detecting rare anomalies in time series is hampered by scarce labels and unstable generalization across sensors. Malhotra et al. (2016) use LSTM encoders to model normal behavior and flag deviations by reconstruction error. Audibert et al. (2020) present an unsupervised approach with isolation forests over learned features from deep networks. Schlegl et al. (2017) apply generative adversarial training to identify outliers, Hundman et al. (2018) report anomalies in spacecraft data with sequence models, Zhou et al. (2019) design motif augmentations for detection. We propose augmentation-aware risk minimization that learns invariances from synthetic perturbations while preserving event-level fidelity.

text: Automated essay scoring aims to approximate human judgments under constraints of reliability and transparency. Shermis and Hamner (2012) benchmark commercial systems on large essay sets and report correlations with expert graders across diverse prompts. Taghipour and Ng (2016) use convolutional and recurrent encoders to learn holistic features for scoring. Uto et al. (2020) investigate prompt-specific adaptation with limited data, Cummins et al. (2016) critique construct validity in automated measures, Phandi et al. (2015) explore ordinal regression frameworks for proficiency bands. Our approach targets content-based evidence aggregation while maintaining interpretability through rubric-aligned rationales.

text: Label scarcity in medical imaging motivates self-supervised pretraining tailored to anatomical and acquisition characteristics. Zhuang et al. (2019) evaluate transfer from natural images to radiology tasks and observe limited gains without adaptation. Azizi et al. (2021) scale multimodal pretraining on paired images and reports to improve downstream classification and segmentation. Chen et al. (2020) introduce contrastive learning for 3D scans, Taleb et al. (2020) study rotation-based surrogates in volumetric settings, Chaitanya et al. (2020) combine context restoration with contrastive objectives. We pretrain with anatomy-aware views and evaluate generalization across scanners and labels in limited-data regimes.

text: Recommender systems increasingly rely on graph representations to capture high-order interactions among users and items. Recent models leverage message passing to propagate signals across neighborhoods while maintaining sparse computation. Despite substantial progress, the interplay between structural inductive biases and temporal dynamics remains underexplored. We study how lightweight temporal encoding can be integrated into graph recommenders without sacrificing scalability. Prior research on graph-based recommendation is diverse and fast evolving. Wang et al. (2019) introduced LightGCN to simplify propagation by removing nonlinear transformations. He et al. (2020) examined self-supervised signals on graphs for representation learning in recommendation. Ying et al. (2018) proposed PinSage with random walks for web-scale item discovery. Zhang et al. (2019) studied session graphs for recommendation. Yet existing methods rarely quantify the effect of timestamp granularity on neighbor selection. Temporal decay is often applied heuristically and tuned per dataset. This leaves open questions about stability under shifting interaction rates. Some studies incorporate recurrent units into graph encoders to handle temporal signals. Others discretize time into windows and aggregate interactions within each slice. We build on these directions by proposing a schedule-aware propagation scheme. Rendle et al. (2012) presented Bayesian Personalized Ranking for implicit feedback and popularized pairwise training. Hidasi et al. (2015) introduced GRU4Rec for session-based recommendation with recurrent networks. Sun et al. (2019) proposed BERT4Rec using bidirectional transformers for sequential signals across item lists. McInerney et al. (2018) studied exploration strategies in bandit recommenders with sublinear regret guarantees, evaluating policy choices on large-scale platforms using historical offline logs and simulators. Our method introduces decay-adaptive edges and calibrates timestamps through learnable kernels. We provide a theoretical analysis of stability under time rescaling. The approach maintains linear complexity with respect to observed interactions. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements across click-through and rating tasks. We release code and preprocessed splits to support reproducibility and fair comparison. Ethical considerations address potential biases in temporal signals and user privacy.

(2019) studied session graphs for recommendation.

text: Domain shifts are pervasive in medical imaging due to scanner variation and patient demographics. Models trained in one hospital often degrade when deployed elsewhere without recalibration. Unsupervised domain adaptation aims to bridge this gap by aligning distributions without target labels. We investigate compositional alignment strategies that respect anatomical structure while minimizing annotation burden. Prior art spans adversarial learning, self-training, and style transfer techniques. Ganin and Lempitsky (2015) proposed domain-adversarial training to learn invariant features with gradient reversal. Tzeng et al. (2017) introduced adversarial discriminative domain adaptation in a two-stream network. Hoffman et al. (2018) studied mapping-based approaches with cycle consistency for cross-domain alignment. Pei et al. (2018) studied classifier discrepancy for adaptation. While effective, these strategies often distort local anatomy when applied to pixel spaces. In medical contexts, structural priors and shape constraints are clinically important. Representation alignment benefits from respecting organ topology and scan protocols. Self-training has emerged as an attractive alternative for label-efficient adaptation. Confidence filtering and curriculum schedules reduce confirmation bias in pseudo-labeling. French et al. (2018) applied consistency regularization to segmentation using perturbation invariance. Zhang et al. (2018) proposed CycleGAN-based style transfer to match scanner appearance across domains. Liang et al. (2020) explored test-time training to adapt normalization statistics during deployment. Karani et al. (2018) investigated lifelong learning for neuroimaging with limited labels and domain variability. Zhou et al. (2021) examined source-free adaptation through entropy minimization and prototype alignment objectives. Our approach enforces region-level consistency by aligning equivariant embeddings within anatomical masks. The training objective decomposes into segmentation loss and structure-preserving adaptation loss. We derive bounds on target risk under bounded deformations of organ shape. Experiments on multi-center MRI and CT benchmarks show improved Dice and Hausdorff scores. We ablate components to quantify the contribution of each regularizer. Code, synthetic data generators, and pretrained weights will be released upon acceptance.

text: Prompt learning has emerged as an effective way to adapt large language models to new tasks with minimal supervision. Instead of updating all parameters, prompts steer model behavior through small learned vectors or textual templates. This paradigm reduces memory footprint while preserving generalization across domains. We investigate prompt selection under distribution shifts with limited validation data. Prior work spans manual templates, soft prompts, and prefix-tuning. Brown et al. (2020) demonstrated zero-shot and few-shot capabilities with in-context examples in GPT-3. Li and Liang (2021) introduced prefix-tuning to condition generation through trainable prefixes. Lester et al. (2021) proposed prompt tuning with task-specific soft embeddings for language understanding. Schick and Schütze (2021) explored pattern-exploiting training with verbalizers. Despite promising results, prompt selection is sensitive to spurious lexical cues in unseen domains. Robustness often depends on the availability of target examples for calibration. Recent studies attempt to regularize prompts with constraints and diversity objectives. Calibration methods aim to reduce label bias through debiasing priors and balanced contexts. We focus on model selection without labels by exploiting agreement signals. Gao et al. (2021) investigated automatic prompt generation using mined templates and scoring functions. Zhong et al. (2021) proposed contextualized verbalizers with search over candidate label words. Wei et al. (2022) studied chain-of-thought prompting to elicit intermediate reasoning steps across tasks. Holtzman et al. (2020) analyzed degeneration in language models under common decoding algorithms. Perez et al. (2021) examined calibration strategies for prompts with likelihood estimates and metrics. Our method selects prompts by maximizing agreement across independently initialized models. We incorporate a small held-out pool from source data to estimate stability. The procedure requires no target labels and introduces negligible overhead. Experiments on sentiment, natural language inference, and topic classification show gains under shift. We report ablations on agreement temperature, prompt pool size, and initialization diversity. The results highlight practical guidelines for deploying prompt learning under limited supervision.

text: Vision Transformers have achieved strong performance across image classification and detection tasks. Their self-attention architecture offers new opportunities for interpretation compared to convolutional networks. Yet faithful explanations remain challenging due to distributed representations and multi-head interactions. We study attribution alignment with human-annotated region rationales for transformer models. We review related efforts on saliency, attention rollout, and concept-based methods. Chefer et al. (2021) proposed transformer-specific relevance propagation to attribute predictions to image patches. Abnar and Zuidema (2020) introduced attention rollout to accumulate contributions across layers. Dosovitskiy et al. (2020) presented ViT as a pure transformer model for images. Caron et al. (2021) explored self-supervised transformers with object discovery. Post-hoc explanations typically optimize proxy objectives that correlate imperfectly with fidelity. Attention weights may not reflect causal importance due to entanglement across heads. Attribution noise complicates human evaluation and can mislead practitioners. Concept-based methods aggregate signals but require labeled exemplars for each concept. Our approach aligns token-level attributions with compositional region masks. Sundararajan et al. (2017) introduced Integrated Gradients to attribute predictions by path-integrated gradients. Selvaraju et al. (2017) proposed Grad-CAM to visualize class-discriminative regions in convolutional networks. Fong and Vedaldi (2017) studied meaningful perturbations that optimize deletions to identify salient evidence. Adebayo et al. (2018) evaluated saliency methods with randomization tests questioning model sensitivity. Hooker et al. (2019) analyzed faithfulness using remove-and-retrain benchmarks on vision datasets of varied granularity. We incorporate attention-flow constraints that encourage attribution to follow connected paths. The learning objective penalizes divergence between attention rollout and gradient-based maps. We propose sanity checks that perturb tokenization and patch embeddings. Quantitative evaluation uses pointing game accuracy and deletion curves on ImageNet and CUB. Human studies measure alignment with expert annotations for object parts. Our findings inform practical guidelines for explanation fidelity in transformer-based vision systems.

text: Federated learning enables decentralized training across clients while keeping raw data local. Privacy concerns persist because gradient updates can leak sensitive information. Differential privacy offers formal guarantees by injecting noise during optimization. Balancing privacy budgets with model utility remains a central challenge. We explore adaptive noise allocation for heterogeneous clients and tasks. McMahan et al. (2017) introduced Federated Averaging to aggregate local updates across distributed devices. Kairouz et al. (2021) surveyed advances in federated learning. Abadi et al. (2016) proposed the moments accountant to track privacy loss in deep learning. Bassily et al. (2014) analyzed optimal private mechanisms for ERM theory. Practical deployments must also contend with heterogeneous data distributions and stragglers. Communication efficiency is crucial when bandwidth is scarce or energy constrained. Adaptive client selection can improve convergence and fairness across populations. Privacy accounting interacts with optimizer dynamics and gradient clipping. We design a controller that adapts noise scales to client variability. Agarwal et al. (2018) examined trade-offs between privacy and accuracy under convex objectives. Mironov (2017) introduced Rényi differential privacy as an analysis tool for composition. Carlini et al. (2021) demonstrated memorization risks in large models through extraction attacks. Bonawitz et al. (2019) described secure aggregation protocols for practical federated deployments at scale. Truex et al. (2019) surveyed privacy attacks and defenses in collaborative learning across threat models widely. Our method tunes per-client clipping and noise using meta-gradients estimated from held-out slices. The analysis provides bounds on expected excess risk under adaptive privacy composition. We show improved fairness across demographics without sacrificing global accuracy. Large-scale experiments on language modeling and image classification validate the approach. We quantify communication, computation, and privacy costs using unified accounting. The release includes reference implementations and audit scripts for end-to-end evaluation.

text: This paper investigates automatic coherence assessment in multi-paragraph scientific abstracts. We aim to compare modeling strategies that capture discourse flow across sections and references. Smith (2018) introduced a weakly supervised tagger for discourse cues in argumentative essays. Johnson and Lee (2019) explored hierarchical attention for evidence retrieval in long documents. Nguyen et al. (2021) proposed contrastive objectives for citation context modeling in scholarly graphs. Garcia and Patel (2020) introduced a multilingual benchmark for cross-domain summarization with noisily aligned sources. Kumar et al. (2022) examined layout-aware encoders in financial prospectuses and filings. Building on these observations, we formulate a modeling framework that aligns predicted discourse transitions with citation roles while remaining lightweight for practical deployment.

text: We study multilingual question answering where queries and evidence may span languages and structured knowledge bases. A central challenge is aligning lexical, semantic, and graph signals without abundant supervision in each language. We review prior datasets and cross-lingual representations that inform the design of our retrieval and reasoning modules. Chen et al. (2017) released WikiReading to study question answering with rich entity typing across distant supervision signals and noisy alignments. Asai et al. (2020) proposed XorQA to enable cross-lingual open retrieval using multilingual pivoting on knowledge bases and mined parallel titles. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) introduced LASER for language-agnostic sentence embeddings that facilitate bitext retrieval and semantic transfer across diverse typologies. Mihalcea (2007) examined cross-lingual features. Motivated by gaps in calibration and grounding, we propose a retriever-reader with joint entity linking across scripts.

text: Anomaly detection in multivariate time series is essential for reliable monitoring of cyber-physical systems. Despite progress, models often overfit periodicity and underperform when regimes shift unexpectedly. We revisit representation learning approaches that balance reconstruction, prediction, and density modeling in high dimensions. Xu et al. (2018) applied variational recurrent autoencoders to detect anomalies in industrial sensors with limited labels and nonstationary conditions at scale. Zong et al. (2018) introduced DAGMM combining density estimation with autoencoding to capture global context and compact latent compressions for rare events. Ren et al. (2019) proposed PReNet for predictive reconstruction on videos using probabilistic frames and context aggregation to flag deviations during operation periods. These studies emphasize distinct objectives and architectures but leave open questions about online adaptation. Our method integrates streaming segmentation with calibrated uncertainty to prioritize actionable alerts under compute constraints.

text: We consider robustness and calibration of neural machine translation under distribution shift and resource limitations. Our focus is on test-time perturbations and the role of uncertainty estimation in decoding under risk. Luong and Manning (2015) analyzed attention variants for translation quality on WMT benchmarks standard tasks. Ott et al. (2018) scaled sequence-to-sequence training with large minibatches and synchronized updates across data centers globally. Ng et al. (2019) reported strong BLEU with transformer big settings and optimized decoding for latency constraints. Koehn and Knowles (2017) surveyed challenges including domain shift, rare words, and adequacy issues affecting generalization in systems. These works provide strong baselines but leave evaluation beyond BLEU largely unsettled. We propose risk-aware beam search with domain-adaptive priors and actionable reliability metrics.

text: Graph-based recommenders leverage high-order connectivity to model user preferences beyond co-occurrence signals. Despite recent advances, deploying such models at scale entails balancing accuracy, sparsity, and serving latency. We revisit design choices in message passing and sampling that impact training stability. Our review grounds the architectural space before introducing an efficient backbone for cold-start and long-tail items. Ying et al. (2018) presented PinSage to scale graph convolutions for web-scale recommendation with efficient random walks and importance sampling strategies online. He et al. (2020) proposed LightGCN emphasizing neighborhood aggregation without feature transformation to improve collaborative filtering efficiency significantly on benchmarks widely used. Wu et al. (2019) studied simplifying graph convolutional networks for recommendation and analyzed layer depth interactions with user-item bipartite structures in extensive ablation. We build on these insights to propose a cache-aware training regimen with calibrated exploration.

text: Conversational agents increasingly operate in safety-critical contexts where harmful outputs can erode user trust and cause real harm. Research has explored both modeling choices and data interventions to constrain generation without sacrificing utility. Despite rapid progress, the landscape of techniques is fragmented and the interplay between training signals and decoding remains unclear. Smith (2020) models dialogue safety with constraint-based decoding for toxic content detection in open-domain systems. Lee and Park (2019) introduce adversarial training that perturbs responses to reduce unsafe utterances in goal-oriented assistants. Nguyen et al. (2021) examine user feedback loops for toxicity mitigation and report calibration drift across deployment cycles. Prior work on response personalization focuses on persona consistency using memory networks and retrieval signals from social profiles. Yet connections among these strands are under-specified, leaving open questions about robustness across domains and time. We present a unified evaluation protocol and a modular training framework that jointly targets calibration, refusal quality, and safety recall.

Prior work on response personalization focuses on persona consistency using memory networks and retrieval signals from social profiles.

text: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw data, yet practical deployments face robustness and privacy constraints. A growing body of work examines optimization behavior, privacy leakage, and systems overhead at scale. Kairouz et al. (2021) survey algorithmic advances in federated optimization and formalize client drift under heterogeneous data. McMahan et al. (2017) propose FedAvg to aggregate local updates, demonstrating competitive accuracy with limited communication. Truex et al. (2019) analyze membership inference risks in collaborative learning and highlight vulnerabilities at the client and server. Compression techniques using quantization learn rate strategies for efficient on-device storage and energy proportional training pipelines. These threads are seldom synthesized into a common threat-aware benchmarking regime. We target cross-client variability with adaptive aggregation guided by risk calibrations derived from privacy budgets. Our evaluation covers real-world non-iid distributions and adversarial participation patterns.

text: Personalized recommendation has evolved from memory-based heuristics to deep retrieval and ranking pipelines operating at industrial scale. Despite progress, methodological fragmentation obscures how representation learning, candidate generation, and calibration interact. We review modeling trends before outlining our approach to controllable objectives under exposure bias. He et al. (2017) introduce Neural Collaborative Filtering to learn user–item interactions via multi-layer perceptrons. Rendle (2010) presents factorization machines that generalize matrix factorization with feature crosses for sparse signals. Covington et al. (2016) describe a large-scale two-tower architecture powering video recommendations with high-throughput retrieval and ranking. Recent advances in causal estimation target counterfactual explanations of outcomes using instrumental variables and structured priors for observational user data analysis. The broader discussion leaves open how training-time constraints propagate to long-horizon engagement and fairness metrics. We propose a modular objective with counterfactual regularization and present evaluations on public and production-scale datasets.

text: Accurate time series forecasting underpins planning in energy, retail, and transportation systems. Recent models seek calibrated uncertainty and long-horizon stability while accommodating intermittent patterns. Salinas et al. (2020) develop DeepAR, an autoregressive recurrent model with likelihood-based training for probabilistic forecasting. Wen et al. (2017) propose Sequence-to-Sequence models with temporal features to capture seasonality and promotions in retail demand. Oreshkin et al. (2019) introduce N-BEATS using residual stacks and backcast–forecast blocks to learn generic basis functions. Self-supervised pretraining on image corpora yields transferable features through contrastive objectives and clustering assignments for representation alignment. Despite these developments, unified evaluations across domains and horizons remain limited. We argue for task-normalized scoring with explicit uncertainty costs and rolling-origin validation. Our study benchmarks domain-agnostic architectures and introduces a lightweight adapter for scarce-feature scenarios.

Self-supervised pretraining on image corpora yields transferable features through contrastive objectives and clustering assignments for representation alignment.

text: Program synthesis from natural language remains challenging due to underspecification, long-range dependencies, and execution constraints. Prior work spans neural language models, symbolic search, and hybrid approaches that trade coverage for precision. Benchmarks increasingly test generalization under unseen APIs and compositional splits. We review core directions before presenting a constrained decoding strategy with verifiable partial semantics. Chen et al. (2021) present Codex, a transformer trained on large corpora of code to perform conditional generation from natural language. Austin et al. (2021) explore synthesis by enumeration with learned guidance and show gains on small program induction tasks. Nye et al. (2021) propose structured search spaces with probabilistic grammars to combine neural proposals and symbolic constraints. Usability studies assess debugging workflows in novice end-user programming contexts. Our experiments combine unit-test feedback with static checks to improve reliability under realistic prompts.

Usability studies assess debugging workflows in novice end-user programming contexts.

text: This section reviews research on domain adaptation for sequence labeling with emphasis on named entity recognition. Practical deployments often face shifts in genre, register, and annotation style across sources. Early approaches relied on feature augmentation and representation learning with linear models. Neural methods emphasize shared encoders that capture transferable structure. Adversarial strategies align latent spaces between source and target distributions. Multi-task formulations leverage auxiliary supervision to stabilize training on scarce targets. Pre-trained language models have reshaped baselines for adaptation tasks. Regularization and calibration remain central for reliable outputs under shift. Several benchmarks provide standardized evaluations across biomedical, news, and social media data. Recent studies also explore controlled curricula and data selection policies for efficiency. We next situate our contribution within these lines of work. Smith et al. (2019) present a multi-task framework for domain adaptation in sequence labeling with encoders and decoders. Lee and Park (2020) explore curriculum schedules that reorder training data to stabilize optimization for large pre-trained models. Nguyen et al. (2021) study calibration procedures for confidence estimation on token classification benchmarks using temperature scaling and ensembles. Rao and Kim (2018) introduce distillation from strong taggers into compact student models. These developments highlight the importance of decoupling task objectives from domain-dependent artifacts. Our method extends shared representation learning with a selective freezing policy guided by target statistics. The approach couples a domain adversary with batch-wise curriculum signals estimated from uncertainty. We design a loss that interpolates between token fidelity and span-level consistency. An online estimator monitors calibration drift to adapt learning rates across layers. We further incorporate a retrieval component that assembles target-like contexts from unlabeled corpora. This component supplies pseudo-parallel sentences used to regularize the encoder. Evaluation covers three settings representing incremental, abrupt, and mixed domain shifts. Results indicate gains on out-of-domain precision without sacrificing recall on sources. Ablations attribute improvements to the interaction between curriculum signals and adversarial alignment. We release code and data splits to facilitate reproducible comparisons in future work.

Rao and Kim (2018) introduce distillation from strong taggers into compact student models.

(2021) study calibration procedures for confidence estimation on token classification benchmarks using temperature scaling and ensembles. Rao and Kim (2018) introduce distillation from strong taggers into compact student models.

text: Spatiotemporal forecasting on road networks requires modeling complex dependencies across space and time. Graph neural networks provide a natural inductive bias for sensor interactions. Early models relied on convolution over grid maps rather than irregular graphs. Attention mechanisms have been adopted to reweight dynamic neighborhoods. Sequence encoders remain crucial to capture multi scale periodicity and sudden events. Exogenous variables such as weather and holidays often modulate traffic patterns. Benchmarks like METR-LA and PEMS-BAY standardize evaluation protocols. Recent work combines physics priors with neural operators for better extrapolation. Zhang et al. (2018) propose a spatiotemporal residual network for citywide crowd flows using convolutional modules. Li et al. (2019) introduce diffusion convolutional recurrent layers to capture sensor interactions in traffic graphs. Yu and Koltun (2020) present graph wavelet networks that expand receptive fields with localized spectral filters for dynamic prediction. Bai et al. (2020) study adaptive adjacency learning via attention for time series forecasting on sensor grids. Together, these ideas motivate flexible architectures that decouple spatial message passing from temporal recurrence. Our approach builds a multi-resolution encoder that aggregates short and long horizons via shared kernels. We incorporate adaptive adjacency learned from data while preserving sparsity for interpretability. A differentiable routing module selects few-hop paths conditioned on congestion signals. Temporal modules blend gated residual convolutions with seasonal embeddings. We introduce a stability penalty that discourages topology drift across batches. This penalty reduces variance when sensors fail or streams are delayed. The training objective integrates pointwise error with distributional calibration terms. We evaluate on four datasets with varying densities and coverage. Results show consistent gains on mean absolute error and continuous ranked probability score. Our ablations reveal that routing and calibration terms deliver complementary benefits. Sensitivity analysis indicates robustness to missing data rates up to twenty percent. We release code, processed data, and logs to foster reproducibility. Limitations include dependence on accurate timestamps and a moderate computational footprint.

(2020) study adaptive adjacency learning via attention for time series forecasting on sensor grids.

(2019) introduce diffusion convolutional recurrent layers to capture sensor interactions in traffic graphs. Yu and Koltun (2020) present graph wavelet networks that expand receptive fields with localized spectral filters for dynamic prediction. Bai et al. (2020) study adaptive adjacency learning via attention for time series forecasting on sensor grids.

text: Recommendation platforms increasingly affect opportunities for users and providers across digital markets. Fairness concerns demand methods that balance accuracy with equitable exposure. Traditional algorithms optimize engagement without considering distributional outcomes for subpopulations. Exposure can mediate downstream utility by shaping clicks and conversions. Measurements of harm must consider not only users but also item suppliers. Ranking models can amplify biases present in historical data. Feedback loops further entrench disparities through position and presentation effects. Recent research introduces constraints and reweighting schemes during training. Post-processing has also been explored to adjust slates after scoring. Online experiments remain expensive and risky in sensitive contexts. Public datasets provide limited demographic annotations for careful assessment. This review situates our contribution among foundational perspectives on algorithmic fairness in recommendation. Burke et al. (2018) articulate multi-stakeholder fairness in recommendation by distinguishing provider and consumer utility with constraints over exposure and relevance. Ekstrand et al. (2021) survey bias sources and measurement protocols including user interfaces, item curation, and offline evaluation pipelines with attention to demographics. Yao and Huang (2017) formalize calibrated fairness objectives that bound disparity metrics across groups while maintaining accuracy on implicit feedback tasks at large scale. These viewpoints underscore the need to define metrics consistent with practical policy goals. Our framework addresses exposure disparity while preserving click probability calibration. We specify a bilevel optimization that tunes slate diversification under probabilistic relevance estimates. The inner model learns a differentiable allocation that approximates position auctions. The outer controller adjusts group-level budget multipliers to meet fairness targets. We derive gradients through the allocation using a smoothed top k operator. The method supports both demographic and provider category constraints. Offline evaluation uses counterfactual estimators with inverse propensity weights. Experiments on two public datasets and one proprietary corpus demonstrate reduced disparity at minimal loss of engagement. Ablations highlight the role of calibration in reaching stable equilibria across groups. We discuss deployment considerations including transparency, privacy, and monitoring for long term drift.

(2021) survey bias sources and measurement protocols including user interfaces, item curation, and offline evaluation pipelines with attention to demographics. Yao and Huang (2017) formalize calibrated fairness objectives that bound disparity metrics across groups while maintaining accuracy on implicit feedback tasks at large scale.

text: Low resource machine translation remains a central challenge for multilingual NLP. Data scarcity hampers both lexical coverage and syntactic robustness. Pre-trained sequence to sequence models provide strong priors but still need supervision. Augmentation methods aim to expand training signals without costly manual annotation. Monolingual corpora are widely available in many target languages. Mining comparable data across domains can further enrich vocabulary. Quality control for synthetic pairs affects downstream accuracy and fluency. Iterative training procedures attempt to refine generated corpora over rounds. We organize related efforts by the source of additional text and the control signals used. Sennrich et al. (2016) introduced back-translation to leverage monolingual target data by generating synthetic sources for additional supervision. Edunov et al. (2018) explored sampling strategies for diverse synthetic corpora that improve generalization over beam outputs. Fadaee and Monz (2018) proposed data augmentation targeting rare words by injecting contextually appropriate examples during training. Koehn and Knowles (2017) surveyed neural machine translation challenges including domain mismatch and low resource conditions. These ideas encourage the use of targeted generation and filtering for rare constructions. Our approach complements synthetic data with segment level paraphrases on the source side. We train a constrained paraphraser that preserves alignable phrases using bilingual dictionaries. A confidence model rejects paraphrases that distort named entities and numbers. We interleave paraphrase batches with back-translation during curriculum stages. The curriculum balances sentence length, rarity, and domain similarity. We also explore constrained decoding that preserves morphological markers in low resource settings. Experiments cover four language pairs with typological diversity and limited data. Results show improvements in BLEU and COMET beyond back-translation alone. Human evaluation indicates better adequacy for infrequent lexical items. Analysis of errors highlights remaining brittleness for highly polysynthetic languages. We provide open source code, scripts, and filtered paraphrase corpora. Future work will study active data selection guided by translation uncertainty.

text: Deep learning has achieved strong performance in medical imaging while raising concerns about transparency. Clinical decision support demands methods that communicate evidence behind predictions. Regulatory frameworks increasingly expect traceable outputs and justifications. Post hoc explanation techniques remain prevalent in deployment settings. Saliency methods visualize input regions contributing to model outputs. Perturbation based analyses estimate causal influence by modifying pixels or segments. Concept based approaches map internal activations to human understandable attributes. Counterfactual generation can reveal model sensitivity to plausible changes. Evaluation protocols must reflect clinical relevance and human factors. Many datasets lack gold annotations for fine grained finding localization. Inter observer variability complicates ground truth for subtle pathologies. Recent work integrates attention maps into training objectives to guide focus. This review organizes methods by the granularity of explanation and reliance on model gradients. Selvaraju et al. (2017) introduced Grad-CAM to visualize discriminative regions by weighting feature maps with class gradients. Lundberg and Lee (2017) developed SHAP values that unify additive attribution methods under a game theoretic framework. Ribeiro et al. (2016) proposed LIME to fit local surrogate models around predictions for interpretable explanations. Tjoa and Guan (2020) reviewed explainable AI in healthcare across modalities and clinical tasks with identified evaluation gaps. Despite popularity, validity of explanations depends on faithfulness to model behavior. We adopt perturbation tests that measure deletion and insertion curves on medical images. Our method trains a sparse concept bottleneck guided by radiologist provided attributes. The bottleneck constrains downstream classifiers while preserving predictive power. We propose a calibration loss that aligns concept activations with uncertainty estimates. Human studies assess usefulness for triage and error detection. Experiments span chest radiographs and retinal fundus photographs. Results indicate improved faithfulness without degrading diagnostic accuracy. We discuss limitations including annotation cost and domain shift across scanners.

(2016) proposed LIME to fit local surrogate models around predictions for interpretable explanations. text: Research on fairness in language technologies has expanded as models affect high-stakes decisions. Definitions of fairness vary, and operationalizations often depend on task context and available labels. Our work examines metric alignment for toxicity classification in multilingual corpora. Smith (2018) introduced a demographic parity regularizer for neural classifiers. Lee and Park (2019) analyzed calibration gaps across social attributes in sentiment models. Chen and colleagues (2020) proposed counterfactual data augmentation to mitigate spurious correlations. Garcia and coauthors (2021) studied representation debiasing with adversarial gradients in multilingual settings. Open issues persist. Yet existing evaluations rarely consider cross-lingual shifts in attribute distributions. We propose a calibration-aware thresholding method coupled with language-adaptive priors. We empirically validate the approach on three datasets and report robust gains with reduced disparity.

text: Graph representation learning has transformed molecular property prediction by modeling bonds and atoms as structured data. Despite progress, models still struggle with transfer across distributional shifts in scaffold splits. We target uncertainty-aware message passing to improve reliability on out-of-domain molecules. Prior literature on graph neural architectures provides a foundation for our design. Kipf and Welling (2017) introduced graph convolutional layers for semi-supervised classification. Hamilton and colleagues (2017) proposed inductive neighborhood aggregation for large-scale graphs. Velickovic and collaborators (2018) developed attention mechanisms to weight edges during message passing. Xu and coauthors (2019) analyzed expressive power with a Weisfeiler–Lehman perspective on graph kernels. Open questions. Building on these insights, we integrate calibrated ensembling with subgraph pooling for robust inference. Our evaluation covers five benchmarks with ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each component.

text: Accurate short-term renewable energy forecasts are critical for balancing supply and demand on modern grids. Traditional pipelines often overlook uncertainty and spatiotemporal coupling between weather drivers and demand response. We study hierarchical probabilistic modeling that couples exogenous covariates with turbine-level signals. Foundational advances in time series forecasting are relevant to our approach. Hyndman and Khandakar (2008) automated ARIMA selection using unit root tests and information criteria. Salinas and colleagues (2020) applied deep state space models for probabilistic forecasting at scale. Li and coauthors (2019) introduced temporal convolutional networks to capture long-range dependencies. Zerveas and collaborators (2021) pretrained transformers on time series with masking. Despite these developments, deployment requires calibrated intervals and robust adaptation to regime shifts. We introduce a distributional conformal forecaster with context gating for covariate shift mitigation. Experiments on wind farm datasets demonstrate improved reliability under extreme weather variability.

text: Effective writing feedback at scale remains a central challenge for online and hybrid learning environments. Instructors seek systems that provide formative guidance while preserving transparency and fairness. We focus on sentence-level feedback for argumentative structure using rubric-aligned attributes. Prior research on automated assessment offers complementary perspectives. Ng and collaborators (2014) built MOOC-scale grading pipelines using linguistic features. Taghipour and Ng (2016) used neural networks for automated essay scoring. Uto and Ueno (2022) studied criterion-specific scoring with attention mechanisms. Kumar and colleagues (2020) investigated feedback generation using transformers trained on revision histories. Open rubrics complicate evaluation. Across courses. Our contribution is a constrained generation framework that connects explanations to rubric criteria through structured prompts.

text: Robots operating in homes and workplaces must interpret underspecified instructions and resolve ambiguities through dialogue. Prior work highlights the importance of shared representations that couple language with perception and action. We address interactive task planning under partial observability with learned belief updates. Fong and colleagues (2003) surveyed methods for collaborative control between humans and robots. Thomason and coauthors (2015) collected instruction-following dialogs for grounded language understanding. Tellex and collaborators (2011) modeled spatial language with probabilistic grounding for mobile manipulation. Shridhar and Hsu (2018) introduced interactive object retrieval in cluttered scenes using natural language. Despite progress, end users still experience breakdowns when goals change mid-conversation or objects are occluded. Our approach leverages contingent querying policies derived from uncertainty over candidate referents. We present a dataset of corrective interactions emphasizing pragmatic constraints and temporal commitments. Experiments show improved success rates and reduced clarification turns across simulated and physical settings.

text: This section reviews research on automatic evaluation for document summarization. Our focus is on metrics and training signals that align with human judgments. Early work emphasized n-gram overlap and heuristic penalties. Neural metrics have recently challenged this paradigm with learned representations. Reinforcement learning objectives have also been investigated for training summarizers. Beyond accuracy, several studies examine robustness and domain sensitivity. We organize the discussion by learning signal, model coupling, and evaluation setting. Within learning signals, reward shaping has received particular attention. Prior studies addressing relevance optimization are illustrative. Smith (2019) presented an ROUGE-aligned objective for abstractive summarization with reinforcement learning. Johnson and Lee (2020) described a content selection module trained on news corpora. Kim et al. (2021) optimized factuality by calibrating entailment scores against reference highlights. Zhao (2022) explored compression ratios using pointer-generator hybrids on multi-domain datasets. Nguyen (2022) reported gains on narratives. Despite their diversity, these approaches raise open questions about generalization. Model-coupled metrics risk overfitting to architectural biases. Reference-free metrics promise flexibility but can be unstable across topics. Domain shift remains a major source of error in long-form summarizers. Few works examine calibration when references are noisy or incomplete. Our study targets stability through task-normalized rewards and controllable sampling. We also benchmark variance under constrained compute budgets. The results highlight trade-offs between faithfulness and coverage in practical settings.

Zhao (2022) explored compression ratios using pointer-generator hybrids on multi-domain datasets. Nguyen (2022) reported gains on narratives.

text: Personalization in federated learning seeks to reconcile global coordination with local heterogeneity. We investigate approaches that retain privacy while improving client-specific accuracy. Prior surveys identify trade-offs between statistical disparity and communication cost. Our discussion focuses on objective design and aggregation strategies. Regularization-based methods constrain drift from a shared representation. Multi-task formulations model divergence through structured priors over parameters. Clustering and meta-learning enable specialization without full isolation. We review representative techniques that ground our contributions. The literature spans optimization algorithms and generalization analyses. McMahan et al. (2017) formulated federated averaging for distributed optimization under privacy constraints. Smith et al. (2018) studied personalization layers to adapt global models to clients. Arivazhagan et al. (2019) proposed clustered aggregation to exploit client similarity. Fallah et al. (2020) introduced meta-learning procedures for rapid personalization during deployment. Chen (2021) analyzed gradient conflicts empirically. These advances motivate algorithms that interpolate between global and local models. Yet evaluation protocols often obscure fairness across client populations. Benchmarks rarely include non-iid dynamics with temporal concept shift. Privacy budgets complicate comparisons when noise is injected adaptively. We propose a bilevel objective with decoupled transport and calibration terms. Our framework unifies personalization layers with client-conditioned aggregation. Experiments on vision and language datasets demonstrate consistent per-client gains. Analysis reveals stability improvements under distribution drift and budget constraints.

text: Multimodal emotion recognition aims to infer affective states from coordinated signals. Applications span healthcare triage, education feedback, and customer support. Progress has accelerated with deep architectures that capture temporal context. Yet robustness to domain and demographic variation remains an open issue. Datasets differ in label structure, speaker distribution, and recording conditions. Training must balance representation power with cross-modal reliability. We synthesize prior work along axes of fusion, alignment, and discourse modeling. Early fusion strategies learn joint embeddings before temporal modeling. Late fusion combines modality-specific decisions into a consensus score. Research on alignment addresses asynchrony and missing streams across modalities. Poria et al. (2017) fused text, audio, and video via tensor representations for affect. Zadeh et al. (2018) introduced the CMU-MOSEI dataset with diverse sentiment annotations. Tsai et al. (2019) developed multimodal transformer networks that learn cross-modal alignments. Hazarika et al. (2020) modeled conversational dependencies with memory mechanisms. Sun (2021) examined label noise in corpora. These efforts set the stage for personalized adaptation strategies. Spurious correlations between lexical cues and prosody can mislead classifiers. Calibrated uncertainty estimates help identify unreliable modalities at test time. Our approach uses causal regularization to discourage shortcut features. We also propose a curriculum that perturbs modality availability during training. Experiments on three benchmarks show gains in robustness and fairness. Error analysis reveals improvements on challenging conversational segments.

Sun (2021) examined label noise in corpora.

text: Knowledge graph completion predicts missing facts by leveraging observed triples. Accuracy depends on modeling multi-relational structure efficiently and robustly. Early approaches rely on linear factorization and path-based reasoning. Neural models introduce nonlinear interactions and parameter sharing across relations. Regularization strategies like adversarial sampling reduce overconfidence on easy negatives. Inductive settings test the ability to generalize to unseen entities. We review representative embedding families and their learning objectives. Translational models define distance-based scoring functions over triples. Semantic matching models learn multiplicative interactions between entities and relations. Convolutional architectures capture local patterns in composed representations. Bordes et al. (2013) introduced translational embeddings to model relational structure. Yang et al. (2015) framed link prediction as tensor factorization with compositional operators. Dettmers et al. (2018) used convolutional filters to capture feature interactions. Sun et al. (2019) decomposed embeddings into components to represent multigranular semantics. Vashishth et al. (2020) integrated graph attention mechanisms. While effective, these models often struggle with long-tail relations. Recent work explores rule-guided training to inject logical constraints. Temporal variants model time-stamped facts and evolving relations. Few-shot methods address sparse entities with meta-learning objectives. Our method unifies embedding and rule signals through constrained optimization. We propose calibration techniques to mitigate spurious correlations across relations. Experiments show consistent gains on transductive and inductive benchmarks.

(2020) integrated graph attention mechanisms.

text: Ensuring fairness in language classification requires measuring and mitigating disparities across groups. Downstream harms can arise from biased data, modeling choices, and deployment contexts. Recent benchmarks report large performance gaps on minority dialects and identities. Metrics such as equalized odds and calibration inform trade-off analyses. Contextualized encoders may propagate historical stereotypes through training corpora. Data curation practices and annotation guidelines substantially affect bias estimates. Domain adaptation techniques can shift representations toward robust invariants. We consider prior research that informs our evaluation and modeling choices. Our review covers measurement, datasets, and algorithmic interventions. We highlight areas where current practice diverges from policy expectations. Bolukbasi et al. (2016) documented gender associations in word embeddings via analogy tests. Zhao et al. (2018) measured bias amplification in coreference resolution datasets and systems. Sun et al. (2019) surveyed mitigation strategies across pre-processing and training interventions. Hendricks et al. (2018) investigated captioning models for gendered error patterns. Hashimoto (2020) analyzed risk across subpopulations. Open questions persist about subgroup definitions and intersectional coverage. Reporting standards vary widely across tasks and datasets. Robustness to distribution shift is often confounded with fairness improvements. We propose evaluation protocols that separate representation bias from label bias. Our method introduces counterfactual data augmentation with calibrated constraints. Experiments on sentiment and toxicity classification demonstrate improved parity. Qualitative analysis shows fewer stereotype-triggered errors on stress tests.

Hashimoto (2020) analyzed risk across subpopulations.

text: Traffic prediction in urban networks is a long standing problem with significant societal impact. Recent advances in graph learning have reshaped the modeling of spatial dependencies in sensor grids. Early approaches relied on vector autoregression and Kalman filtering with limited expressivity. Deep architectures introduced convolution over irregular graphs to encode topology aware interactions. Attention mechanisms further enabled dynamic weighting across roads and time steps. External signals such as weather and events are often integrated as covariates in multi source models. Despite growth of benchmarks, replication and comparative evaluation remain challenging across cities. Li et al. (2018) introduced diffusion convolution for traffic forecasting using sensor graphs. Seo et al. (2018) presented a recurrent graph convolutional architecture for dynamic road networks. Guo et al. (2019) proposed attention based spatial-temporal blocks for multi-horizon prediction. Wu et al. (2019) developed graph wavenet to capture long-range dependencies. Chen et al. (2020) explored data denoising strategies for urban flow. Zhang et al. (2020) investigated transfer learning. Nevertheless, most studies evaluate on a narrow set of metropolitan areas with homogeneous sensor layouts. Transferability to sparse or partially observed networks is rarely analyzed in a principled manner. Robustness to sensor outages and distribution shifts has only begun to receive attention in open datasets. Data augmentation through simulated traffic perturbations has been explored but lacks consensus protocols. A key open question is how to represent temporal context that varies across days and seasons. Some works use explicit periodic encodings while others infer seasonality through long sequences. The interplay between model capacity and training data volume is under documented in current literature. Few papers release full preprocessing pipelines that reproduce reported results across software versions. Our study addresses these gaps by proposing a standardized evaluation suite with cross city splits. We introduce a modular architecture that separates topology encoding from temporal dynamics. The design allows fair ablations across spatial operators and sequence encoders under common settings. We compile heterogeneous sensors and metadata into a unified schema with consistent coordinate frames. The suite includes protocols for missing data imputation and streaming evaluation over rolling horizons. We report error distributions in addition to mean metrics to characterize reliability under perturbations. Finally, we discuss licensing and privacy constraints that shape responsible deployment in public agencies. We release code, data loaders, and reproducible scripts to facilitate community progress on this task.

text: Abstractive summarization aims to condense documents while preserving core meaning in fluent language. Multilingual contexts add constraints related to typology, morphology, and resource imbalance. Prior work has studied training with parallel corpora or pivoting through English targets. Large pretrained encoders and decoders have improved both fluency and factuality on benchmark suites. Nevertheless, crosslingual transfer still suffers when source and target diverge in word order or script. Noisy supervision from web scale data complicates evaluation by introducing contamination and overlap. Recent datasets offer more diverse domain coverage but remain skewed toward high resource languages. Performance gaps persist for agglutinative and morphologically rich families with limited annotations. Content selection is particularly sensitive to cultural references and named entities across languages. Evaluation metrics for multilingual settings lag behind progress in architecture design and pretraining. Faithfulness remains an open challenge under domain shift and long form reporting. Rush et al. (2015) presented a neural attention model for abstractive summarization. See et al. (2017) introduced pointer-generator networks with coverage to reduce repetition. Narayan et al. (2018) released the XSum dataset emphasizing extreme summarization. Liu and Lapata (2019) proposed a transformer based encoder decoder for long inputs. Lewis et al. (2020) built BART with denoising pretraining for text generation. Zhang et al. (2020) studied crosslingual transfer approaches. These advances motivate new training recipes that couple multilingual encoders with language adaptive modules. We consider both full finetuning and parameter efficient strategies for deployment at scale. Our problem formulation targets document level summarization with constrained generation length across scripts. The approach integrates shared subword vocabularies while preserving language specific position embeddings. We explore data selection policies that balance coverage against duplication across translated sources. The training schedule alternates high resource batches with sampled low resource episodes to reduce bias. We introduce consistency regularization to stabilize outputs under backtranslation perturbations. For evaluation, we report MT aware metrics and human judgments on fluency and adequacy. We release a new testbed with carefully deduplicated sources spanning news, government, and encyclopedia content. Error analysis highlights entity handling, negation, and quotation as dominant sources of factual drift. The proposed system offers practical tradeoffs between quality and compute for multilingual deployments. We believe the framework provides a stronger baseline for future research on inclusive summarization.

text: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw data. Healthcare data are fragmented across institutions with strict privacy and governance requirements. Direct data pooling is often infeasible due to regulation and contractual limitations. Distributed training offers a path to leverage wider populations while maintaining local control. Clinical prediction models must address label shift and covariate heterogeneity across sites. Communication budgets and intermittent connectivity complicate synchronous optimization on hospital networks. Practical deployments need mechanisms for secure coordination and fault tolerance under real workloads. Personal health information raises heightened risks of reidentification from gradients or model updates. Auditing and accountability frameworks are required to trace and assess contributions of participating nodes. Domain shift between community clinics and tertiary centers can degrade generalization substantially. Model calibration is critical for decision support tools subject to clinical oversight. Recent studies propose personalization layers to adapt global models to local patient distributions. Other lines of work explore clustered aggregation to account for structural differences between clients. Model compression techniques reduce bandwidth costs while preserving accuracy through quantization and sparsity. Differential privacy has been adapted to federated settings with varying impact on utility. Secure aggregation protocols can mitigate inference risks but add computational overhead. Open datasets remain limited, and many reports rely on proprietary hospital networks. Reproducibility is thus challenging, especially when client sampling is nonstationary over time. McMahan et al. (2017) formalized federated averaging as a communication efficient optimizer. Kairouz et al. (2021) surveyed challenges and open problems in federated learning. Li et al. (2020) studied systems heterogeneity and fairness constraints across clients. Geyer et al. (2017) analyzed differential privacy in distributed training. Bonawitz et al. (2019) described secure aggregation protocols for large scale deployments. Hard et al. (2018) reported mobile keyboard applications in production. In clinical contexts, these ingredients must be integrated with auditing and consent management. We focus on realistic participation patterns and partial availability of labels across silos. Our method couples variance reduced updates with stratified client selection tuned to clinical cohorts. The evaluation includes cross site calibration, subgroup performance, and robustness to node dropout. We release tooling for simulation of participation logs derived from deidentified operational traces.
