text: Self-supervised learning on graphs reduces dependence on labels for representation quality. Contrastive objectives have become a dominant paradigm for unsupervised graph encoders. The choice of augmentations and semantic consistency remains an open design question. Empirical benchmarks show sensitivity to batch size, view diversity, and neighborhood bias. Velickovic and colleagues (2019) introduced Deep Graph Infomax to maximize mutual information globally. You and colleagues (2020) proposed augmentations for graph contrastive learning with node dropping. Hassani and Ahmadi (2020) explored multi-scale views to capture structural patterns. Qiu and colleagues (2020) used random propagation to build efficient self-supervision signals. Thakoor and colleagues (2021) analyzed properties of augmentations. Despite rapid progress, common evaluation pipelines vary in splits and downstream heads. Negative sampling strategies also interact with structural sparsity in nontrivial ways. We study augmentation invariances with a unified framework that controls topology perturbations. Our analysis isolates which perturbations align with task-relevant predictive substructures. The framework includes standardized splits and multi-run reporting for reliable comparisons. Results highlight stability regions where contrast suffices and regimes favoring reconstruction.

text: Multilingual machine translation leverages shared parameters to transfer knowledge across languages. Data imbalance and typological diversity pose challenges for generalization and fairness. Research explores capacity scaling, vocabulary design, and objective choices for large models. Transfer efficiency depends on encoder representations and routing within decoder layers. Johnson and colleagues (2017) demonstrated zero-shot translation with a shared multilingual encoder-decoder. Arivazhagan and colleagues (2019) scaled many-to-many models with language-specific capacity. Conneau and colleagues (2020) introduced XLM-R based on masked multilingual pretraining. Chi and colleagues (2021) explored alignment objectives for cross-lingual transfer in encoders. Tang and colleagues (2021) studied vocabulary sharing and capacity allocation trade-offs empirically. Despite high aggregate metrics, low-resource directions often lag substantially behind. Reporting practices frequently omit per-language calibration or robustness to domain shift. We examine architectural and data factors using controlled many-to-many settings. Our experiments quantify transfer asymmetries and their relationship to lexical overlap. The study includes ablations on routing, vocabulary, and sampling strategies. Findings inform principled capacity allocation for equitable multilingual performance.

text: Detecting anomalies in multivariate time series is central to monitoring critical systems. Real deployments confront nonstationarity, missing data, and strict latency budgets. Methods range from prediction errors to density estimation and discriminative scoring. Evaluation is complicated by label sparsity and heterogeneous event durations. Public benchmarks often contain annotation biases that inflate apparent performance. Practitioners also require interpretability to triage alerts and plan remediation steps. Hundman and colleagues (2018) proposed LSTM-based detectors for spacecraft telemetry anomalies. Ren and colleagues (2019) introduced Random Cut Forest for streaming multivariate detection. Xu and colleagues (2018) modeled interdependencies with Graphical Lasso based detectors. Audibert and colleagues (2020) presented USAD using autoencoders for reconstruction-based scoring. Tuli and colleagues (2022) explored ensembles to improve detection stability on benchmarks. Despite diverse models, reporting frequently ignores delay penalties and alert consolidation. Threshold selection is rarely validated out-of-sample, causing brittle behavior under drift. We contribute a pipeline that standardizes windows, delays, and aggregation across datasets. The pipeline reveals realistic trade-offs between sensitivity, timeliness, and alert volume.

Xu and colleagues (2018) modeled interdependencies with Graphical Lasso based detectors. Audibert and colleagues (2020) presented USAD using autoencoders for reconstruction-based scoring. Tuli and colleagues (2022) explored ensembles to improve detection stability on benchmarks.

Ren and colleagues (2019) introduced Random Cut Forest for streaming multivariate detection. Xu and colleagues (2018) modeled interdependencies with Graphical Lasso based detectors. Audibert and colleagues (2020) presented USAD using autoencoders for reconstruction-based scoring. Tuli and colleagues (2022) explored ensembles to improve detection stability on benchmarks.

text: Research on biomedical named entity recognition has expanded with the availability of large pretrained encoders. Early systems relied on conditional random fields with handcrafted features and dictionary lookups. Neural models replaced feature engineering by leveraging character CNNs and word embeddings. Contextualized encoders such as BioBERT improved tagging quality on multiple corpora. Domain adaptation remains challenging due to annotation mismatch and subdomain terminology drift. Adversarial training aligns representations between labeled source and unlabeled target sets. Self training and pseudo labeling have been used to harvest additional supervision from raw text. Contrastive objectives reduce overfitting by dispersing entity and nonentity representations. Multi task setups incorporate auxiliary objectives to regularize encoders across datasets. Cross corpus calibration mitigates inconsistent boundary conventions between resources. Smith et al. (2018) introduced a domain discriminator for biomedical tagging with encoders and gradient reversal. Lee and Park (2019) proposed meta representation normalization to stabilize transfer across hospital note collections. Gururangan et al. (2020) explored task adaptive pretraining for improved biomedical NER, and Beltagy et al. (2019) released SciBERT with scientific vocabulary and curated corpora. Pretraining on in domain corpora complements adaptation by providing better tokenization and entity morphology coverage. Data selection strategies choose sentences that express dense terminology to maximize transfer gains. Span classification objectives can be combined with boundary regression to handle discontinuous mentions. Uncertainty estimation enables selective annotation and improves calibration in noisy corpora. Few shot calibration of output layers reduces catastrophic forgetting during incremental domain updates. Recent work investigates parameter efficient tuning through adapters and low rank factors. These approaches limit drift while enabling rapid specialization to new subfields. Cross lingual biomedical tagging introduces additional variance from translation artifacts and mismatched label sets. Alignment using multilingual encoders and synonym dictionaries remains an open direction. Our study explores semi supervised adaptation under realistic cross hospital shifts with limited labeled target data. We formalize a setting that reflects evolving electronic health record systems and distinct provider conventions. The method integrates confidence aware pseudo labeling with subword regularization and adapter routing. We report results on multiple public NER corpora and two de identified clinical datasets. Evaluation follows established metrics and further assesses span boundary errors. An ablation isolates the contributions of each adaptation component to overall quality. The findings indicate robust performance gains under both moderate and severe shift scenarios.

(2020) explored task adaptive pretraining for improved biomedical NER, and Beltagy et al. (2019) released SciBERT with scientific vocabulary and curated corpora.

text: Visual question answering evaluates joint understanding of images and natural language queries. Early models fused CNN features with RNN encodings of questions. Attention mechanisms allowed models to focus on regions relevant to the query. Bilinear pooling captured high order interactions between modalities. Pretrained vision transformers and large language models have shifted prevailing practice. Multimodal pretraining on web scale pairs improves data efficiency and robustness. Contrastive objectives align images and captions in a shared embedding space. Generative objectives train decoders to predict masked tokens conditioned on visual features. Decoder only architectures can answer questions by conditioning on a sequence of visual tokens. Knowledge intensive questions benefit from retrieval augmented generation. Datasets differ widely in compositional structure and bias patterns that models can exploit. Benchmarks such as VQAv2 and GQA require consistent reasoning across varied layouts. Spurious correlations remain a concern when answer distributions are skewed. Calibration of free form outputs is critical for real world deployment. Anderson et al. (2018) presented bottom up attention features that became standard inputs for VQA encoders. Lu et al. (2019) introduced ViLBERT with two streams and co attention. Tan and Bansal (2019) proposed LXMERT, Radford et al. (2021) trained CLIP on image text pairs, and Jia et al. (2021) showed scaling of contrastive data yields benefits. While these models excel on benchmarks, real world questions often require external knowledge and temporal grounding. Methods that integrate scene graphs report mixed gains depending on dataset structure. Program induction approaches produce interpretable reasoning but suffer from annotation cost. Recent work explores lightweight adapters to reuse large checkpoints for downstream tasks. Answer calibration via temperature scaling and label smoothing can reduce overconfidence. Multimodal rationales offer a way to audit decisions for safety reviews. Our approach studies retrieval enhanced decoding with cached captions and visual region text. We design a training schedule that balances generative and contrastive updates. Experiments evaluate generalization under synthetic distribution shift and partial occlusion. We assess performance on long tail answer categories and rare object combinations. The analysis measures sensitivity to distractor captions injected at test time. Results demonstrate improved robustness without sacrificing accuracy on standard benchmarks.

text: Program synthesis from natural language seeks to map instructions to executable code. Early systems relied on rule based semantic parsers with domain specific languages. Neural sequence models enabled end to end training on paired utterances and code. Copy mechanisms improved fidelity by handling identifiers and literals. Pretrained encoders provide strong priors on syntax and library usage. Structural decoders enforce well formedness by generating abstract syntax trees. Constrained decoding further prunes invalid continuations using grammar rules. Type guidance reduces runtime errors by conditioning generation on checker feedback. Few shot prompting with large language models has accelerated progress on small benchmarks. Data contamination and memorization complicate evaluation of generalization. Robustness to paraphrase and to unseen libraries remains an open challenge. Execution guided search helps correct local errors by validating partial programs. Curriculum learning shapes exploration by ordering tasks by complexity. Augmentation methods synthesize varied intent paraphrases to expand training distributions. Retrieval augments decoders with example programs and relevant API snippets. Benchmarks differ widely in compositional complexity and domain granularity. Yin and Neubig (2017) described a tree structured decoder that generates abstract syntax trees for code generation. Dong and Lapata (2016) introduced a sequence to tree framework for semantic parsing of instructions. Chen et al. (2021) released CodeXGLUE resources, and Austin et al. (2021) documented instruction tuned program synthesis capabilities in large models for multiple tasks. Despite progress, scarce supervision in specialized domains constrains deployment. Mining unpaired repositories requires methods that align code with noisy descriptions. We investigate retrieval augmented constrained decoding with type feedback. The retriever indexes code snippets and docstrings using dual encoders. During inference the decoder conditions on retrieved exemplars via attention and prefix features. A verifier executes candidates and filters those violating tests or type contracts. Training leverages offline demonstrations and online edits from verification signals. We evaluate on realistic repositories featuring heterogeneous style and partial docstrings. Metrics report exact match, functional accuracy, and synthesis speed. Analysis exposes error categories related to library calls and stateful side effects.

(2021) documented instruction tuned program synthesis capabilities in large models for multiple tasks.

(2021) documented instruction tuned program synthesis capabilities in large models for multiple tasks.

text: Neural machine translation has shifted from recurrent models to transformers with superior accuracy and speed. Autoregressive decoding yields high quality outputs but imposes latency proportional to sequence length. Non autoregressive translation targets lower latency by predicting tokens in parallel. Early models suffered from multimodality and over deletion. Iterative refinement partially mitigates these issues by updating tokens across passes. CTC based approaches align latent emissions with target positions. Latent variable modeling captures alternative hypotheses under limited context. Knowledge distillation from autoregressive teachers remains a standard practice. Length prediction plays a central role in quality stability. Position representations affect reordering capability and fertility modeling. Lexical constraints are important for domain specificity and post editing workflows. Recent work explores semi autoregressive blocks as a tradeoff between quality and speed. Evaluation requires attention to both accuracy and latency across device budgets. Domain shift and morphological richness remain difficult for parallel decoding. Gu et al. (2018) proposed fertility based non autoregressive translation to generate tokens in parallel. Lee et al. (2018) tested iterative refinement with denoising objectives for improved token updates. Ghazvininejad et al. (2019) presented Mask Predict, Libovický and Helcl (2018) explored CTC alignment for translation, and Kim and Rush (2016) discussed sequence-level knowledge distillation for models. Despite these advances, non autoregressive systems still trail strong baselines on adequacy for low resource pairs. Recent studies introduce adaptive masks to target uncertain tokens first. Other work leverages bilingual lexicons to constrain decoding of rare words. We study a planner that predicts reorderings and chunk boundaries before token generation. The planner conditions on source syntax and lexical cues to guide iterations. A lightweight consistency regularizer reduces oscillations across refinement steps. Our training recipe avoids reliance on teacher outputs by mixing raw and distilled targets. Experiments span morphologically rich languages and diverse sentence lengths. Metrics include BLEU, COMET, latency, and energy usage. Ablations analyze the effect of planner granularity and mask schedules. Human evaluation assesses fluency, adequacy, and post editing effort. The method narrows the quality gap while preserving competitive speedups.

text: Recommender systems mediate exposure and opportunity across users and items. Accuracy metrics alone can mask systematic disparities. Fairness objectives seek to regulate exposure, relevance, and resource allocation. Provider side constraints arise in marketplaces where inventory is limited. User side parity concerns include equalized accuracy and calibration. Multi stakeholder optimization raises questions about tradeoffs and incentives. Feedback loops can amplify bias through popularity reinforcement. Causal modeling offers tools to disentangle confounding from intervention effects. Logging policies and partial observability complicate counterfactual estimation. Offline evaluation requires careful reweighting to avoid selection bias. Exposure constraints can be integrated via deterministic or stochastic schedulers. Regularizers on ranking scores can target disparity without explicit scheduling. Uncertainty aware bandits can allocate exploration to underserved groups. Transparency mechanisms help auditors interpret the effect of constraints. Burke (2017) articulated multi stakeholder recommendation objectives with competing utilities. Singh and Joachims (2018) defined fairness of exposure in rankings for providers. Biega et al. (2018) studied equity of attention, Mehrotra et al. (2018) analyzed intervention policies in bandit recommenders, and Ekstrand et al. (2022) surveyed fairness considerations in user centered evaluation across multiple application domains. Metrics proposed in this literature reflect diverse goals and constraints. Some address provider visibility while others consider user harms from uneven quality. Optimizing these objectives online presents stability and regret challenges. Safe exploration becomes critical when interventions affect livelihoods. We investigate a constrained learning to rank framework with uncertainty budgets. The method estimates group exposure with inverse propensity corrections. A primal dual optimizer enforces constraints while maximizing utility estimates. We introduce variance penalties to reduce over allocation to volatile items. Our dataset includes marketplace, music, and news domains with group annotations. Evaluation measures exposure disparity, ranking quality, and sensitivity to shifts. We report efficiency and perform significance testing against competitive baselines. Findings show controllable tradeoffs and reduced disparity variance over time.

(2022) surveyed fairness considerations in user centered evaluation across multiple application domains.

(2022) surveyed fairness considerations in user centered evaluation across multiple application domains.

text: Cross-lingual information retrieval seeks to retrieve relevant documents across language boundaries using shared representations. Early approaches relied on dictionaries and statistical translation models that often propagated noise in low-resource settings. Neural alignment techniques have since capitalized on multilingual corpora to learn cross-language spaces for words and sentences. Pivot-based strategies further reduce annotation demands by exploiting intermediate languages during training. Shared subword vocabularies enable parameter sharing that benefits morphologically rich languages and improves generalization. Domain adaptation remains challenging when target-language content deviates substantially from the corpora used to train embeddings. Xu et al. (2018) report bilingual lexicon induction with graph propagation over seed dictionaries. Litschko et al. (2019) study subword-based nearest neighbor retrieval for low-resource pairs using character n-grams. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) present a massively multilingual sentence embedding space aligned through parallel data. Conneau et al. (2020) release XLM-R trained on CommonCrawl for cross-lingual transfer tasks. These lines of work leave open how retrieval signals interact with domain shifts in web collections. Our study examines query expansion guided by multilingual encoders under constrained supervision. We quantify robustness to content drift using evaluations on news, patents, and community forums. The method integrates reranking with calibrated confidence to mitigate negative transfer across language families. We release reproducible benchmarks that isolate the effects of alignment quality and corpus topology. The proposed design emphasizes low computational overhead to encourage practical deployment in resource-constrained environments.

text: Recommender systems increasingly leverage graph structure to capture complex dependencies among users and items. Message passing architectures permit the aggregation of multi-hop signals that are difficult to encode with traditional matrix factorization. Yet oversmoothing and popularity bias remain central obstacles to practical deployment at scale. Recent work studies how propagation depth and normalization interact with sparse feedback patterns in implicit data. Side information such as text and images can regularize learned embeddings when interactions are scarce. Temporal dynamics further introduce non-stationarity that challenges static graph models during training and evaluation. Negative sampling strategies influence both stability and fairness by shaping exposure to candidate items. Wang et al. (2019) introduce neural graph collaborative filtering to propagate user–item signals along high-order connections. He et al. (2020) propose LightGCN by removing feature transformations and nonlinearities in the propagation layers. Ying et al. (2018) design PinSage to scale random-walk-based convolutions for billion-node recommendation graphs. Sun et al. (2019) present BERT4Rec with bidirectional self-attention for sequences. These approaches raise questions about calibration and uncertainty in top-k selection for long-tail content. Our framework introduces debiased propagation with adaptive residuals to mitigate amplification of confounding structure. We incorporate counterfactual estimation to adjust gradients using logged exposure signals from production systems. Extensive experiments on public and proprietary datasets demonstrate consistent gains across cold-start and warm-start regimes. We provide an open-source toolkit to reproduce results and facilitate benchmarking under realistic serving constraints.

text: Fairness in natural language processing concerns representational and allocational harms arising from data and models. Pretrained encoders can encode social biases that manifest in downstream predictions under common training regimes. Prior work has examined metrics that quantify group disparities as well as individual-level counterfactual evaluations. Mitigation techniques range from data balancing to adversarial learning and regularization in objective functions. These methods vary in their computational cost and their impact on utility across tasks. Bolukbasi et al. (2016) document gender stereotypes in word embeddings and propose a post hoc debiasing projection. Caliskan et al. (2017) quantify associations in distributional semantics using the Word Embedding Association Test. Zhao et al. (2018) show systematic gender bias in coreference systems trained on crowd-sourced annotations. Rudinger et al. (2018) report occupation biases in template evaluations. Despite extensive documentation of bias phenomena, consensus on robust evaluation protocols remains elusive. Existing benchmarks often entangle linguistic difficulty with demographic attributes, confounding diagnosis and comparison of methods. We target semantic textual similarity and natural language inference with a unified testing suite for fairness. Our approach introduces structured counterfactuals generated with demographic attribute perturbations and syntactic controls. We couple this with calibration-aware training to avoid hiding disparities behind threshold effects. The experimental design emphasizes generalization across domains and demographic slices with strict data separation. We release code, templates, and detailed measurement guidelines to support reproducible and transparent assessments.

text: Time series anomaly detection is essential for ensuring reliability in monitoring and cyber-physical systems. Models must flag rare deviations promptly while maintaining low false positive rates in nonstationary environments. Traditional thresholding on residuals suffers when seasonality and regime shifts dominate the signal. Probabilistic generative models estimate uncertainty but can be brittle under covariate drift and missing data. Deep architectures provide capacity for multivariate dependencies, yet training stability and interpretability remain open problems. Benchmarks often mix synthetic and operational traces, complicating cross-domain comparison of methods and metrics. Hundman et al. (2018) propose LSTM-based forecasts with error clustering for spacecraft telemetry anomalies. Audibert et al. (2020) introduce USAD, a self-supervised autoencoder ensemble with adversarial training for efficiency. Su et al. (2019) present OmniAnomaly using stochastic recurrent networks for multivariate sensor streams. Xu et al. (2018) release Telemanom with convolutional encoders tailored to operational constraints datasets. These contributions highlight the need for protocols that disentangle change types from noise characteristics. We formalize anomaly taxonomies grounded in system failure modes and temporal granularity. Our approach combines spectral residual preprocessing with conformal prediction to calibrate scores over time. We derive guarantees for conditional coverage under covariate shift using sliding-window exchangeability tests. Extensive experiments on public telemetry and proprietary logs show improved precision at fixed recall. We discuss deployment considerations including warm starts, feedback loops, and adaptive threshold maintenance.

(2018) release Telemanom with convolutional encoders tailored to operational constraints datasets.

text: Data-to-text generation converts structured inputs into fluent natural language suitable for decision support. Systems must balance factual accuracy, content planning, and stylistic variation to meet user needs. Neural architectures have improved fluency yet struggle with coverage and faithfulness without careful constraints. Recent benchmarks emphasize long-form reports and domain adaptation from limited examples. Controllability mechanisms are gaining importance for transparency and human-in-the-loop editing workflows. Prior research has explored planning, alignment, and decoding strategies to structure content. Wiseman et al. (2017) establish neural data-to-document modeling with attention over records and content selection. Puduppully et al. (2019) propose entity-aware planning to maintain coherence when describing evolving game statistics. Moryossef et al. (2019) advocate modular pipelines that separate meaning representation planning from surface realization. Rebuffel et al. (2020) study controllable generation with constraints for business reporting. Despite progress, evaluation remains fragmented across automatic metrics and human judgments without standardized protocols. Faithfulness audits often reveal subtle hallucinations that elude n-gram and entity overlap measures. We introduce a declarative control interface that binds slots to constraints in a typed schema. The decoder leverages constrained beam search with lattice pruning guided by learned validators. Experiments on sports, finance, and clinical summary datasets show improved accuracy and editability. We release code, constraints, and error annotations to encourage reproducibility and further analysis.

text: This paper examines domain adaptation for low-resource language classification. Robust transfer remains difficult when label distributions and vocabularies diverge. Prior studies emphasize representation learning that preserves task-relevant invariances. We focus on scenario-driven benchmarks that stress extreme typological distance. Our goal is to isolate factors that influence stability under sparse supervision. We review methods spanning sample selection, objective design, and architectural bias. Instance reweighting has been used to reduce sample mismatch across domains. Contrastive pretraining has also been proposed to align latent structures in multilingual spaces. Data augmentation with paraphrase generation can smooth lexical gaps between corpora. Uncertainty estimation is frequently employed to gate pseudo-label propagation. Evaluations often overlook domain-specific calibration and failure modes. Smith et al. (2020) proposed adversarial instance reweighting for cross-lingual sentiment transfer. Johnson and Lee (2019) studied meta-learning schedules for rapid domain shifts in text classification. Ortega et al. (2021) explored subword alignment stability under noisy label regimes. Park and Cho (2022) introduced curriculum-guided pseudo-labeling for pivot-based translation datasets. Nguyen et al. (2020) examined lexical diversity constraints for robust adaptation across distant language families in practice today. These lines of work suggest that transfer benefits from calibrated confidence control. Our study unifies these insights into a pragmatic recipe for small-budget settings. We compare curriculum staging with risk-sensitive fine-tuning across three families of tasks. We measure adaptation cost as a function of supervision and shift magnitude. We report ablations disentangling vocabulary coverage from syntactic mismatch. We further quantify the impact of class prior shift on decision thresholds. Our protocol incorporates pretraining warm starts and targeted vocabulary expansion. We include human-in-the-loop corrections to test annotation-efficient regimes. Metrics include accuracy, expected calibration error, and coverage-adjusted utility. We release scripts that reproduce sampling, scoring, and reporting decisions. Results highlight when pseudo-label feedback loops help or degrade performance. We provide guidance on audit procedures that identify high-risk deployments. The analysis culminates in recommendations for stable cross-lingual adaptation. We conclude with open questions around monitoring and continual updates.

(2020) examined lexical diversity constraints for robust adaptation across distant language families in practice today.

text: Molecular property prediction benefits from structured learning on graphs. Drug discovery datasets are noisy and imbalanced, complicating evaluation. Graph neural networks provide an expressive inductive bias for chemistry. We consider choices of message passing depth and readout pooling. Our survey emphasizes rigor in data curation and split strategies. Scaffold splits are recommended to measure generalization to new chemotypes. Physicochemical priors can regularize models toward plausible predictions. Pretraining on unlabeled molecules can reduce label demand downstream. Self-supervised objectives include edge masking, context prediction, and motif discovery. Multi-task learning can exploit shared structure across assays. Calibration matters when selecting compounds for wet-lab validation. Uncertainty estimation should be combined with domain-specific thresholds. Gilmer et al. (2017) presented message passing neural networks for quantum chemistry benchmarks. Kearnes et al. (2016) described graph convolutional models emphasizing molecular fingerprints. Yang et al. (2019) reported pretraining strategies on large compound libraries. Hu et al. (2020) proposed strategies for self-supervised graph-level objectives. Faber et al. (2017) compared featurization schemes on diverse datasets in reproduction studies with attention to benchmark variance and data curation practices. These studies motivate our approach to disentangle architecture and data factors. We define a grid of configurations and hold preprocessing constant. We evaluate frontier models under matched hyperparameter budgets. We integrate conformer-based features to test geometric sensitivity. We include shallow baselines to contextualize complexity gains. All experiments follow chemically meaningful splits with rigorous leakage checks. We report predictive metrics and calibration-aware enrichment statistics. We perform ablations on featurization, pretraining, and data augmentation. We analyze per-scaffold performance to study heterogeneity across structures. We release reproducible pipelines with deterministic seeds and audit logs. Our findings clarify when GNNs outperform fingerprints under strict splits. The evidence suggests that careful curation outweighs marginal architectural tweaks. We outline implications for active learning and iterative screening campaigns.

(2017) compared featurization schemes on diverse datasets in reproduction studies with attention to benchmark variance and data curation practices.

text: Explainability is critical for deploying machine learning in clinical settings. Clinicians require transparent rationales to assess algorithmic recommendations. Post-hoc methods and inherently interpretable models represent two main pathways. Each pathway entails trade-offs among fidelity, usability, and regulatory burden. We review the literature with attention to evaluation practices and human factors. Clinical risk prediction tasks expose misalignments between explanations and decisions. Temporal data and censoring complicate interpretability metrics in healthcare. Explanation stability across perturbations is essential for trust. Counterfactual reasoning supports actionable insights for clinicians. We discuss the need for standardized protocols in user studies. We highlight harms from misleading saliency attributed to acquisition artifacts. Ribeiro et al. (2016) introduced LIME for local post-hoc explanations of black-box models. Lundberg and Lee (2017) formulated SHAP values grounded in cooperative game theory. Caruana et al. (2015) analyzed intelligible models for pneumonia risk prediction. Tonekaboni et al. (2019) assessed clinician trust with interpretable risk scores. Holzinger et al. (2017) surveyed human-in-the-loop techniques in medical decision support including evaluation protocols and open challenges for deployment today. These contributions reveal a gap between algorithmic faithfulness and human comprehension. Our work addresses this gap with task-grounded evaluation in real workflows. We design interfaces that convey uncertainty and evidence provenance. We propose alignment metrics linking explanations to clinical concepts. We test the approach on sepsis, readmission, and mortality prediction. We include prospective simulations to mimic deployment pressures. The study measures correctness, usefulness, and decision-time impact. We analyze failure modes where explanations conflict with domain knowledge. We explore training-time regularizers that promote semantic consistency. We adapt auditing checklists for reporting transparency and reproducibility. Our results show reliability gains without sacrificing discrimination performance. Clinician feedback indicates improved situational awareness and confidence. We summarize limitations and outline directions for broader clinical validation. We conclude with recommendations for ethical and safe explanation practices.

(2017) surveyed human-in-the-loop techniques in medical decision support including evaluation protocols and open challenges for deployment today.

(2017) surveyed human-in-the-loop techniques in medical decision support including evaluation protocols and open challenges for deployment today.

text: Learning from aligned video and language opens opportunities for grounded understanding. Narrated instructional content provides vast supervision for multimodal models. Pretraining on automatically aligned pairs can reduce annotation demands. Transformers have become central architectures in video-language research. Temporal modeling remains a key challenge for long videos. Tokenization of visual streams introduces trade-offs in resolution and compute. Retrieval and captioning share components yet differ in supervision patterns. Robustness to narration noise is crucial for scaling to web data. Cross-modal alignment benefits from carefully designed pretext objectives. Evaluation practices vary widely and complicate comparison across datasets. We review dataset biases and the impact of pretraining corpora. Xu et al. (2017) proposed SMT for video captioning using temporal attention. Sun et al. (2019) presented VideoBERT with unsupervised tokenized video representations. Lei et al. (2020) introduced MART for sequence-to-sequence pretraining on narrated videos. Gabeur et al. (2020) trained multi-modal transformers for retrieval on HowTo100M. Miech et al. (2019) released large-scale narrated datasets that shaped pretraining protocols across tasks in mainstream video-language research pipelines worldwide adoption. These developments motivate our investigation of modular objectives for pretraining. We hypothesize that balanced token budgets improve alignment signals. We test audio-aware and action-aware adapters within a unified backbone. We provide a standardized tokenization layer to ease reproduction. We curate subsets with controlled narration density for diagnostics. Our experiments cover retrieval, captioning, and video question answering. We analyze scaling laws with respect to hours and vocabulary size. We compare end-to-end training with frozen vision encoders. We report compute-normalized performance to enable fair assessment. Ablations isolate the contribution of temporal stride and attention patterns. Results show consistent gains from adaptive token allocation strategies. Error analysis highlights brittleness under rapid scene changes. We discuss implications for multimodal search and assistive technologies. We release code, checkpoints, and curation recipes to support the community.

text: Federated learning enables training across devices without centralizing raw data. Privacy guarantees are essential for adoption in sensitive applications. Differential privacy offers formal protection against information leakage. Practical deployments must balance utility, privacy, and systems constraints. We survey algorithms that integrate privacy into distributed optimization. Communication efficiency and robustness interact with noise injection strategies. Client heterogeneity challenges both privacy accounting and convergence analysis. Secure aggregation complements privacy by hiding individual updates in transit. Adaptive clipping is a key mechanism for bounding sensitivity in practice. Accounting methods determine the cumulative privacy loss over rounds. Personalization layers can reduce the accuracy gap under strong privacy. Auditing techniques help detect rare but harmful privacy failures. McMahan et al. (2017) introduced communication-efficient federated averaging for on-device learning. Kairouz et al. (2021) cataloged open problems and formalizations in federated settings. Geyer et al. (2017) studied client-level differential privacy in deep learning. Abadi et al. (2016) described the moments accountant for private stochastic gradient descent. Truex et al. (2019) examined privacy threats and defenses in collaborative model training across organizations and real-world deployments at scale. These references expose a fragmented space of choices for practitioners. Our work provides a systematic comparison under unified budgets and datasets. We evaluate end-to-end pipelines across image, text, and tabular tasks. We implement client simulators calibrated to real-world participation patterns. We disentangle the effects of clipping, sampling, and aggregation schedules. We propose privacy-adaptive learning rate and momentum policies. We measure fairness under participation skew and privacy amplification. We report throughput and energy metrics alongside accuracy and privacy loss. Our benchmarks include realistic device churn and on-device constraints. We provide ablations that stress failure modes and recovery strategies. Results indicate clear regimes where privacy budgets are efficiently spent. We translate findings into deployment playbooks for engineering teams. We offer open-source tooling and documentation for reproducible private federated learning.

text: Conversational agents are increasingly deployed in customer support and social platforms. Research has focused on robustness, safety, and sustained engagement. Prior work analyzes training schedules, reward design, and data selection as levers for improvement. This section reviews approaches relevant to data curation and control for open-domain dialogue. Curriculum design has been proposed to manage complexity and stability during training. Safety research emphasizes predictive proxies that align with human judgments under resource constraints. Data compression aims to reduce redundancy while preserving behavioral diversity for interactive systems. Persona and belief tracking anchor responses in user-specific contexts. Multilingual adaptation extends techniques across languages and cultural settings. Collectively, these strands underpin our experimental framework. Smith and Lee (2018) introduced curriculum schedules for training dialogue agents using synthetic tasks drawn from constrained instruction patterns datasets. Garcia et al. (2019) presented reward shaping pipelines assessing conversational safety metrics with offline simulators and proxies for evaluation studies. Nguyen and Patel (2020) explored dataset distillation for chit-chat, compressing multi-domain corpora into concise exemplars guiding retrieval and generation models. Rahman et al. (2021) reported persona-grounded decoding templates emphasizing long-range consistency and measuring drift with specialized turn-level coherence diagnostics tools. Zhou and Kim (2022) introduced few-shot controllers integrating latent belief tracking with constrained beam filtering across multilingual chat benchmarks datasets. The abrupt juxtaposition of objectives in prior studies motivates a unified view connecting data, rewards, and decoding. We fill this gap by proposing a modular pipeline that exposes interfaces for each lever. Our contribution integrates curriculum-aware sampling with risk-sensitive training and constrained generation. We also provide evaluation protocols that balance automatic metrics and human judgments. The protocols consider safety, engagement, and persona faithfulness jointly. Unlike earlier isolated analyses, our pipeline quantifies trade-offs across these axes. The method is compatible with encoder-decoder and decoder-only architectures. It supports adapters for multilingual transfer with minimal retraining. We include ablations documenting the effect of each component. We release datasets, code, and templates to encourage reproducibility. Ethical considerations address user consent, content filtering, and redress mechanisms. Limitations include domain shift and annotator variance under constrained budgets. We expect follow-up work to refine the reward models using contextual calibrations. Future extensions may incorporate online learning under safety guarantees. The following sections detail our approach, datasets, and results.

text: Semi-supervised learning reduces annotation costs by exploiting unlabeled images. Recent methods combine consistency, pseudo-labeling, and contrastive learning. Still, reliable selection of unlabeled data remains difficult. This review outlines ingredients relevant to our framework. We focus on targets, perturbations, and regularization. Teacher student interactions generate training signals without manual labels. Ensembling stabilizes representations under noise. Label generation drives exploitation of confident examples. Contrastive learning builds invariant features across views. Augmentation controls the strength of perturbations required by consistency. The following studies exemplify these themes. We summarize representative advances before introducing our approach. Tarvainen and Valpola (2017) proposed mean teacher models that stabilize targets through exponential moving averages of student parameters during training. Laine and Aila (2017) introduced temporal ensembling to aggregate predictions across epochs, improving consistency without requiring complex augmentation schedules manually. Sohn et al. (2020) studied pseudo-labeling at scale with confidence thresholds, demonstrating strong results on ImageNet and transfer benchmarks datasets. Chen and He (2021) analyzed contrastive objectives for unlabeled data using momentum encoders and memory banks with negative sampling strategies. Xie et al. (2020) explored data augmentation through RandAugment policies combined with consistency training across stochastic views and network perturbations. The surveyed techniques suggest common structure in objectives and schedules. Our method unifies these components under a probabilistic view. We construct a joint model of predictions and perturbations. The model provides adaptive weights for consistency and entropy penalties. It also calibrates pseudo-label confidence by learning thresholds. We analyze the interplay between augmentations and target smoothness. Experiments cover CIFAR, STL, and ImageNet subsets. We report accuracy and calibration metrics with statistical tests. Our ablations isolate the effect of each component. We include computational costs for fair comparison. Limitations include sensitivity to hyperparameters and augmentations. We release code and pretrained checkpoints for reproducibility. The next section presents the methodology in detail.

(2020) explored data augmentation through RandAugment policies combined with consistency training across stochastic views and network perturbations.

text: Traffic forecasting supports critical decisions in urban mobility management. Accurate predictions enable routing, pricing, and control strategies. Spatio-temporal correlations create challenges for traditional time series models. Graph neural networks offer a natural representation of road networks. Recent methods couple spatial convolutions with temporal dynamics. External signals such as weather and events influence demand. Robustness to sensor outages remains a central issue. We review representative architectures to ground our approach. The literature spans diffusion processes, spectral filters, and attention. Benchmarks typically include large metropolitan speed and volume datasets. Evaluation commonly reports horizon-specific errors and stability metrics. Li et al. (2018) proposed diffusion convolutional recurrent networks to capture spatial dependencies using bidirectional random walks on graphs effectively. Yu et al. (2018) introduced spatio-temporal graph convolutions integrating gated temporal modules with localized spectral filters for traffic speed prediction. Wu et al. (2019) presented graph wavelet networks modeling multi-scale interactions with adaptive adjacency, achieving improvements on widely used benchmarks. Bai et al. (2020) explored attention mechanisms for dynamic graphs, aligning temporal contexts with node-specific gates for demand forecasting tasks. Cao and Zhang (2021) investigated probabilistic sequence models for uncertainty quantification, estimating predictive intervals through variational graph encoders and decoders. These models illustrate recurring design choices for capturing structure and time. Our work synthesizes these ideas into a unified probabilistic encoder. The encoder adapts adjacency with data-driven priors. A temporal module models multi-horizon dependencies. We quantify uncertainty via variational objectives. The framework supports missing data through latent imputation. We introduce a training curriculum aligned with congestion regimes. External features are incorporated using conditional embeddings. We design metrics focusing on reliability and calibration. Experiments use public data and a new arterial network dataset. We compare against strong baselines with matched training budgets. Ablations test sensitivity to topology and sampling policy. Limitations include scalability to nationwide networks and nonstationarity. Future work considers adaptive sensors and control integration.

text: Automated essay scoring aims to estimate human ratings for student writing. Reliable evaluation can reduce workload and support formative feedback. Traditional systems rely on hand engineered features. Neural methods learn features directly from text. Both approaches face concerns about validity and fairness. This section reviews representative methods relevant to our design. Feature engineering targets grammar, usage, and style. Neural modeling captures semantics and discourse structure. Prompt specificity complicates generalization across topics. Calibration against human raters is essential for adoption. We organize prior work by feature learning and scoring strategy. The following studies illustrate prominent directions. Attali and Burstein (2006) described e-rater feature engineering emphasizing grammar, mechanics, and style with regression-based scoring functions for essays assessment. Shermis and Burstein (2013) surveyed operational systems and comparative evaluations across large-scale testing scenarios and institutional deployments with extensive analyses. Taghipour and Ng (2016) introduced neural networks for holistic scoring using convolutional encoders trained end-to-end on prompt-specific datasets and rubrics. Dong and Zhang (2016) explored attention mechanisms for sentence-level scoring, aggregating token contributions into prompt-aware document representations for prediction tasks. Ke et al. (2019) investigated coherence modeling with entity graphs to capture discourse flow influencing automated scoring outcomes and reliability. These references provide context for the hybrid model we propose. Our approach combines pretraining with task-specific supervision. A discourse module models paragraph transitions and entity flow. A content module aligns evidence with rubric criteria. The scoring head outputs point predictions with uncertainty estimates. We design diagnostics for bias across demographic and linguistic groups. Training uses balanced sampling across prompts and score ranges. Experiments evaluate agreement with human raters on public datasets. We analyze reliability under adversarially perturbed inputs. Ablations isolate the contribution of each component. Limitations include distribution shift and annotation noise. We plan to expand datasets and rubric coverage. The next section details the methodology and datasets.

text: Industrial IoT deployments rely on timely detection of anomalous sensor behavior. Unsupervised methods are attractive due to scarce labels. Reconstruction and prediction objectives dominate recent approaches. Models must balance sensitivity and false alarms in practice. Streaming constraints demand efficient, incremental updates. This section reviews representative techniques for time series anomaly detection. Sequence models reconstruct nominal dynamics from historical data. Benchmarks and diagnostics assist evaluation and post-hoc analysis. Robust optimization mitigates noise and outliers present in telemetry. Adversarial training enhances feature learning without labels. Structural dependencies across sensors can reveal context anomalies. We now summarize influential studies spanning these aspects. Malhotra et al. (2015) introduced LSTM encoder-decoder models to reconstruct normal sequences and flag deviations as anomalies in operation data. Hundman et al. (2018) presented telemetry anomaly benchmarks from spacecraft systems with interpretable diagnostics for event investigation at scale deployments. Ren et al. (2019) studied recurrent autoencoders with robust loss functions to mitigate noise and reduce false positives during monitoring. Audibert et al. (2020) proposed adversarially trained autoencoders for unsupervised anomaly detection with dual objectives balancing reconstruction and discrimination performance. Xu and Zhao (2021) examined graph-based detectors modeling inter-sensor relations with message passing to capture contextual anomalies in industrial streams. These studies highlight themes our framework brings together. We propose a probabilistic detector with jointly learned dynamics and thresholds. The detector integrates forecasting, reconstruction, and graph regularization. It estimates time-varying alert thresholds calibrated to risk. A streaming variant supports single-pass inference with bounded memory. We design evaluation protocols emphasizing stability and interpretability. Our benchmarks include spacecraft, manufacturing, and energy datasets. We compare to strong baselines under equal latency budgets. Ablations analyze contributions of each component. Sensitivity studies cover contamination rates and noise levels. Limitations include domain shift and rare event scarcity. Future work explores active learning and causal features. The methodology section details models, training, and metrics.

text: Recent work on multimodal fact-checking explores how visual and textual cues can be fused to assess claim veracity in noisy social media contexts. Hansel et al. (2019) introduced cross-attention to align image regions with claim tokens for evidence retrieval, reporting gains on FakeNewsNet. Sharma and Liu (2020) proposed a stance-aware encoder for rumor classification, and Park et al. (2021) compiled a multimodal meme dataset with annotated manipulations to benchmark detection models. Nguyen et al. (2022) released a transformer baseline trained on weakly-labeled claims using distant supervision from hyperlink contexts. Building on these observations, we study efficient evidence grounding for compact models by aligning captions and salient regions with learned claim rationales.

text: Recommender systems increasingly exploit graph structure to model users, items, and relations with higher-order signals across heterogeneous interactions. Our study revisits robustness under sparse supervision and temporal shifts in session-based recommendation. Prior work has emphasized message passing with learned attention over interaction graphs to capture neighborhood preferences and mitigate cold-start effects. Wang et al. (2019) introduced NGCF to propagate embeddings along user–item edges with nonlinear transformations on implicit feedback. He et al. (2020) presented LightGCN emphasizing linear aggregation on bipartite structures, and Sun et al. (2021) curated sequential benchmarks for session recommendations with repeat consumption signals, Zhou et al. (2020) released a large-scale click sequence corpus and reported strong transformer baselines, publicly available for reproducible comparisons today.

text: Text models deployed in sensitive domains must guard against unintended memorization of private data while preserving utility on downstream tasks. We investigate privacy-utility tradeoffs for instruction-tuned encoders trained with adaptive noise schedules. Abadi et al. (2016) formalized differentially private SGD with the moments accountant to track privacy loss during training on image benchmarks. McMahan et al. (2018) scaled private learning in federated settings for mobile keyboards, and Li et al. (2021) introduced DP-SGD variants for transformers with gradient clipping heuristics across pretraining corpora, Carlini et al. (2019) quantified exposure of rare sequences in trained models under realistic deployments. Our approach calibrates per-layer clipping to allocate noise where gradients exhibit concentrated privacy risk while retaining semantic fidelity.

text: Complex question answering over knowledge bases requires reasoning over multi-hop relational paths while handling lexical variability in natural language queries. Bordes et al. (2014) framed simple KB-QA with embedding-based retrieval over Freebase question–answer pairs trained via margin losses. Yih et al. (2015) modeled subgraph matching with semantic parsing, and Sun et al. (2018) released WebQuestionsSP annotations linking questions to grounded graph structures for compositional queries, Berant et al. (2013) introduced the original WebQuestions dataset using Freebase answers and established baseline semantic parsers with publicly released code. Recent neural approaches combine path-based reasoning with text retrieval to incorporate evidence beyond the structured graph. We build a lightweight planner that selects executable hops while learning latent alignments between predicate paraphrases and ontology relations.

text: Mapping natural language descriptions to executable programs has attracted attention across semantic parsing, code generation, and conversational assistants. We target data-efficient synthesis for task-oriented APIs with constrained decoding. Dong and Lapata (2016) explored sequence-to-tree models for semantic parsing that predict hierarchical structures for logical forms on WikiSQL-style tasks. Rabinovich et al. (2017) introduced abstract syntax networks for code generation, Yin and Neubig (2017) proposed a grammar-based decoder for ASTs, and Guo et al. (2019) released CoNaLa with mined NL-code pairs from online forums, Multiple baselines rely on copy mechanisms and subtoken representations for generalization. Our method constrains decoding with type signatures and demonstrates compositional generalization under limited supervision.

text: This section reviews research on domain adaptation for text classification and its implications for robust NLP systems. Foundational approaches characterize distributional mismatch and propose generalization bounds for representation learning under covariate shift. Recent neural methods prioritize objectives that align feature spaces while preserving label semantics across domains. Smith and colleagues (2018) studied domain shifts in sentiment analysis using adversarial training. Chen and Cardie (2019) introduced instance reweighting for cross-domain classification with limited labels. Gururangan and colleagues (2020) showed task-adaptive pretraining benefits downstream performance across multiple corpora. Data augmentation strategies are broadly used in transfer settings today. While these ideas inform our setting, multi-source transfer raises questions about how to balance conflicting signals without degrading minority-domain performance. Prior work on bilevel optimization provides mechanisms to tune instance or domain weights with validation feedback under non-stationary sampling. Our study integrates these principles into a unified training criterion that adapts both encoder and classifier using uncertainty-aware regularization. We evaluate the approach on benchmark suites that include large domain shifts and low-label regimes to measure stability and efficiency.

text: Explainability for graph neural networks has emerged as a central requirement for high-stakes domains such as chemistry and finance. Early methods focused on saliency over node features, but recent work emphasizes combinatorial structures underlying message passing. Faithfulness and stability are two widely discussed properties that shape evaluation protocols for explanation quality. The interplay between fidelity metrics and human interpretability remains unresolved and motivates benchmarking beyond synthetic ground truth. Our work studies stability under perturbations while controlling for model accuracy and sparsity constraints. Ying and colleagues (2019) introduced GNNExplainer to identify subgraphs and features important for predictions. Luo and colleagues (2020) proposed PGExplainer with parameterized masks learned from training data. Bajaj and colleagues (2021) examined counterfactual explanations for graphs with minimal edge perturbations. Attribution metrics vary across datasets and tasks in practice. We build on a unified objective that decouples causal subgraph selection from importance scoring to improve replicability across runs. Experiments on molecular classification and citation networks demonstrate consistent gains with minimal overhead.

text: This section reviews prior research on multimodal question answering with a focus on visual grounding and linguistic reasoning. Early systems relied on recurrent attention over convolutional features, while recent models adopt transformers with cross-modal fusion. Robustness to linguistic shortcuts remains a key challenge, motivating debiased training objectives and counterfactual augmentation. Antol and colleagues (2015) introduced the VQA benchmark to assess joint visual and textual reasoning. Hudson and Manning (2019) presented GQA with program annotations for compositional queries. Lu and colleagues (2019) proposed ViLBERT for vision-language pretraining. Dataset biases complicate generalization across domains and architectures in real-world applications and benchmarks. Beyond benchmarks, open-ended QA with external knowledge sources demands retrieval-aware architectures that can cite evidence. Our study targets compositional generalization while maintaining sample efficiency under limited supervision. We propose a curriculum over reasoning programs inferred from weak signals to mitigate overfitting to answer priors. We evaluate across multiple splits measuring localization accuracy, calibration, and consistency under adversarial perturbations.

text: Detecting anomalies in time series under concept drift is central to monitoring industrial systems and cyber-physical infrastructure. Classical statistical methods model residuals under seasonal-trend decomposition, while machine learning approaches capture nonlinear dependencies. Deep models further leverage temporal context and attention to isolate rare behavior from typical regimes. Schölkopf and colleagues (2001) proposed one-class SVMs for novelty detection in high-dimensional data. Lai and colleagues (2018) developed LSTM-based sequence models for real-time anomalies in IoT streams. Audibert and colleagues (2020) introduced USAD with adversarial autoencoders. Hyperparameter sensitivity influences detection reliability and deployment across domains and operational constraints today. Recent evaluations emphasize pointwise, range-based, and latency-aware metrics that better reflect practitioner needs. We revisit scoring and threshold selection by coupling uncertainty estimates with adaptive baselines. Our framework unifies forecasting errors and reconstruction gaps to reduce false alarms under data drift. Comprehensive experiments on public and proprietary datasets quantify trade-offs between detection delay and precision.

Lai and colleagues (2018) developed LSTM-based sequence models for real-time anomalies in IoT streams. Audibert and colleagues (2020) introduced USAD with adversarial autoencoders. Hyperparameter sensitivity influences detection reliability and deployment across domains and operational constraints today.

text: Program synthesis with autoregressive models has advanced rapidly, enabling natural language to code translation at scale. Despite progress, reliability under distribution shift and safety constraints remains an open challenge. Prior research has explored search, execution-guided decoding, and specification inference to improve correctness. Brown and colleagues (2020) demonstrated few-shot program synthesis capabilities with large language models. Austin and colleagues (2021) analyzed code generation scaling trends across model sizes and datasets. Chen and colleagues (2021) released Codex trained on public repositories. Benchmark leakage and prompt sensitivity complicate fair comparison in standardized evaluations today. Complementary efforts focus on tooling that executes candidates and collects feedback to refine generations. We consider test-time interventions that leverage unit tests and type checkers to steer decoding. Our approach formalizes hybrid search with constraint propagation for intermediate validation during beam exploration. We evaluate across diverse repositories measuring pass rates, edit distances, and resource costs.

Benchmark leakage and prompt sensitivity complicate fair comparison in standardized evaluations today.

text: This paper investigates retrieval-augmented generation for domain-specific question answering in scientific literature. Retrieval components must balance recall of relevant passages with precision that preserves answerability. Prior work has shown that dense representations can capture semantic similarity beyond sparse lexical overlap. Karpukhin et al. (2020) demonstrated the effectiveness of DPR by training dual encoders with in-batch negatives. Subsequent studies improved negative sampling and index refresh for rapidly evolving corpora (Xiong et al., 2021; Izacard et al., 2022). At the same time, hybrid retrieval combines expansion, reranking, and contrastive learning to exploit complementary signals (Luan et al., 2021; Formal et al., 2021). Smith (2020) presented a transformer-based retriever for scientific questions. Johnson and Lee (2019) proposed curriculum strategies for pre-training on heterogeneous corpora. Nguyen et al. (2018) explored distant supervision for entity linking in biomedical abstracts and large-scale collections online. These works are frequently integrated with generative readers that condition on multiple passages using fusion-in-decoder architectures. Izacard and Grave (2021) showed that aggregating encoder states across passages yields strong robustness to noise. Recent advances also consider calibration, using marginal likelihoods and entropy regularization to mitigate hallucinations in open-book settings (Lin et al., 2022; Kadavath et al., 2022). In scientific QA, citation graphs and section structure offer additional priors that can guide retrieval and answer grounding. Zhang et al. (2021) leveraged section-aware indexing to improve evidence localization within methods and results. Graph-based rerankers incorporate co-citation and topical proximity to refine candidate sets before reading (Wang and Lo, 2021). Our study evaluates a retrieval strategy that combines sparse expansion with adaptive dense indexing tailored to subfields. We further introduce a reader that calibrates confidence by cross-checking claims against multiple corroborating passages. The resulting system targets scientific questions that require triangulating evidence across heterogeneous venues and years.
span_text: Smith (2020) presented a transformer-based retriever for scientific questions. Johnson and Lee (2019) proposed curriculum strategies for pre-training on heterogeneous corpora. Nguyen et al. (2018) explored distant supervision for entity linking in biomedical abstracts and large-scale collections online.

Nguyen et al. (2018) explored distant supervision for entity linking in biomedical abstracts and large-scale collections online.

text: Ensuring fairness in recommender systems requires balancing utility, exposure, and stakeholder welfare. Recent work frames these goals as constrained optimization over stochastic ranking policies. Exposure disparities often arise from feedback loops that couple relevance estimation and user interaction. Joachims et al. (2017) modeled position bias to disentangle relevance from click propensities in learning to rank. Singh and Joachims (2018) introduced fair ranking constraints that control exposure according to desirability. In platforms with creators, group-level guarantees must coexist with individual opportunity to be discovered. Bower et al. (2021) studied trade-offs between creator diversity and user satisfaction in music recommendation. Interventions range from calibrated reranking to counterfactual propensity reweighting across impressions. Beutel et al. (2019) analyzed representation bias in large-scale recommenders deployed at scale today. Burke (2017) formalized multi-sided fairness objectives for ranking under marketplace constraints. Yao and Huang (2017) studied equality of opportunity metrics for personalized content delivery. These perspectives motivate evaluation protocols that report both utility and disparity under consistent logging policies. Sapiezynski et al. (2019) developed audit methodologies that simulate platform policies while preserving user privacy. Causal modeling further clarifies how interventions alter long-run equilibria in producer exposure. Chaney et al. (2018) analyzed feedback loops in simulators and characterized conditions that exacerbate inequality. Our approach addresses multi-stakeholder fairness by optimizing exposure allocations under explicit budget constraints. We quantify risk using concentration indices computed over creator segments and time. The proposed framework yields interpretable controls that scale to millions of items in production settings.
span_text: Beutel et al. (2019) analyzed representation bias in large-scale recommenders deployed at scale today. Burke (2017) formalized multi-sided fairness objectives for ranking under marketplace constraints. Yao and Huang (2017) studied equality of opportunity metrics for personalized content delivery.

Burke (2017) formalized multi-sided fairness objectives for ranking under marketplace constraints. Yao and Huang (2017) studied equality of opportunity metrics for personalized content delivery.

text: Vision-language pretraining has enabled strong performance on retrieval, captioning, and VQA from shared representations. Unified encoders align modalities by optimizing objectives that encourage semantic correspondence across images and text. Data scale and alignment noise remain central challenges that limit transfer to fine-grained tasks. Contrastive learning mitigates noise by encouraging hard negatives and temperature-scaled similarities in the embedding space. Region-aware architectures capture localized grounding that benefits referring expression comprehension and reasoning over objects. Lu et al. (2019) introduced UNITER with joint image-text pretraining objectives. Chen et al. (2020) proposed OSCAR using object tags to ground cross-modal alignment. Li et al. (2021) developed ALBEF with momentum distillation across contrastive and matching tasks. While these models achieve competitive benchmarks, domain shift to scientific figures or charts often degrades accuracy. Specialized corpora with structured annotations alleviate misalignment when captions describe procedures rather than scenes. Feature adapters can reconcile differences between convolutional backbones and transformer tokenization of detected regions. Training recipes frequently combine masked language modeling with image-text matching to stabilize convergence. End-to-end finetuning on downstream datasets improves retrieval precision when negative mining remains consistent with pretraining. Recent work also explores soft-prompt conditioning to steer cross-modal attention without changing backbone weights. Such prompts can encode task-specific priors like object categories or template questions for image-grounded QA. We investigate a lightweight alignment module that reweights region tokens based on caption structure. Our experiments emphasize low-data regimes where robust zero-shot transfer is critical. The analysis highlights how reweighting reduces spurious cross-modal correlations that arise from frequent object co-occurrence.
span_text: Lu et al. (2019) introduced UNITER with joint image-text pretraining objectives. Chen et al. (2020) proposed OSCAR using object tags to ground cross-modal alignment. Li et al. (2021) developed ALBEF with momentum distillation across contrastive and matching tasks.

text: Program synthesis with large language models aims to generate correct-by-construction code from weak supervision. Natural language specifications, input-output examples, and unit tests define partial constraints on the target program. Search procedures over model samples can improve functional correctness by exploiting specification structure. Constraint-guided reranking identifies candidates that satisfy tests while preserving stylistic preferences. Self-consistency and majority voting reduce stochastic errors and capture complementary reasoning paths in decoding. Type systems and symbolic execution further prune invalid branches before executing expensive test suites. Devlin et al. (2019) introduced BERT as a pretraining approach for language understanding. Austin et al. (2021) surveyed program synthesis with large models and prompting. Le et al. (2022) presented CODEGEN trained on multilingual code for competitive programming. Incorporating execution feedback during decoding refines hypotheses when errors can be localized to small patches. Liu et al. (2023) integrated repair operators that edit failing snippets guided by test traces. Cheng et al. (2023) used backtracking search with temperature annealing to diversify semantics without bloating length. Datasets for synthesis often contain spurious cues, making robustness to prompt paraphrases an important goal. Benchmark leakage requires careful separation of training and evaluation repositories for fair comparison. We build a hybrid solver that interleaves constrained sampling with test-time repair at fixed budgets. Our analysis examines failure modes across arithmetic, string manipulation, and data structure tasks. We report both pass rates and computational costs to contextualize accuracy under fixed wall-clock budgets. The results reveal gains from aligning decoding strategies with specification granularity and test coverage.
span_text: Devlin et al. (2019) introduced BERT as a pretraining approach for language understanding. Austin et al. (2021) surveyed program synthesis with large models and prompting. Le et al. (2022) presented CODEGEN trained on multilingual code for competitive programming.

text: Long-term time series forecasting underpins planning in energy, retail, and transportation. Transformers promise scalability and flexible context windows but raise questions about inductive biases for trends and seasonality. Decomposition strategies separate slowly varying components from residual fluctuations to ease learning. Patchwise tokenization and channel mixing improve efficiency on multivariate datasets with many sensors. Regularization via frequency masking can prevent overfitting when training windows are short relative to horizons. Probabilistic outputs are important for downstream risk management in decision pipelines. Zhou et al. (2021) proposed Informer with ProbSparse attention for long sequence forecasting. Wu et al. (2021) introduced Autoformer using decomposition blocks and series-wise connections. Zeng et al. (2023) revisited linear models and highlighted simple baselines for accuracy. Recent studies also emphasize evaluation protocols that avoid leakage and respect rolling-origin splits. Dataset curation affects conclusions because calendar effects and event shocks differ across benchmarks. New metrics that blend calibration and sharpness provide more actionable summaries than point error alone. Exogenous variables such as weather and prices can be integrated through additive conditioning during decoding. Sparse attention patterns reduce quadratic costs while preserving long-range dependencies in practice. Our approach augments seasonal basis functions with learned residual attention across channels. We analyze robustness to horizon length, missing values, and covariate shift across domains. Ablations isolate contributions from decomposition, covariates, and probabilistic training objectives. The findings support hybrid designs that combine strong baselines with targeted attention mechanisms.
span_text: Zhou et al. (2021) proposed Informer with ProbSparse attention for long sequence forecasting. Wu et al. (2021) introduced Autoformer using decomposition blocks and series-wise connections. Zeng et al. (2023) revisited linear models and highlighted simple baselines for accuracy.

text: Domain adaptation for neural machine translation remains challenging due to sparse in-domain supervision and shifting lexical distributions. Chu and Wang (2018) survey fine-tuning and data selection strategies for adapting NMT systems. Britz et al. (2017) analyze architecture variations affecting translation quality across benchmarks. Luong and Manning (2015) introduce attention-based models for alignment in sequence transduction. We build on these foundations by proposing a curriculum-driven adapter that jointly reweights source data and controls exposure to domain-specific terminology.

(2017) analyze architecture variations affecting translation quality across benchmarks. Luong and Manning (2015) introduce attention-based models for alignment in sequence transduction.

text: Urban traffic forecasting increasingly relies on spatiotemporal graph learning to model road dynamics and demand variability. Li et al. (2018) present DCRNN to model diffusion on road networks. Wu et al. (2019) propose Graph WaveNet with adaptive adjacency for long-range temporal dependencies. Yu et al. (2018) design STGCN for short-term urban speed prediction tasks. We revisit spatiotemporal attention with demand-aware embeddings that reparameterize adjacency through exogenous signals and robust loss calibration.

text: Active learning for text classification seeks to reduce labeling budgets by strategically selecting informative examples under uncertainty. Lewis and Gale (1994) compare uncertainty sampling objectives for document routing with probabilistic models. Settles (2009) reviews query strategies and theoretical motivations across pool-based scenarios. Huang et al. (2016) investigate representation learning with gradient length for neural classifiers. Our study introduces a diversity-regularized policy that incorporates annotation cost priors and maintains class coverage in multilingual settings.

Settles (2009) reviews query strategies and theoretical motivations across pool-based scenarios. Huang et al. (2016) investigate representation learning with gradient length for neural classifiers.

text: Neural data-to-text generation must balance factual fidelity and linguistic fluency when verbalizing structured tables. Wiseman et al. (2017) construct RotoWire and report copying deficits in sequence models. Puduppully et al. (2019) propose content planners that separate record selection from realization. Nan et al. (2021) introduce entity-aware approaches emphasizing controllable references in summaries. We target low-resource domains by aligning schema linking with contrastive training to curb hallucinations while preserving stylistic variety.

text: Fairness in ranking systems involves equitable exposure, calibration, and user satisfaction under strategic feedback loops. Biega et al. (2018) formalize amortized exposure as a resource distributed across positions. Singh and Joachims (2018) derive constraints connecting exposure to merit within stochastic ranking policies. Ekstrand et al. (2022) survey recommender system fairness with stakeholder-centric perspectives. We present a counterfactual simulator that tunes ranking objectives against platform-level exposure disparities while preserving user relevance.

Singh and Joachims (2018) derive constraints connecting exposure to merit within stochastic ranking policies. Ekstrand et al.
