text: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw data across personal devices. Mobile health applications benefit from this paradigm due to strict privacy, connectivity, and power constraints. Recent studies emphasize robust optimization under device heterogeneity and intermittent participation. Our work examines personalization and drift mitigation for physiological time series collected on smartphones and wearables. We contextualize the approach within prior advances in communication efficiency and privacy preservation. Li et al. (2019) investigated client drift in decentralized optimization and noted unstable convergence under non-IID data. Kairouz et al. (2021) summarized privacy challenges in federated learning and cataloged threat models across deployment settings. Bonawitz et al. (2017) introduced a secure aggregation protocol for mobile devices in production systems for large, diverse user populations today. These references collectively highlight the operational realities that shape model design for health monitoring. Beyond systems aspects, clinical signals exhibit nonstationarity that challenges global aggregation rules. Personalization strategies seek to reconcile shared representations with user-specific adaptations in changing conditions. We focus on representation learning that respects temporal dynamics and preserves on-device privacy budgets. Communication costs remain a core barrier for high-frequency sensing pipelines in practice. Gradient compression and update sparsity reduce bandwidth but can distort optimization trajectories under skewed data. Adaptive sampling policies further limit transmissions by selecting salient windows, trading coverage for fidelity. Evaluating these trade-offs requires datasets with realistic usage patterns, multiple sensor modalities, and longitudinal labels. Hard et al. (2018) proposed adaptive participation to accommodate device availability and connectivity constraints. Becker et al. (2022) presented personalization layers that align local objectives with user-specific loss functions in mobile keyboards. Zhao et al. (2020) studied compression for gradient updates using sparsification and quantization in bandwidth-limited settings. Truex et al. (2019) surveyed privacy attacks including property inference and data reconstruction, and Nasr et al. (2021) evaluated federated defenses under black-box threat assumptions across varied tasks and deployment contexts worldwide. Despite progress, the interplay of personalization, compression, and privacy is underexplored for mobile health targets. Existing benchmarks seldom capture symptom fluctuations and medication effects that drive nonstationary drift. Our study introduces a protocol for episodic training and testing aligned with daily living cycles. We report communication, privacy, and accuracy metrics that reflect deployment constraints and clinical relevance. The synthesis motivates methods that remain stable under participation volatility while providing individualized predictions.

(2021) summarized privacy challenges in federated learning and cataloged threat models across deployment settings. Bonawitz et al. (2017) introduced a secure aggregation protocol for mobile devices in production systems for large, diverse user populations today.

(2021) summarized privacy challenges in federated learning and cataloged threat models across deployment settings. Bonawitz et al. (2017) introduced a secure aggregation protocol for mobile devices in production systems for large, diverse user populations today.

text: Predicting molecular properties is a central task for accelerating chemical discovery and toxicity assessment. Graph neural networks treat molecules as graphs and learn representations directly from structure. This section reviews foundations and highlights dataset and evaluation issues relevant to our study. We focus on architectures that aggregate neighborhood information and support message passing over bonds. Duvenaud et al. (2015) introduced neural fingerprints that replace fixed descriptors with learned convolutional features on graphs. Gilmer et al. (2017) formalized message passing neural networks and provided a unified view across architectures. Kearnes et al. (2016) explored graph convolutions tailored to molecular graphs with atom and bond features used by many current multimodal pretraining pipelines and datasets. These early contributions established a foundation for end-to-end learning on molecular graphs. Subsequent work extended expressive power with attention mechanisms and edge-conditioned filters. The choice of featurization and pooling strategy remains important for tasks varying from solubility to bioactivity. Our approach emphasizes inductive biases that reflect chemistry domain knowledge while maintaining scalability. We evaluate design choices across curated benchmarks and realistic scaffold splits. Data curation and evaluation protocols strongly influence reported progress in the literature. Benchmark suites with consistent loaders, metrics, and splits reduce variance across implementations. Hu et al. (2020) proposed strategies for pretraining graph neural networks on large corpora of unlabeled molecules. Yang et al. (2019) released OGB benchmarks with standardized splits and evaluator implementations for fair comparison. Scalia et al. (2020) studied uncertainty estimation for molecular property prediction using ensembling and calibration techniques. Stokes et al. (2020) demonstrated antibiotic discovery with deep learning on chemical space, and Jiang et al. (2021) examined scaffold-based data splits for assessing generalization gaps in molecular models in practice. While these studies span diverse goals, they inform the components we evaluate in our pipeline. Pretraining can accelerate convergence and improve low-data performance on specialized endpoints. Uncertainty estimation is critical for prioritizing compounds and managing experimental throughput. We adopt calibration metrics aligned with decision thresholds used in screening. To probe generalization, we include cold-start evaluations on unseen scaffolds and uncommon functional groups. The analysis surfaces sources of leakage that arise from near-duplicate structures in training folds. These considerations shape the modeling and evaluation choices presented in our experiments.

(2017) formalized message passing neural networks and provided a unified view across architectures. Kearnes et al. (2016) explored graph convolutions tailored to molecular graphs with atom and bond features used by many current multimodal pretraining pipelines and datasets.

text: Vision–language models learn joint representations that connect visual content with natural language semantics. Pretraining on large corpora of aligned pairs has unlocked strong zero-shot and few-shot performance on diverse tasks. Early datasets and methods established the foundations for reasoning over images and text at scale. Antol et al. (2015) introduced VQA with a large question answering dataset pairing images and natural language. Karpathy and Fei-Fei (2015) proposed alignment models linking visual regions to sentence fragments for captioning. Chen et al. (2015) released MSCOCO captions enabling large-scale training of image description systems used by many current multimodal pretraining pipelines and datasets. These resources framed the interplay between visual regions and linguistic units in recognition tasks. Subsequent research explored attention mechanisms that align words with localized cues. Models now incorporate region features, patch embeddings, and token-level objectives to refine grounding. Our study investigates fine-grained retrieval under domain shift from web images to scientific figures. We also examine language prompts and adapter modules for parameter-efficient specialization. Contrastive pretraining has become a standard pathway for strong transfer on downstream tasks. Architectures differ in how they balance unimodal pretraining and cross-modal fusion. Data cleaning and deduplication strongly impact learning dynamics in noisy web corpora. We compare design choices while holding tokenization, augmentations, and optimization schedules constant. Radford et al. (2021) presented CLIP, training on image–text pairs to learn transferable representations via contrastive objectives. Jia et al. (2021) scaled weakly supervised image–text pretraining with ALIGN using noise-tolerant contrastive learning on web data. Li et al. (2021) introduced ALBEF that combines contrastive objectives with cross-modal matching and momentum distillation. Zhai et al. (2022) reported LiT for zero-shot transfer by freezing a pre-trained vision encoder and training a text tower with objective alignment on varied image retrieval benchmarks worldwide. These developments illustrate a spectrum of coupling between image and text encoders. Choice of objective shapes retrieval precision, caption quality, and localization accuracy. We analyze temperature scaling and negative sampling strategies that affect contrastive gradients. Our evaluation spans domain-shifted retrieval and captioning benchmarks with controlled contamination. The experiments quantify trade-offs between data scale, model capacity, and finetuning budgets. Findings inform principled selections of objectives and adapters for efficient transfer.

text: Vision–language models learn joint representations that connect visual content with natural language semantics. Pretraining on large corpora of aligned pairs has unlocked strong zero-shot and few-shot performance on diverse tasks. Early datasets and methods established the foundations for reasoning over images and text at scale. Antol et al. (2015) introduced VQA with a large question answering dataset pairing images and natural language. Karpathy and Fei-Fei (2015) proposed alignment models linking visual regions to sentence fragments for captioning. Chen et al. (2015) released MSCOCO captions enabling large-scale training of image description systems used by many current multimodal pretraining pipelines and datasets. These resources framed the interplay between visual regions and linguistic units in recognition tasks. Subsequent research explored attention mechanisms that align words with localized cues. Models now incorporate region features, patch embeddings, and token-level objectives to refine grounding. Our study investigates fine-grained retrieval under domain shift from web images to scientific figures. We also examine language prompts and adapter modules for parameter-efficient specialization. Contrastive pretraining has become a standard pathway for strong transfer on downstream tasks. Architectures differ in how they balance unimodal pretraining and cross-modal fusion. Data cleaning and deduplication strongly impact learning dynamics in noisy web corpora. We compare design choices while holding tokenization, augmentations, and optimization schedules constant. Radford et al. (2021) presented CLIP, training on image–text pairs to learn transferable representations via contrastive objectives. Jia et al. (2021) scaled weakly supervised image–text pretraining with ALIGN using noise-tolerant contrastive learning on web data. Li et al. (2021) introduced ALBEF that combines contrastive objectives with cross-modal matching and momentum distillation. Zhai et al. (2022) reported LiT for zero-shot transfer by freezing a pre-trained vision encoder and training a text tower with objective alignment on varied image retrieval benchmarks worldwide. These developments illustrate a spectrum of coupling between image and text encoders. Choice of objective shapes retrieval precision, caption quality, and localization accuracy. We analyze temperature scaling and negative sampling strategies that affect contrastive gradients. Our evaluation spans domain-shifted retrieval and captioning benchmarks with controlled contamination. The experiments quantify trade-offs between data scale, model capacity, and finetuning budgets. Findings inform principled selections of objectives and adapters for efficient transfer.

text: Estimating causal effects from observational text is increasingly important for policy analysis and social science. Foundations of modern causal inference inform modeling choices when language enters as a measurement proxy. Imbens and Rubin (2015) formalized potential outcomes and clarified assumptions underlying causal identification. Pearl (2009) developed graphical models and do-calculus to encode causal structure for reasoning about interventions. Angrist and Pischke (2009) provided applied econometric tools for causal analysis with designs such as instrumental variables and difference-in-differences used in policy evaluation across diverse empirical settings. These theoretical perspectives inform identification strategies and assumptions for downstream applications with language data. Text introduces high dimensionality and potential selection mechanisms that complicate standard estimators. Researchers must disentangle linguistic variation related to treatment assignment from variation related to outcomes. Representation learning provides a pathway to reduce dimensionality while preserving relevant causal structure. Our work studies design choices that interact with propensity estimation and outcome modeling. Recent methods leverage deep encoders to construct sufficient summaries under plausibility conditions. These models must be evaluated for balance, overlap, and robustness to domain shift. Validating identification requires synthetic controls and diagnostic metrics tailored to language features. Roberts et al. (2020) discussed causal inference with text, highlighting measurement and identification issues when using language as data. Veitch et al. (2020) proposed text embeddings for causal inference that retain information relevant to treatment and outcome. Keith et al. (2020) analyzed sensitivity to unobserved confounding in high-dimensional settings with text features. Pryzant et al. (2021) presented adversarially regularized representations to control for observed confounding while preserving predictive content in observational studies across reviews, forums, and news corpora worldwide today. Together these works suggest avenues for principled use of text in observational studies. We operationalize diagnostics that check balance at token and topic levels across groups. Our pipeline integrates propensity modeling with inverse weighting and doubly robust estimators. We introduce evaluation tasks that vary label entropy and vocabulary drift to probe identifiability. The experiments examine bias under misspecification and limited overlap in high-dimensional covariates. We report treatment effect errors alongside calibration and coverage metrics for uncertainty quantification. Reproducible protocols and open resources support careful comparison and future work. The goal is to align modeling decisions with causal identifiability while respecting text-specific constraints.

text: Detecting anomalies in industrial Internet of Things time series is essential for reliability and safety. Data are high dimensional, nonstationary, and exhibit complex dependencies driven by processes and control loops. Learning-based methods must balance detection accuracy with interpretability and deployment constraints on edge devices. Filonov et al. (2016) evaluated LSTM-based detectors for industrial sensor anomalies on benchmark process data. Hundman et al. (2018) proposed an LSTM encoder–decoder framework for spacecraft telemetry with error reconstruction. Malhotra et al. (2015) introduced stacked LSTM models for multipoint anomaly detection using predictive distributions over sequences in operational environments with limited labeled anomalies today. These sequence models motivated a surge of research on robust forecasting and reconstruction objectives. Reconstruction error alone can be brittle when operating conditions drift or exogenous events occur. Density modeling and probabilistic calibration help differentiate rare events from noisy but normal variations. Our framework integrates forecasting, reconstruction, and density components with adaptive thresholds. Realistic benchmarks include long horizons, variable sampling rates, and missing data patterns. Model selection is sensitive to window sizes, seasonality, and process phase transitions. We consider architectures that include attention and temporal convolutions to capture multi-scale effects. The evaluation includes early detection metrics and cost-sensitive scores aligned with operations. We report latency and compute overhead to reflect edge deployment budgets. Zong et al. (2018) developed Deep Autoencoding Gaussian Mixture Models that combine reconstruction with density estimation for unsupervised detection. Su et al. (2019) presented OmniAnomaly using stochastic VAE with planar flows on multivariate sequences. Audibert et al. (2020) proposed USAD, a GAN-inspired autoencoder training scheme to detect anomalies in generic time series. Wu et al. (2021) introduced graph-based temporal modeling for industrial sensors with learned inter-sensor dependencies and reported gains on complex process datasets under realistic noise and maintenance schedules. While progress is substantial, challenges remain in handling simultaneous faults across subsystems. Interpretability techniques such as attributions and counterfactuals can aid root cause analysis. We propose structured explanations based on learned sensor graphs and temporal saliency. Domain knowledge from control theory informs constraints that stabilize training and inference. Our experiments use public benchmarks and a simulated refinery to assess generalization. The results guide practical deployment choices for monitoring complex industrial assets.

text: Traffic forecasting is a canonical benchmark for spatiotemporal learning with significant societal stakes. Prior work has emphasized capturing both road topology and time-varying congestion processes. Early statistical models relied on ARIMA variants with handcrafted features and limited spatial scope. Deep models subsequently exploited convolution, recurrence, and attention to encode complex correlations. Graph neural networks emerged as a natural formulation over sensor graphs and road maps. Diffusion processes on directed graphs help represent upstream and downstream influences. Temporal modules must handle seasonality, burstiness, and nonstationary events in sensor data. Benchmark suites such as METR-LA and PEMS-BAY standardized evaluation and comparative analysis. Li et al. (2018) model road networks with diffusion convolution to capture spatial dynamics. Wu et al. (2019) introduce graph wavelet transforms for multiresolution traffic patterns. Cini et al. (2022) leverage attention over adaptive adjacency matrices to refine temporal dependencies across sensors. Yu et al. (2020) report gains on metropolitan datasets. Despite widespread adoption, many models entangle temporal and spatial priors in ways that hinder interpretability. Recent decomposition strategies separate node-wise autoregression from graph message passing to improve clarity. External variables such as weather, incidents, and holiday calendars further complicate forecasting pipelines. Data sparsity arises when sensors fail or new roads come online without history. Semi-supervised learning on partially observed graphs mitigates cold-start conditions. Uncertainty quantification is essential for risk-aware routing and control. Probabilistic forecasters combine aleatoric noise models with epistemic ensembles for calibrated predictions. Our study targets modular architectures that disentangle space, time, and exogenous drivers under a unified loss. We propose diagnostics that isolate error modes by node degree, path betweenness, and regime shifts. The resulting framework yields actionable insights for deployment in urban mobility platforms.

text: Prompting has reshaped how practitioners adapt large language models without expensive fine-tuning. The central idea is to elicit latent skills through carefully constructed inputs and labels. Early work focused on hand-crafted templates that map tasks to a pre-training space. Subsequent approaches learned soft embeddings that steer model behavior more flexibly. Retrieval-augmented prompting incorporates external evidence at inference time. Evaluation remains challenging due to sensitivity to wording, order, and calibration. Robustness also depends on the distributional alignment between pre-training and target tasks. We review techniques that manipulate exemplars, templates, and scoring rules. Brown et al. (2020) demonstrate in-context learning with large autoregressive models on diverse tasks. Gao et al. (2021) formulate prompt-based retrieval to select label-consistent exemplars. Schick and Schütze (2021) recast classification as cloze prediction with pattern-verbalizer pairs to exploit pre-trained knowledge. Min et al. (2022) analyze when examples actually help users. These advances motivate a systematic examination of controllability under deployment constraints. We study mechanisms for composing discrete prompts with continuous adapters. Our formulation measures prompt capacity via mutual information with target label spaces. Data-efficient selection reduces the need for exhaustive trial and error. We calibrate scores using generative likelihoods and include abstention options for ambiguity. To map failure modes, we quantify variance due to exemplar position and paraphrase class. The analysis identifies stable regions where small perturbations do not flip decisions. We release a benchmark that isolates prompt effects from retrieval quality and model size. The resulting guidelines align with ergonomic constraints faced by non-expert practitioners. This framework advances reproducible prompt engineering grounded in measurable guarantees.

text: Fairness in recommender systems has gained prominence as platforms mediate access to information and opportunity. The literature spans user-level equity, item-side provider outcomes, and societal externalities. Measurement is complicated by feedback loops and the absence of counterfactual exposure. Offline metrics may not reflect business constraints or long-term welfare. Algorithmic interventions can adjust rankings, diversify slates, or personalize constraints. Stakeholders often include consumers, creators, and intermediaries with conflicting objectives. Recent surveys highlight tensions between transparency, privacy, and competitive dynamics. We focus on exposure-aware objectives for large-scale recommendation. Audit methodologies attempt to detect disparate treatment in historical logs. Burke et al. (2018) propose multi-stakeholder fairness metrics for recommenders. Yao and Huang (2017) study exposure disparities across user groups in collaborative filtering. Mehrotra et al. (2018) design re-ranking methods to trade off relevance and provider fairness. Ekstrand et al. (2021) conduct audits of public datasets and algorithms. Industry deployments lag. While these strands are informative, practical systems must integrate constraints into serving stacks. We view exposure as a resource and define budgets that cap cumulative disadvantage. Our approach learns dual variables that price fairness at request time. The mechanism composes with bandit exploration and delayed feedback models. We evaluate on impression logs with simulated providers and propensity-corrected outcomes. Results show reduced disparity with minimal regret relative to pure relevance baselines. Ablations indicate robustness to traffic shifts and catalog churn. The framework offers tractable levers for policy while preserving user experience.

text: Multimodal summarization aims to distill salient information from text, images, and video. Real-world applications include news briefs, instructional guides, and meeting digests. The challenge lies in aligning cross-modal evidence while avoiding redundancy. Early methods relied on heuristic fusion and late-stage re-ranking. Neural approaches enable joint learning of content selection and surface realization. Pre-training across modalities has further improved representation quality. Attention mechanisms allocate capacity to modality-specific cues and shared semantics. We survey model architectures and resources relevant to document-centric multimedia. Li et al. (2020) introduce a multimodal transformer that fuses visual regions with sentence tokens. Sun et al. (2019) pretrain video-text representations with hierarchical attention for summarization. Chen and Bansal (2018) learn pointer-based models guided by salient frames. Liu et al. (2020) explore graph encoders over scene entities. Datasets remain limited. Despite progress, summary faithfulness deteriorates when modalities conflict or captions are noisy. To mitigate hallucination, constrained decoding can enforce entity grounding across streams. Coverage penalties help deter repetitive phrasing in long videos with transcripts. Our study isolates alignment errors using counterfactual masking of visual or textual inputs. We evaluate cross-domain transfer and measure reliance on specific modalities. The proposed model employs a disentangled planner and generator to enhance controllability. We adopt calibrated uncertainty estimates to abstain when evidence is insufficient. A comprehensive ablation quantifies the contribution of each fusion pathway. The framework provides reproducible baselines and protocols for future research.

(2019) pretrain video-text representations with hierarchical attention for summarization. Chen and Bansal (2018) learn pointer-based models guided by salient frames. Liu et al. (2020) explore graph encoders over scene entities. Datasets remain limited.

(2020) explore graph encoders over scene entities. Datasets remain limited.

text: Privacy-preserving machine learning has become central to responsible deployment of data-driven systems. Differential privacy offers a formal guarantee that bounds information leakage about individuals. In deep learning, privacy is typically enforced by perturbing gradients and limiting sensitivity. Achieving useful accuracy under strict budgets requires careful accounting and hyperparameter choices. Noise addition interacts with optimization dynamics and model architectures in nontrivial ways. Public benchmarks help quantify the trade-off between accuracy and privacy consumption. Privacy amplification by subsampling is a key ingredient in modern analyses. We review algorithmic components and accounting tools for private training. Abadi et al. (2016) formalize DP-SGD with a moments accountant for tight composition. McMahan et al. (2018) adapt private training to federated optimization at scale. Papernot et al. (2017) propose PATE to transfer knowledge from ensembles under privacy. Mironov (2017) introduces Rényi differential privacy for improved analysis. Industry practice still lags. Despite these foundations, practitioners often face data imbalance and long-tailed labels in private regimes. We explore clipping strategies that equalize gradient contributions across classes. Our analysis characterizes privacy-utility frontiers under varying sampling rates and clipping norms. We additionally compare accounting methods that target tightness and runtime efficiency. Calibration with public pretraining can reclaim accuracy without enlarging privacy budgets. We report ablations across optimizers, activations, and model width under fixed epsilon. The results suggest stable recipes for private training at moderate scale. Our open-source toolkit automates accounting, evaluation, and audit trails. These contributions aim to lower operational barriers to privacy-first learning.

text: Stance detection remains a challenging task across domains and platforms. Prior research has explored linguistic cues, pragmatic signals, and network features. With the rise of pretrained language models, new methods have emerged rapidly. This paper examines domain-robust stance detection with limited supervision. We focus on aligning targets and evidential spans under distribution shifts. We analyze errors related to topic drift and implicit sentiment cues. Existing surveys have summarized architectures and datasets in narrow contexts. Cross-target transfer is central for practical applications in emerging events. Evaluation protocols often conflate stance with sentiment and topic relevance. Park et al. (2018) surveyed stance detection datasets in political discourse in online debates. Mohammad et al. (2017) introduced a shared task emphasizing cross-target generalization and multilingual settings. Allaway and McKeown (2020) proposed target-aware architectures for implicit stance. Glandt et al. (2021) released COVID-19 stance corpora with noisy annotations. Despite extensive benchmarks, generalization across unseen targets is still limited. Prior datasets rarely annotate fine-grained evidence supporting stance labels. Annotation quality influences robustness more than model size in some settings. Social context and user history can provide useful priors for target linking. Yet privacy constraints require models that rely on content only. Recent approaches incorporate rationale extraction to improve interpretability. Among pretrained encoders, domain-adaptive pretraining shows mixed results. Data augmentation with counterfactual rewriting has been tested on small corpora. Few-shot prompt-based methods show promise but suffer from label bias. Our method builds a target-aware encoder with event-invariant contrastive objectives. We design a curriculum that gradually introduces perturbations in target descriptions. The training data combines curated sources and weakly labeled social media posts. We introduce an evaluation suite spanning politics, health, and entertainment. We report metrics at target, domain, and event levels for clarity. The contributions include a robust framework and a transparent evaluation protocol. We release code and data to facilitate reproducibility and further research.

(2017) introduced a shared task emphasizing cross-target generalization and multilingual settings. Allaway and McKeown (2020) proposed target-aware architectures for implicit stance. Glandt et al. (2021) released COVID-19 stance corpora with noisy annotations.

(2021) released COVID-19 stance corpora with noisy annotations.

text: Traffic forecasting is a canonical problem in intelligent transportation systems. Urban mobility patterns exhibit strong spatial and temporal dependencies. Graph neural networks have become the dominant modeling paradigm for road networks. Reliable forecasts enable congestion mitigation and adaptive signal control. Yet robustness to sensor failures and distribution shift remains underexplored. We study architectures that integrate topology learning with uncertainty estimation. Our focus is on scalable training under sparse supervision. Prior reviews have emphasized receptive fields and learning objectives. Benchmark comparisons often ignore missing data and irregular sampling. We revisit standard datasets to isolate structural and stochastic sources of error. The literature on spatio-temporal modeling is extensive and rapidly evolving. Li et al. (2018) proposed Diffusion Convolutional RNNs for spatial traffic dynamics. Yu et al. (2018) introduced Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutional Networks using gated convolutions. Wu et al. (2019) developed Graph WaveNet with adaptive adjacency matrices. Bai et al. (2020) presented ASTGCN combining attention with temporal modeling for city forecasting. These models highlight complementary design choices that can be unified. Existing work rarely quantifies calibration under peak congestion. We incorporate heteroscedastic regression losses to capture variability. Sensor outages are simulated to test reliability in adverse conditions. Our topology module infers latent edges from mobility covariates. We propose a sparsity prior that preserves critical corridors. Data from multiple cities is harmonized with a shared ontology. The evaluation includes probabilistic and deterministic metrics. We analyze error propagation across horizons and subnetworks. Efficiency is measured in wall-clock time and memory usage. A comprehensive ablation identifies contributions of each component. We compare against classic baselines and recent graph transformers. Our findings bridge accuracy with reliability for practical deployment. Reproducible code and processed datasets are provided with full documentation.

Wu et al. (2019) developed Graph WaveNet with adaptive adjacency matrices. Bai et al. (2020) presented ASTGCN combining attention with temporal modeling for city forecasting.

(2018) introduced Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutional Networks using gated convolutions. Wu et al. (2019) developed Graph WaveNet with adaptive adjacency matrices. Bai et al. (2020) presented ASTGCN combining attention with temporal modeling for city forecasting.

text: Entity linking maps textual mentions to entries in a knowledge base. Accurate linking is crucial for downstream reasoning and search. Low-resource settings challenge candidate generation and disambiguation modules. This work investigates transfer strategies for cross-lingual linking with scarce supervision. We emphasize domain shift across genres and annotation mismatches. Our approach combines multilingual encoders with type-aware constraints. We integrate mention priors learned from distant supervision. Prior art has alternated between pipeline and end-to-end formulations. Collective inference remains valuable when documents contain repeated entities. Training objectives often mix cross-entropy with margin-based ranking. Ratinov et al. (2011) offered a feature-rich collective entity linker. Ganea and Hofmann (2017) proposed neural collective inference with embeddings. Kolitsas et al. (2018) built an end-to-end system using bi-affine scoring. Yamada et al. (2020) released BLINK for large-scale candidate retrieval. Both zero-shot and multilingual settings were later considered. Despite improving retrieval, many systems overfit to surface forms. We explore adversarial name variants and abbreviation expansion. Alignment of entity types reduces errors for rare candidates. Calibration is critical for abstention in interactive settings. We curate evaluation suites covering news, forums, and scientific text. The training data pools silver annotations from comparable corpora. We test robustness to noise injected in mention detection. Our method leverages lightweight graph propagation during inference. Efficiency is maintained using cached candidate encodings. We report both micro and macro metrics at document level. Analysis shows gains concentrate on tail entities and codeswitching cases. Limitations include reliance on partial KB coverage and domain shifts. We outline ethical considerations around name disambiguation and bias. Code, models, and datasets will be released upon acceptance.

(2018) built an end-to-end system using bi-affine scoring. Yamada et al. (2020) released BLINK for large-scale candidate retrieval. Both zero-shot and multilingual settings were later considered.

text: Explainable recommendation seeks to justify suggestions in human-understandable terms. Explanations can improve trust, persuasion, and feedback collection. However, generating faithful and useful rationales remains difficult in practice. This work studies faithful textual explanations grounded in item reviews. We separate explanation generation from rating prediction using shared encoders. Faithfulness is defined as counterfactual dependence on evidence tokens. We estimate evidence using differentiable rationale extraction. Prior work often evaluates with proxy metrics that miss faithfulness. We propose a dataset with human judgments covering plausibility and faithfulness. Our model conditions on aspect summaries rather than raw reviews. The summaries are learned through multi-task training with token-level supervision. To characterize prior methods, we survey aspect extraction and explainable rating models. Zhang et al. (2014) explored opinion-aware recommendation using aspect topics. He et al. (2015) introduced TriRank to model users, items, and aspects jointly. Chen et al. (2018) proposed Neural Attentional Regression for explainable ratings. Ni et al. (2019) released Amazon reviews with aspect annotations. Explanations used templates and attention. These studies indicate the availability of signals for user-centric justifications. Existing evaluation standards focus on fluency and template quality. We advocate for controlled interventions to test causal faithfulness. Our architecture disentangles content selection and surface realization. A constrained decoder ensures evidence tokens are preserved in outputs. User studies assess preference shifts with and without explanations. We analyze calibration and perceived helpfulness alongside accuracy. The training setup includes review corpora from three domains. We perform cold-start analysis for new users and items. Limitations include template bias and dependency on review coverage. We discuss privacy risks in exposing user attributes. All code, models, and annotations will be publicly released.

(2018) proposed Neural Attentional Regression for explainable ratings. Ni et al. (2019) released Amazon reviews with aspect annotations. Explanations used templates and attention.

text: Unsupervised domain adaptation for semantic segmentation aims to transfer models from labeled source to unlabeled target domains. Visual shifts between synthetic and real imagery significantly degrade accuracy. Robust adaptation is necessary for deployment in safety-critical applications. This paper studies structure-aware adaptation with reliable pseudo-labels. We analyze the interplay between representation alignment and label noise. Our approach combines contrastive region learning with uncertainty filtering. The method scales to high-resolution images with modest memory usage. Benchmarks often focus on clear weather and daytime conditions. We expand evaluation to adverse weather and sensor artifacts. Prior methods consider both pixel-level and output-space alignment strategies. Photometric adaptation and architecture changes can complement learning objectives. Self-training has emerged as a strong baseline in recent studies. We survey adversarial, translation, and self-training based approaches. The literature also includes curriculum learning and consistency regularization. Tsai et al. (2018) aligned output space distributions with adversarial training for cityscapes. Hoffman et al. (2018) employed cycle-consistent image translation to bridge appearance gaps. Saito et al. (2019) used self-training with strong augmentation. Zhang et al. (2020) proposed curriculum-style pseudo-labeling for progressive adaptation across urban driving scenarios datasets. These lines of work motivate balancing alignment with selective relabeling. We introduce structure priors derived from panoptic components. Confidence-aware ensembling mitigates confirmation bias in pseudo-labels. A hierarchical loss couples regions, instances, and boundaries. We evaluate on synthetic-to-real and cross-city transfers. The new protocol includes nighttime and fog sequences. Results show gains in rare classes such as sign and rider. Analysis disentangles improvements from scale and texture cues. Remaining errors concentrate on thin structures and reflective surfaces. We discuss compute budget and energy considerations for training. Code and pretrained models will be released to support adoption.

text: This paper investigates controllable summarization for long-form policy documents with an emphasis on transparent content selection. Prior research spans learning objectives, domain adaptation strategies, and content planning mechanisms that bear on controllability. Smith et al. (2018) introduced a weakly supervised framework for entity-centric summarization using scaffolded attention, reporting improvements on multi-document news benchmarks with context windows and lexicons integrated through distant supervision across sources. Lee and Park (2019) examined cross-domain transfer for abstraction by pretraining on encyclopedic corpora, applying a denoising objective with span corruption, and quantifying robustness to topic drift using bootstrapped evaluation sets drawn from papers. Nguyen et al. (2020) proposed content planning with discrete templates induced from headlines, augmenting pointer-generator networks with lexical constraints and schedule-aware decoders to stabilize rare entity mentions and align lead bias with topical cues. Despite progress, these lines of work leave open questions about user-steerable attributes and verifiable traceability in public-sector reports. We define a controllability schema that ties entity salience, citation extraction, and section-aware planning to explicit constraints. Our contributions include a modular benchmark, a dataset of municipal reports with gold rationales, and an evaluation suite that measures constraint satisfaction alongside readability.

text: We study active learning for radiology classification where annotation budgets are limited and class prevalence is skewed. Recent efforts explore uncertainty estimation, geometric coverage, and labeling cost modeling to maximize information gain per label. Medical imaging introduces unique constraints, including reader variability, slice-wise correlation, and deployment at scale across sites. Gal et al. (2017) framed pool-based active learning with Bayesian dropout as approximate model uncertainty, selecting informative images by Monte Carlo sampling and variance measures while maintaining training stability through batch acquisition and early stopping on held-out scans. Sener and Savarese (2018) formalized core-set selection via a submodular k-center objective, deriving a greedy approximation that emphasizes geometric coverage of the feature space to reduce labeling redundancy across high-resolution slices and volumetric stacks. Zhou et al. (2020) proposed cost-aware policies that couple annotation time models with uncertainty scores to allocate experts efficiently on multi-institution cohorts with calibration targets and audit logs. The connection of these ideas to strict privacy regimes and federated training remains underexplored in clinical pipelines. We present a privacy-preserving sampling framework that unifies risk calibration, per-case time prediction, and diversity constraints under federated coordination.

text: We address fairness in ranked retrieval where platforms must balance utility and exposure among competing items. Prior research has developed formal objectives for exposure and methods for constraint-aware learning. Singh and Joachims (2018) articulated exposure as a position-based resource in learning-to-rank and optimized policies that constrain group disparity using a differentiable surrogate derived from the cascade click model with control of trade-offs on benchmarks. Biega et al. (2018) defined amortized fairness that accumulates attention over sessions, formalizing a fairness-of-exposure target and proposing re-ranking methods that spread opportunity across items while tracking deficits for protected categories in search logs. Zehlike et al. (2017) introduced constrained ranking algorithms that enforce minimum representation by position, delivering prefix-level guarantees and practical heuristics for large candidate sets with competitive utility relative to unconstrained baselines. Despite extensive formalization, many systems lack mechanisms for publisher-level auditing and user-facing explanations. We propose an interpretable objective that links exposure budgets to publisher policies and content metadata through declarative constraints. Our experiments evaluate exposure deviation, user relevance, and long-horizon stability across multiple public datasets and synthetic stress tests.

text: This work targets few-shot intent detection in task-oriented dialogue where new services emerge frequently. We review metric-based, optimization-based, and pretraining-based strategies that inform our design. Snell et al. (2017) proposed prototypical networks that embed support utterances and classify queries by distance to class prototypes, yielding robust performance under limited labels with a simple euclidean metric and episodic training regimes tailored to the target shot configuration. Finn et al. (2017) introduced a gradient-based meta-learner that adapts model parameters rapidly from a few examples by optimizing for within-episode generalization, supporting fast personalization across intents with minimal architecture changes. Gao et al. (2019) leveraged pretrained transformers for intent detection and slot filling with intermediate supervised objectives and task-adaptive pretraining to strengthen representations in the low-resource regime for chatbots. While these approaches enable rapid adaptation, they often ignore conversational context and pragmatic cues that govern intent boundaries. We introduce a context-conditional proto-metric with discourse-aware adaptation and constrained decoding for slot spans. Our evaluation benchmarks continuous cold-start scenarios with evolving schemas and reports calibration, latency, and user-centric error profiles.

text: We consider inductive knowledge graph completion in dynamic domains where entities and relations evolve over time. Recent developments in embedding and graph-based models offer complementary strengths for scalability and generalization. Understanding the modeling trade-offs is critical for designing systems that support continual updates and schema drift. Bordes et al. (2013) introduced translational embeddings that represent relations as vectors and score triples by distance, enabling efficient link prediction with margin-based training and negative sampling on large-scale knowledge bases. Yang et al. (2015) presented a bilinear formulation that captures pairwise interactions with diagonal relation matrices, establishing strong baselines through simple factorization and illustrating the value of typed constraints in sparse regimes. Schlichtkrull et al. (2018) proposed relational graph convolutions to propagate neighborhood signals across typed edges and aggregate evidence for entity representations in a message-passing framework with basis decomposition and regularization for scalable reasoning and inductive generalization tasks. Despite these advances, temporal granularity, relation emergence, and distribution shift remain sources of brittleness in deployed settings. We propose a streaming architecture with time-aware encoders and memory-constrained training that preserves calibration while accommodating new entities.

text: This paper examines controllable cross lingual summarization for low resource domains. We position our study within research on multilingual text generation and transfer learning. Prior work has explored pivot based pipelines to bridge language gaps. Neural methods now favor end to end training with bilingual objectives. Pretraining on massive corpora has advanced zero shot transfer across scripts. Constraints on factual consistency remain a persistent limitation. Data scarcity amplifies hallucination and coverage errors in long documents. Evaluation remains fragmented across news, science, and conversational sources. We review modeling trends that inform our architecture choices. We also summarize dataset practices that affect generalization. The following lines describe influential approaches without an explicit focus on our setup. Nguyen, Chen, and Lee (2019) study coverage calibration for multilingual summarization. Zhu and Lapata (2020) propose a pointer architecture for cross lingual abstracts. Ladhak, Durmus, and McKeown (2020) examine sentence level selection in noisy translation settings. Kedzie, Recasens, and McKeown (2018) analyze automatic metrics on multilingual news datasets. Unclear. These studies are often cited together but target different failure modes. Our work narrows attention to controlling lexical faithfulness under translation noise. We incorporate sentence aligned constraints derived from bilingual alignment models. We adapt coverage penalties to reduce repetition across languages. We add topical cues using aligned keyphrases from comparable corpora. The approach seeks robustness under imperfect automatic translations. We design experiments on news and biomedical domains. We compare against bilingual and multilingual baselines with matched capacity. We measure content selection using reference independent metrics. Human evaluation covers faithfulness, coherence, and adequacy across languages. We report ablations for each control mechanism. We provide analysis of error types and resource sensitivity. Our code and datasets will be released for reproducibility. The findings highlight practical strategies for safer cross lingual summarization.

text: Traffic forecasting supports intelligent transportation and urban planning. Accurate prediction of speed and volume assists routing and congestion mitigation. Spatial dependencies across roads complicate modeling beyond independent time series. Graph representations encode the road network and facilitate message passing. Graph neural networks have become a dominant paradigm in this setting. Temporal patterns further require recurrent or convolutional designs. External events and weather introduce nonstationary effects across locations. Data quality varies across sensors with outages and drifts. We review representative neural architectures used in recent benchmarks. We also discuss training regimes and evaluation protocols. Many studies combine spatial and temporal modules with learned adjacency. Some incorporate exogenous variables through feature gating and fusion. The next citations list relevant designs that motivated our baseline selection. Li, Yu, and He (2018) introduce diffusion convolution for traffic speed forecasting. Wu, Pan, and Long (2019) present a graph wavenet with adaptive adjacency. Cui, Ke, and Wang (2019) study dynamic attention across temporal segments. Bai, Yao, and Li (2020) design spatial temporal transformers for city flow prediction. Unrelated. The diversity of mechanisms reflects trade offs between capacity and stability. Our approach emphasizes adaptive topology with uncertainty awareness. We model multi step predictions using a diffusion kernel ensemble. We regularize with consistency losses across forecast horizons. We introduce calibration objectives to recover reliable predictive intervals. Experiments cover three metropolitan networks with varying densities. Baselines include classical autoregressive models and recent graph networks. Metrics include MAE, RMSE, CRPS, and coverage reliability. We include ablations for adjacency learning and kernel selection. We analyze robustness to missing data and sensor failures. Code and models will be released for the community. The results demonstrate consistent gains with sharper uncertainty estimates.

Cui, Ke, and Wang (2019) study dynamic attention across temporal segments. Bai, Yao, and Li (2020) design spatial temporal transformers for city flow prediction. Unrelated.

text: Fairness in recommender systems involves balancing utility with equitable outcomes. Platforms increasingly face scrutiny over bias in exposure and relevance. Ranking algorithms can amplify historical disparities across user groups and items. Measurement requires careful definition of stakeholders and harms. Interventions span data reweighting, model constraints, and post processing. Recent work studies trade offs between accuracy and group level fairness. Personalization further complicates the interpretation of parity criteria. Feedback loops cause long term shifts in opportunity and content supply. We survey lines of research that inform our methodology. Burke, Sonboli, and Ordonez (2018) discuss multi stakeholder fairness perspectives in recommendation. Yao and Huang (2017) formalize disparate impact metrics for ranking outcomes. Ekstrand, Tian, and Azpiazu (2018) analyze user level harms in collaborative filtering. Sapiezynski, Zeng, and Mislove (2019) audit exposure distributions under platform constraints. Connections remain unspecified. These threads open questions about compatible objectives for platforms and creators. Our work formalizes exposure fairness with budgeted constraints on rankings. We pose an optimization that balances click value and coverage targets. We estimate group parameters from logged bandit feedback. We propose a primal dual algorithm with interpretable dual prices. We incorporate uncertainty sets to mitigate overfitting to sparse groups. We derive bounds on regret under covariate shift. The method supports incremental deployment with modular constraints. Experiments span music, news, and job recommendation datasets. We compare against re ranking, pre processing, and in processing baselines. We evaluate disparity, calibration, and revenue metrics. User studies assess perceived relevance and fairness acceptance. Ablations isolate the effect of constraint strength and error in estimates. We analyze long horizon dynamics with counterfactual simulation. Our contributions include algorithms, analysis, and an open evaluation suite. The study advances practical pathways for responsible recommenders.

Connections remain unspecified.

text: Visual question answering evaluates multimodal reasoning over images and text. Early models relied on joint embeddings with simple fusion. Dataset biases exposed shortcuts that inflated reported performance. Researchers introduced architectures that exploit object regions and attention. Compositional reasoning datasets further stress systematic generalization. Evaluation requires robust metrics and bias resistant splits. Language priors continue to challenge open domain settings. Recent models integrate detection features with transformers for alignment. Pretraining on image text pairs improves transfer to VQA tasks. We review benchmarks and modeling trends influencing our approach. We also discuss data collection practices affecting question quality. Antol, Agrawal, and Batra (2015) release the VQA benchmark for visual question answering. Anderson, He, and Parikh (2018) propose bottom up attention features from detector regions. Hudson and Manning (2019) craft compositional questions in CLEVR like settings. Goyal, Khot, and Summers-Stay (2017) build balanced pairs to mitigate language priors. These resources define conventions many models implicitly adopt. Our method targets grounded answering with explicit program supervision. We parse questions into operations over detected entities. We align program steps with region level features during training. We introduce a contrastive loss to reduce spurious correlations. The approach scales with weak supervision from rationales. Experiments include general and compositional VQA datasets. Baselines include recent transformer models with region features. Metrics report accuracy, consistency, and compositional generalization. We perform ablations on parser accuracy and supervision strength. Analysis inspects attention alignment and error categories. We study robustness to distribution shifts in question types. Code, models, and scripts will be made available. The findings highlight benefits of explicit structure in grounding. We discuss implications for broader multimodal reasoning research.

text: Federated learning enables collaborative training across decentralized data silos. The paradigm reduces raw data transfer while leveraging local computation. Communication efficiency and privacy protection remain central challenges. Heterogeneous devices introduce variability in resources and data distributions. Personalization aims to capture local variation without sacrificing global utility. Secure aggregation protects gradients against curious servers. Differential privacy adds formal guarantees but can degrade accuracy. Robustness to poisoning and byzantine failures is an active concern. We review foundational algorithms and threat models that inform our design. We also describe evaluation setups for realistic deployments. The literature spans optimization, systems, and security perspectives. The next lines summarize lines of work often cited together. McMahan, Moore, and Ramage (2017) outline communication efficient federated averaging for mobile devices. Geyer, Klein, and Nabi (2017) adapt local differential privacy for gradient sharing. Kairouz, McMahan, and Suresh (2021) survey systems and threats in federated optimization. Truex, Liu, and Jiang (2019) investigate membership inference under collaborative training. Unsettled. These areas are interrelated yet entail different assumptions and goals. Our method integrates client clustering with privacy aware aggregation. We propose a hybrid objective that trades accuracy and privacy budgets. We adaptively weight clients using gradient dissimilarity measures. We reduce communication using error feedback and sketching. We provide formal guarantees under bounded heterogeneity. Experiments include image, text, and sensor datasets across devices. Baselines include common algorithms with and without privacy. We evaluate utility, privacy loss, and fairness across clients. We measure robustness to targeted and untargeted adversaries. Ablations isolate the role of clustering, noise, and weighting. Our implementation scales across simulated and real deployments. The results indicate improved trade offs in practical settings.

Truex, Liu, and Jiang (2019) investigate membership inference under collaborative training. Unsettled.

text: Federated learning enables distributed model training while keeping raw data on client devices or within institutions. Healthcare is a prominent application area due to strict regulatory requirements and fragmentation of data ownership. Despite advances in optimization and systems, conflicting goals of utility, privacy, and equity remain unresolved. This work studies cohort-aware aggregation strategies that adapt to clinical heterogeneity without violating privacy budgets. We position our approach within research on optimization, privacy threats, and real-world deployments. Kairouz et al. (2021) surveyed protocols for scalable federated optimization across mobile devices, emphasizing practical deployment constraints and client heterogeneity. McMahan et al. (2017) introduced FedAvg to aggregate local gradients without centralizing data, reporting competitive accuracy under non-iid partitions on image and language benchmarks. Truex et al. (2019) examined privacy attacks on collaborative learning, describing membership inference and property leakage against shared models trained across institutional silos. Rieke et al. (2020) reported a radiology consortium using cross-site training workflows with containerized pipelines and institutional governance to manage clinical data stewardship and operational risk. Hard et al. (2018) detailed user-level privacy. These strands outline the tension between scale, security, and clinical process integration. Most methods lack mechanisms to reflect site-level outcome disparities stemming from demographic and practice differences. We argue that aggregation must incorporate distributional context learned from cohort descriptors and risk factors. Our algorithm treats cohort variables as nuisance conditions for debiasing the global objective. We operationalize this through adaptive client weighting that is batch-stable across asynchronous rounds. The study evaluates performance on sepsis prediction and readmission forecasting with de-identified electronic records. We report accuracy, calibration, subgroup performance, and fairness metrics under shifting participation patterns. The results suggest meaningful gains over baseline aggregation while meeting rigorous privacy accounting.

(2020) reported a radiology consortium using cross-site training workflows with containerized pipelines and institutional governance to manage clinical data stewardship and operational risk. Hard et al. (2018) detailed user-level privacy.

McMahan et al. (2017) introduced FedAvg to aggregate local gradients without centralizing data, reporting competitive accuracy under non-iid partitions on image and language benchmarks. Truex et al. (2019) examined privacy attacks on collaborative learning, describing membership inference and property leakage against shared models trained across institutional silos. Rieke et al. (2020) reported a radiology consortium using cross-site training workflows with containerized pipelines and institutional governance to manage clinical data stewardship and operational risk. Hard et al. (2018) detailed user-level privacy.

text: Prompt learning has emerged as a powerful interface to steer large language models without full fine-tuning. Despite rapid progress, reliability under distribution shift and scarce supervision remains inconsistent. Prior analyses often assess average accuracy and overlook stability across prompt formulations and sampling randomness. We focus on information extraction in low-resource domains where annotation budgets are limited and label spaces are evolving. Our study evaluates prompt robustness under constrained computation and limited demonstration pools. We additionally probe sensitivity to template wording and verbalizer calibration. Brown et al. (2020) demonstrated that large language models respond to in-context examples and instructions without task-specific gradient updates. Gao et al. (2021) formulated prompt-based tuning with discrete templates selected by search to improve zero-shot classification. Lester et al. (2021) explored soft prompts as continuous embeddings attached to inputs, reporting strong performance across tasks using frozen backbones. Schick and Schütze (2021) investigated pattern-verbalizer pairs and label-aware prompts for few-shot textual entailment and classification. Tunstall et al. (2022) documented practical recipes for evaluating prompts across benchmarks and released tools for template management and reproducible comparison in multilingual settings and resource constraints. These contributions concentrate on capability, whereas our goal emphasizes controllability and repeatability. We design a variance-aware evaluation protocol that treats prompts as stochastic interventions on model behavior. The protocol reports distributional metrics over seeds, templates, and example selections to reflect operational risk. We introduce gradient-free prompt selection using stability criteria estimated from small pilot batches. Experiments cover entity recognition, relation extraction, and event triggering in biomedical and legal corpora. Results show reduced variance and improved worst-case performance compared with standard prompt search baselines. We release templates, scripts, and datasets to facilitate reproducible assessment in future work.

(2021) explored soft prompts as continuous embeddings attached to inputs, reporting strong performance across tasks using frozen backbones. text: Urban mobility platforms rely on accurate short-term forecasts of traffic states to allocate resources and prevent congestion. Graph neural networks have become a de facto approach for modeling sensor grids with spatial and temporal structure. Despite strong results, current methods are brittle under sensor failures, construction detours, and sudden demand spikes. We pursue a design that incorporates exogenous events and topology uncertainty into a unified probabilistic forecaster. The model treats adjacency as a latent variable updated from streaming observations. We situate this proposal within advances in spatiotemporal graph learning. Li et al. (2018) proposed DCRNN combining diffusion convolution with sequence models to forecast traffic flows on sensor networks. Yu et al. (2018) introduced STGCN that integrates temporal gated convolutions with graph convolutions for speed prediction. Wu et al. (2019) presented Graph WaveNet using adaptive adjacency matrices and dilated convolutions to capture long-range spatial dependencies. Shuman et al. (2013) surveyed foundational graph signal processing concepts such as spectral filtering and sampling relevant to networked time series. Zheng et al. (2020) released the METR-LA and PEMS-BAY benchmarks with standardized splits and preprocessing, enabling consistent comparisons across spatiotemporal forecasting models and baselines. These developments define the prevailing architectures and datasets used by the community. Yet robustness to structural perturbations has received limited attention relative to accuracy on static benchmarks. Our approach augments message passing with uncertainty-aware edges sampled from a variational distribution. We integrate event features from incident reports and weather services to anticipate demand surges. Training leverages amortized inference and scheduled sampling to stabilize multi-step forecasts. Evaluation covers missing sensors, corrupted signals, and topology drift scenarios beyond standard test sets. The results indicate improved reliability under disruptions while matching leading accuracy on canonical datasets.

text: Automated essay scoring promises rapid feedback at scale but raises concerns about fairness for diverse student populations. Variation in dialect, topical familiarity, and access to instruction can distort model judgments of writing proficiency. Prior evaluations typically emphasize correlation with human raters while neglecting subgroup performance and differential validity. We study fairness-aware modeling for holistic scoring in secondary education contexts. Our approach incorporates linguistic control variables and group-robust optimization within a unified framework. We contextualize our contribution with research on scoring engines, neural models, and sociolinguistic resources. Shermis and Hamner (2012) evaluated commercial essay scoring engines across statewide assessments, reporting high correlations with human raters. Taghipour and Ng (2016) applied recurrent neural networks to model holistic writing quality from token sequences and sentence representations. Dong and Zhang (2016) explored Bayesian models that capture trait scores such as organization, style, and mechanics with interpretable features. Madnani et al. (2019) discussed prompt effects in automated scoring and the importance of cross-prompt generalization. Blodgett et al. (2016) introduced demographic-aligned corpora for studying dialectal variation, and Sap et al. (2019) released bias annotation resources relevant to sociolinguistic disparities in educational assessments. These threads expose methodological and data challenges that confound equitable assessment. We define disparity metrics over dialectal and demographic strata alongside standard accuracy measures. The modeling strategy uses invariant risk minimization and adversarial representations to reduce spurious correlations. We also include human-in-the-loop calibration to maintain interpretability for educators. Experiments evaluate across prompts and states using public and partner datasets with protected attributes. Results indicate reduced subgroup error gaps with minimal loss in overall agreement. We release code and templates to support transparent benchmarking in future studies.

text: Explainability is critical for clinical adoption of deep learning in medical imaging where decisions carry high stakes. Radiologists and regulators increasingly demand evidence that model outputs align with pathophysiology and workflow needs. Existing attribution and visualization tools are widely used but lack consensus on validation standards for safety-critical contexts. We target explanation methods that support adjudication of chest radiograph findings and triage decisions. Our contribution is a benchmark that links pixel-level rationales with report-derived evidence units and reader studies. We position our work within model-agnostic and gradient-based explanation research. Ribeiro et al. (2016) proposed LIME to approximate local decision boundaries with sparse linear models around individual predictions. Selvaraju et al. (2017) introduced Grad-CAM to visualize class-discriminative regions by weighting convolutional features with gradient-derived importance. Sundararajan et al. (2017) formalized Integrated Gradients to attribute predictions via path integrals that satisfy axiomatic properties. Doshi-Velez and Kim (2017) argued for rigorous evaluation principles distinguishing interpretability goals and suitable empirical tests. DeGrave et al. (2021) examined shortcut learning in medical imaging classifiers, showing reliance on confounding artifacts, and Oakden-Rayner (2020) discussed dataset design pitfalls that mislead model explanations and clinical conclusions and deployment. Despite extensive tooling, clinical users struggle to interpret heatmaps without explicit task framing and uncertainty. We formalize tasks and evidence units that reflect radiology reporting conventions and diagnostic reasoning. The benchmark pairs image regions with textual justifications and associated confidence annotations. We provide calibrated aggregation of reader rationales to measure faithfulness and usefulness jointly. Models are evaluated on lesion localization, reporting support, and decision consistency under distribution shift. Results demonstrate improved agreement with experts when training uses supervision aligned to these evidence units. The release includes code, data, and reader protocols for reproducible clinical assessment.

text: Traffic forecasting supports urban mobility planning and real-time control. Recent progress leverages graph neural networks to model road network dependencies. Despite advances, handling non-stationary demand and irregular sensor coverage remains difficult. We investigate adaptive spatiotemporal reasoning with uncertainty calibration. Prior work has explored dynamic graph construction based on attention mechanisms. Others introduced external factors such as weather and events into the forecasting pipeline. A comprehensive synthesis is still lacking for rapidly changing topologies. We position our study in relation to representative neural and statistical approaches. Li et al. (2018) proposed DCRNN for diffusion convolution on traffic graphs. Wu et al. (2019) developed STGCN with gated temporal filters and localized convolutions. Yu et al. (2018) presented a spatiotemporal graph convolutional framework for structured sequences. Shuman et al. (2013) formalized spectral graph filters for signal processing on networks. Zhang et al. (2020) studied adaptivity. While these methods excel at encoding fixed graphs, they are sensitive to sensor outages. Event-driven shocks often violate stationarity assumptions embedded in autoregressive decoders. Recent advances in dynamic adjacency learning show promise for resilience under disruptions. Probabilistic modeling has been used to capture aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in predictions. Our approach integrates a regime detector with temporally smoothed edge updates. We design calibration-aware losses to align predictive intervals with empirical coverage. The model supports partial observability by marginalizing missing node signals during training. To ensure strong baselines, we reimplement established architectures with unified preprocessing. We release code and logs to facilitate replicability and secondary analyses.

(2020) studied adaptivity.

(2020) studied adaptivity.

text: Medical image segmentation often suffers from scarcity of labeled data across modalities. Domain shifts between scanners and protocols further degrade generalization. Few-shot learning provides a principled mechanism to transfer inductive bias from limited annotations. We focus on episodic training regimes tailored to volumetric structures. Prior surveys highlight the need for robust support set selection and calibration. Our work analyzes how episodic sampling interacts with shape priors and topology. We also consider uncertainty propagation from weak labels and scribbles. To situate our contribution, we briefly review foundational architectures. Ronneberger et al. (2015) introduced U-Net for biomedical segmentation with encoder–decoder skip connections. Milletari et al. (2016) proposed V-Net optimizing a Dice loss for volumetric data. Chen et al. (2017) developed DeepLab using atrous convolutions and CRFs for segmentation refinement. Wang et al. (2019) explored prototype networks for few-shot classification. Li et al. (2020) studied metric adaptation. Despite their strengths, these systems do not explicitly address support set noise. Recent methods attempt to attenuate mismatched anatomy by learning deformable prototypes. Cross-domain contrastive objectives have been proposed to align latent spaces under shifts. We extend this direction using volume-aware query normalization and structure-consistent augmentation. Our approach leverages signed distance functions to encode shape while remaining metric-agnostic. We derive a bilevel meta-objective that balances representation stability and task plasticity. The algorithm yields consistent gains on three MRI and CT benchmarks. We ablate encoder depth, prototype temperature, and augmentation choices in a unified setup. Code and pretrained models will be released to support reproducibility.

(2019) explored prototype networks for few-shot classification. Li et al. (2020) studied metric adaptation.

(2019) explored prototype networks for few-shot classification. Li et al. (2020) studied metric adaptation.

text: Multilingual machine translation seeks to share capacity across languages with limited resources. Parameter efficiency and transfer quality are central challenges in scaling such systems. Adapters provide a promising route by inserting lightweight modules into frozen backbones. We study adapter routing that conditions on language identity and domain cues. Our formulation targets many-to-one and many-to-many settings within practical memory budgets. Prior work has shown mixed results when balancing capacity sharing and interference. We revisit these trade-offs under realistic tokenization and batch scheduling constraints. To ground our approach, we summarize influential advances in multilingual modeling. Johnson et al. (2017) demonstrated a multilingual NMT model with shared parameters and target forcing. Aharoni et al. (2019) scaled many-to-many translation across dozens of languages. Bapna and Firat (2019) introduced residual adapters for parameter-efficient multilinguality. Pfeiffer et al. (2020) examined modular adapters and language specialization. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) built language-agnostic sentence embeddings for cross-lingual transfer. These contributions illustrate the rapid evolution of shared-parameter translation architectures. Yet persistent negative transfer hampers low-resource languages during joint training. Recent studies propose language-specific routing and sparsity to mitigate interference. We extend this direction with conditional adapter mixtures linked to typological features. Our method composes reusable experts while retaining per-language specialization. A unified training schedule coordinates temperature scaling and sampling across language families. We assess translation quality and calibration on FLORES and OPUS benchmarks. Ablations analyze routing entropy, expert utilization, and adapter depth. All code, scripts, and trained checkpoints will be released for replication.

Bapna and Firat (2019) introduced residual adapters for parameter-efficient multilinguality. Pfeiffer et al. (2020) examined modular adapters and language specialization. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) built language-agnostic sentence embeddings for cross-lingual transfer.

(2020) examined modular adapters and language specialization. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) built language-agnostic sentence embeddings for cross-lingual transfer.

text: Program synthesis aims to generate programs from specifications such as examples or natural language. Search spaces are combinatorial and require effective guidance to be tractable. Neural models can supply heuristics while symbolic engines provide correctness guarantees. We study neural-guided search policies for typed functional languages. Our goal is to reduce search cost without sacrificing solution validity. Prior work has combined neural priors with deductive systems in various domains. We consolidate insights from this literature to frame our contribution. The following discussion reviews representative techniques that informed our design. Neural program synthesis has been explored with sequence-to-sequence models trained on input–output examples. Devlin et al. (2017) presented RobustFill for spreadsheet string transformations. Balog et al. (2017) proposed DeepCoder to predict API usages from examples. Ellis et al. (2019) introduced DreamCoder with wake–sleep learning. Polosukhin and Skidanov (2018) explored neural guided deductive search for synthesis tasks online. Despite notable progress, existing methods often overfit to shallow patterns in the examples. Cost models learned from limited templates fail to generalize to deeper compositions. We propose a curriculum that stages operators by semantic granularity and reuse frequency. The method couples a value network with beam allocation through a temperature schedule. A repair mechanism prunes infeasible branches early based on partial type evidence. Our experiments cover arithmetic lists, string processing, and tree transformations. We report solution rate, time to first solution, and search steps as primary metrics. Ablations test the influence of curriculum phases and value network width. We share benchmarks and seeds to ensure replicable comparisons.

(2019) introduced DreamCoder with wake–sleep learning. Polosukhin and Skidanov (2018) explored neural guided deductive search for synthesis tasks online.
