(2019) introduced DreamCoder with wake–sleep learning. Polosukhin and Skidanov (2018) explored neural guided deductive search for synthesis tasks online.

text: Credit scoring models increasingly rely on complex machine learning. Regulatory frameworks demand transparency and fairness for high-stakes decisions. Explanations help institutions validate risk policies and support consumer rights. Yet many explanation tools were not designed for strict auditing workflows. We study explanation consistency and actionability in credit decision pipelines. Our setting considers scorecards, gradient boosting, and neural networks trained on tabular loans. We evaluate methods for both global monitoring and individualized recourse. The discussion first outlines representative explanation approaches in the literature. We emphasize approaches that have seen practical adoption across financial services. Ribeiro et al. (2016) introduced LIME to approximate local decision boundaries with sparse linear models. Lundberg and Lee (2017) developed SHAP based on additive feature attributions. Sundararajan et al. (2017) proposed Integrated Gradients for path-based importance scores. Wachter et al. (2017) formulated counterfactual explanations for recourse. Karimi et al. (2020) studied algorithmic recourse under feasibility constraints assumptions. These techniques vary widely in stability, faithfulness, and susceptibility to manipulation. Post-hoc surrogates may deviate from the true model around decision thresholds. Feature attributions can mislead when correlations induce spurious explanatory mass. Counterfactual generation without feasibility models suggests actions borrowers cannot take. We propose a protocol that couples attribution smoothing with constraint-aware recourse search. The protocol computes credible intervals for explanations and validates proposed actions. Benchmarks on two credit datasets assess consistency across model classes and time. We release documentation and templates to support adoption by compliance teams.

(2020) studied algorithmic recourse under feasibility constraints assumptions.

(2017) formulated counterfactual explanations for recourse. Karimi et al. (2020) studied algorithmic recourse under feasibility constraints assumptions.

text: Research on multimodal sarcasm detection has expanded with the growth of social platforms rich in text and images. Early approaches relied on textual features such as n-grams, punctuation, and emoticons to capture ironic cues. Neural models brought stronger representations by integrating pretrained language models with attention over context. Image signals complement text by highlighting incongruities between captions and visual content. Datasets spanning Twitter, Instagram, and Reddit have enabled cross-domain comparisons and robustness testing. Multitask setups have also been proposed to link sentiment, stance, and sarcasm signals. Our work focuses on learning cross-modal inconsistency under limited supervision. We examine prior corpora and objectives relevant to this aim. Kim et al. (2019) introduced a contrastive loss for pairing sarcastic captions with conflicting images. Gupta and Saha (2020) compiled Reddit posts displaying sentiment reversals across threads to infer irony without manual labels. Oprea and Magdy (2019) studied reply structures that precede sarcasm in conversational networks on Twitter. Sulis et al. (2016) analyzed lexicon-level polarity shifts in figurative language using crowdsourced annotations from diverse communities. Muresan (2015) examined metaphor in hashtags. These disparate observations leave open how to align textual contrast with pixel-level evidence in a unified formulation. Cross-modal attention can emphasize mismatches, but it often collapses when strong priors dominate visual features. We propose a calibration module that reweights token- and region-level activations based on inconsistency scores. The module is trained jointly with a caption-image encoder using a margin-based loss. Our dataset augments existing posts with synthetic counterfactuals that flip the intended meaning. We report comprehensive ablations across imbalance ratios and domain shifts. The evaluation includes human preference studies for interpretability of highlighted regions. We release code and models to support reproducibility and downstream exploration. Ethical considerations include potential misuse for adversarial content targeting and privacy risks from scraped media.

(2016) analyzed lexicon-level polarity shifts in figurative language using crowdsourced annotations from diverse communities. Muresan (2015) examined metaphor in hashtags.

text: Traffic forecasting underpins intelligent transportation systems and urban planning. Accurate predictions must account for complex spatial and temporal dependencies among sensors. Classical autoregressive methods struggle with nonlinearities and missing observations. Deep learning models with graph structure have become the dominant paradigm. They leverage connectivity to propagate information through a network-aware representation. Yet the choice of graph and temporal operator remains an open question. We focus on robust forecasting when topology and demand both shift. Prior research provides architectural priors and datasets that we build upon. We review key neural approaches in recent literature. Wu et al. (2019) proposed Graph WaveNet to capture dynamic diffusion processes on road networks. Li et al. (2018) introduced DCRNN with random walk modeling for sensor time series. Yu et al. (2018) designed STGCN combining temporal convolutions with spectral graph filters on transit graphs. Pan et al. (2020) studied meta-learning for cold-start prediction using limited trajectory data. Zheng et al. (2021) explored adaptive adjacency estimation from sparse urban GPS records. These studies emphasize architecture, while deployment often faces unstable sensor availability. Training-time missingness differs from long-range outages that occur after installation. Our approach estimates uncertainty-aware edges and schedules reconstructions for absent nodes. We introduce a self-conditioning decoder that reduces exposure bias across horizons. Experiments span four cities with varying congestion and construction patterns. We measure accuracy, calibration, and stability under synthetic and real outages. The results show consistent gains without additional latency or memory. We release code, data splits, and detailed protocols for reproducibility.

Yu et al. (2018) designed STGCN combining temporal convolutions with spectral graph filters on transit graphs. Pan et al. (2020) studied meta-learning for cold-start prediction using limited trajectory data. Zheng et al. (2021) explored adaptive adjacency estimation from sparse urban GPS records.

(2020) studied meta-learning for cold-start prediction using limited trajectory data. Zheng et al. (2021) explored adaptive adjacency estimation from sparse urban GPS records.

text: Biomedical question answering requires high-recall retrieval and precise synthesis of evidence. Clinical language differs from general web text due to terminology and abbreviations. Existing datasets capture either consumer health or professional literature scenarios. Dense retrievers benefit from in-domain pretraining but face lexical mismatch issues. Sparse methods remain competitive when supervision is limited or noisy. We target long-form answer generation that cites specific supporting passages. Retrieval and generation must interact tightly to control hallucination and coverage. Prior work on datasets and rerankers informs our design choices. We summarize representative contributions in open-domain and biomedical IR. Roberts et al. (2020) released MS MARCO biomedical variants with passage-level relevance. Nogueira and Cho (2019) applied monoBERT reranking to open-domain QA with strong gains on passage retrieval. Seo et al. (2017) presented DrQA for coupling document retrieval and span extraction in a unified pipeline. Lin et al. (2019) studied neural re-ranking baselines and term-matching heuristics for reproducible IR experiments. MacAvaney et al. (2020) proposed domain-specific encoders for biomedical retrieval tasks. These lines of work motivate hybrid indexing that preserves exact terminology. We also incorporate citation-aware decoding to track which passages support each claim. Our method alternates retrieval with drafting to iteratively refine answers. The architecture includes a query reformulator tuned by passage-level feedback. We train on distant-supervised labels derived from abstracts and clinical guidelines. Evaluation covers recall at high depths and factual faithfulness in summaries. Results show improved grounding without sacrificing generation quality. We discuss limitations regarding bias in source corpora and domain shift.

(2017) presented DrQA for coupling document retrieval and span extraction in a unified pipeline. Lin et al. (2019) studied neural re-ranking baselines and term-matching heuristics for reproducible IR experiments. MacAvaney et al. (2020) proposed domain-specific encoders for biomedical retrieval tasks.

(2019) studied neural re-ranking baselines and term-matching heuristics for reproducible IR experiments. MacAvaney et al. (2020) proposed domain-specific encoders for biomedical retrieval tasks.

text: Fairness in recommender systems has emerged as a central research focus. Platforms mediate attention and opportunity, raising normative and legal concerns. Personalization can inadvertently disadvantage users or suppliers in systematic ways. Auditing outcomes requires clear definitions and reliable measurement protocols. Accuracy alone is insufficient to judge the social impact of algorithms. Stakeholders often pursue conflicting objectives that must be balanced explicitly. We study fairness constraints that operate at ranking time under budgeted changes. Our approach fits large catalogs while preserving real-time latency bounds. We ground our design in prior conceptual and empirical investigations. Burke et al. (2018) introduced multi-sided fairness to account for both users and providers in recommendation. Ekstrand et al. (2019) surveyed fairness risks in collaborative filtering and measurement pitfalls. Yao and Huang (2017) proposed metrics for disparate impact and mistreatment tailored to ranking settings. Chaney et al. (2018) analyzed feedback loops in recommenders that amplify popularity bias over time. Morik et al. (2020) studied exposure constraints to balance creator visibility online. These efforts highlight the need for metrics that reflect exposure as a resource. We formulate exposure as a flow variable constrained across groups and items. A dual decomposition solver yields efficient Lagrange multipliers for large batches. We couple the solver with a differentiable surrogate loss for training. Simulations on semi-synthetic data quantify trade-offs between utility and parity. An online A/B study assesses user satisfaction and provider engagement. Results indicate stable improvements in exposure equity with minimal click loss. We discuss governance implications and open challenges for cross-stakeholder accountability.
span_text: Burke et al. (2018) introduced multi-sided fairness to account for both users and providers in recommendation. Ekstrand et al. (2019) surveyed fairness risks in collaborative filtering and measurement pitfalls. Yao and Huang (2017) proposed metrics for disparate impact and mistreatment tailored to ranking settings. Chaney et al. (2018) analyzed feedback loops in recommenders that amplify popularity bias over time. Morik et al. (2020) studied exposure constraints to balance creator visibility online.

text: Automated program repair aims to localize and correct faults with minimal human effort. Traditional techniques rely on search or constraint solving over patch spaces. Learning-based approaches exploit patterns in historical fixes and code context. Large language models have accelerated progress by enabling free-form edits. Benchmarks evaluate repairs under compilability, tests, and behavioral similarity metrics. Despite gains, overfitting to tests and spurious patches remain prevalent. We investigate instruction-tuned models for multi-hunk bug fixing with provenance tracking. The method conditions on diagnostics, execution traces, and developer comments. Prior work on syntax correction and edit modeling provides valuable building blocks. Gupta et al. (2017) introduced DeepFix for syntax error correction in student programs. Tufano et al. (2019) modeled code changes with sequence-to-sequence networks trained on commits. Chen et al. (2021) presented Codex capable of translating natural language to code and performing edits across languages. Allamanis et al. (2018) surveyed machine learning for code and highlighted the role of learned representations. Le et al. (2020) explored fix patterns mined from large repositories. These references emphasize the spectrum from token-level to semantic changes. Our contribution is a verifier-guided decoding strategy that prunes untestable proposals. The verifier includes static checks and concolic execution for path coverage. We introduce a patch memory that conserves hypotheses across decoding steps. A counterexample buffer feeds back failing constraints to the generator. Experiments on Defects4J and QuixBugs test multi-hunk generalization and edit distance. The approach reduces overfitting while maintaining repair rate and code readability. We release prompts, patches, and failure cases to support future study.

text: This section reviews work on neural document summarization and contextual pretraining. Early systems focused on extractive strategies with sentence ranking and feature engineering. Graph-based centrality methods established baselines for multi-sentence selection. Neural approaches reframed summarization as conditional generation over long inputs. Pointer-generator networks bridged extractive and abstractive methods with copy mechanisms. Reinforcement learning aligned training objectives with evaluation metrics like ROUGE. Pretrained language models transformed summarization by supplying strong contextual priors. Cross-domain generalization remains a key challenge when transferring models to new genres. Smith et al. (2018) surveyed extractive summarization models across news corpora. Nallapati et al. (2017) introduced a sequence-to-sequence framework with pointer networks. Paulus et al. (2018) reported reinforcement learning to optimize ROUGE. Liu and Lapata (2019) presented BERTSUM for strong baselines. See et al. (2017) addressed repetition with coverage. Despite rapid progress, factual consistency and hallucination persist as major concerns. Recent studies connect entity planning to improved faithfulness by constraining decoding. Large instruction-tuned models offer promising zero-shot performance but are compute intensive. Domain adaptation with parameter-efficient fine-tuning reduces costs for practitioners. Controllable summarization integrates user-specified length and focus through constrained decoding. Our study investigates curriculum schedules that interleave extractive and abstractive signals. We hypothesize that stagewise mixing improves compression without sacrificing salience. We evaluate on news, science, and podcasts with automatic and human metrics. The results show gains in faithfulness and readability relative to strong pretrained baselines.

text: We review advances in object detection that motivate our unified training recipe. Early pipelines decomposed detection into proposal generation, feature extraction, and classification. Anchors and multi-scale pyramids improved coverage of object sizes across images. One-stage methods emphasized speed by eliminating external proposals while preserving localization quality. Redmon et al. (2016) proposed YOLO for real-time detection. Ren et al. (2015) introduced Faster R-CNN with region proposal networks. Lin et al. (2017) developed RetinaNet using focal loss. He et al. (2017) released Mask R-CNN extending detection to instance segmentation. Bochkovskiy et al. (2020) presented YOLOv4 for benchmarks. These architectures established a tradeoff between throughput and average precision that guides deployment. Subsequent work explored transformer backbones to capture long-range dependencies in dense scenes. Self-supervised pretraining closed the performance gap on limited annotations for specialized domains. Strong data augmentation and multi-scale training became standard components. Label assignment strategies were refined to stabilize learning with extreme class imbalance. Knowledge distillation allowed compact models to inherit accuracy from heavy teachers. Our approach targets reproducibility by standardizing schedules and reporting cost-aware metrics. We unify augmentation, assignment, and loss scaling into a simple baseline. The method scales from mobile settings to large backbones without per-model tuning. We present ablations that disentangle contributions of each component. Evaluation spans COCO, Objects365, and aerial imagery with consistent protocols. We also examine cross-dataset generalization under distribution shift. The results show competitive accuracy at favorable latency across hardware tiers.

text: This section reviews prior work on network anomaly detection relevant to our streaming design. Classic systems framed intrusion detection as classification over engineered flow features. Statistical approaches monitored deviations in link-level or host-level traffic measurements. Dimensionality reduction helped isolate subspaces with abrupt structural variations. Flow aggregation and sketching enabled fast processing on backbone links. Machine learning methods expanded coverage to previously unseen attack patterns. Deep architectures captured temporal dependencies in sequences of packets and flows. Public datasets remain limited in realism and evolve slowly over time. Evaluation protocols differ widely across studies, complicating direct comparison. Lakhina et al. (2004) used PCA to detect anomalies in network traffic matrices. Chandola et al. (2009) surveyed anomaly detection across domains. Ahmed et al. (2016) reviewed network anomaly detection techniques comprehensively. Ring et al. (2019) introduced a flow-based benchmark. Bhuyan et al. (2014) categorized intrusion detection methods systematically. More recent work leverages graph representations to incorporate topology and routing context. Unsupervised and self-supervised learning reduce reliance on brittle labels. Domain adaptation seeks robustness under shifting traffic mixes and encryption. Our approach combines sketches with contrastive pretraining for efficient detection. The method adapts online using memory-bounded prototypes of normal behavior. We propose a streaming benchmark with controlled drift and background churn. Comprehensive metrics report delay, false alarms, and operator effort. The results indicate improved sensitivity at fixed alert budgets in production traces.

(2014) categorized intrusion detection methods systematically.

text: We survey work on intelligent tutoring systems to position our adaptive feedback model. Research has explored how tutors represent knowledge and assess learner states. Early designs emphasized rule-based problem solvers with explicit skill hierarchies. Data-driven approaches now infer skills and misconceptions from interaction traces. Pedagogical strategies balance guidance with opportunities for productive struggle. Koedinger et al. (1997) analyzed cognitive tutors using model tracing. VanLehn (2011) synthesized results on tutoring effectiveness across modalities. Nye et al. (2014) reviewed intelligent tutoring systems for open-ended tasks. Graesser et al. (2005) developed AutoTutor for conversational learning. Chi et al. (2001) examined self-explanation strategies in problem solving. These lines of research inspired mechanisms for step-by-step hints and mastery learning. A central goal is estimating when to intervene and when to let practice continue. Bayesian knowledge tracing provided a probabilistic foundation for skill acquisition. Deep knowledge tracing extended capacity but raised questions of interpretability. Recent work incorporates offline policy evaluation to prevent harmful interventions. Multimodal sensing offers signals about affect and engagement during problem solving. Open learner models can increase metacognition by revealing progress and uncertainty. Our approach predicts hint helpfulness conditioned on student intent and context. We design counterfactual estimators to evaluate alternative tutor actions retrospectively. The framework integrates human-in-the-loop review for safety and transparency. We report experiments in algebra and programming with authentic classroom logs. Results indicate improved learning gains and reduced hint overuse across cohorts.

text: We review medical image segmentation methods that inform our boundary-aware approach. Early pipelines relied on atlas registration and deformable models to delineate anatomy. Convolutional networks replaced hand-crafted features with hierarchical representations. Skip connections improve localization by fusing low-level and high-level features. Loss functions beyond cross-entropy address class imbalance and shape priors. Data augmentation is crucial given scarce labeled volumes and inter-scanner variability. Multimodal fusion integrates complementary information from MR sequences or CT phases. Test-time adaptation mitigates domain shift across sites and protocols. Recent work studies uncertainty estimation for safety-critical predictions. Semi-supervised and self-supervised learning reduce annotation requirements substantially. Benchmarking practices emphasize standardized preprocessing and robust validation splits. Ronneberger et al. (2015) introduced U-Net for biomedical segmentation. Çiçek et al. (2016) extended the architecture to volumetric data. Milletari et al. (2016) proposed V-Net with Dice loss. Zhou et al. (2018) presented UNet++ with nested skip pathways. Isensee et al. (2018) released nnU-Net as a self-configuring framework baseline. These architectures motivate design choices for receptive field, depth, and normalization. Augmented training with elastic deformations and intensity jitter is standard practice. Our method targets fine boundaries through contour-aware supervision and hybrid losses. We integrate lightweight attention to focus features near ambiguous interfaces. Experiments span BraTS, ACDC, and LiTS with rigorous cross-validation. Results demonstrate improved Dice and Hausdorff metrics while preserving efficiency.

text: Recommender systems increasingly require explanations that align with user preferences and organizational policy. Recent advances in graph neural networks promise structured signals that can support faithful rationales. Yet the evidence base that links graph learning to user-understandable explanations remains fragmented. We review representative approaches to collaborative filtering and knowledge transfer to situate our contribution. Zhang et al. (2019) introduce neural graph collaborative filtering with message passing for top-N recommendation. Wang et al. (2020) exploit knowledge graphs to refine user-item connectivity using multi-hop reasoning. He and Chua (2017) present Neural Collaborative Filtering with implicit feedback signals. Rendle (2010) proposes Bayesian Personalized Ranking for pairwise learning. Kang and McAuley (2018) model sequential signals with self-attentive encoders for next-item prediction tasks. These strands motivate our exploration of path-based rationales that trace how messages propagate from entities to delivered items. Our method unifies attribution with calibration, yielding verifiable explanations while preserving competitive accuracy.

text: Vision-language models achieve strong accuracy yet often rely on spurious correlations that obscure causal reasoning. Understanding and correcting such shortcuts requires aligning learning objectives with interventional semantics. Pearl (2009) formalizes structural causal models and interventions for reasoning about counterfactuals. Goyal et al. (2019) propose counterfactual samples for visual question answering to mitigate language shortcuts. Niu et al. (2021) learn disentangled representations for vision-language compositionality. Cadene et al. (2019) introduce RUBi for bias reduction in multimodal classifiers. Clark et al. (2019) curate VQA-CP to assess generalization under shifted answer distributions and biases. These references underscore the fragmentation between causal principles and practical system design for deployed settings. We propose a targeted intervention module that perturbs cross-modal alignments at inference time. Our framework operationalizes do-calculus proxies without access to structural graphs during training. Experiments demonstrate improved out-of-distribution robustness on VQA-CP and compositional splits with minimal overhead.

text: Automatic speech recognition in low-resource domains remains vulnerable to domain shift between training and deployment. Background noise, speaker demographics, and recording hardware induce distributional drift that impairs acoustic and language models. Recent advances in self-supervised pretraining and unsupervised adaptation offer partial remedies but lack consistent evaluation. We focus on test-time strategies that adapt normalization and confidence without updating decoder parameters. To contextualize our design, we outline representative adaptations from the transfer learning literature. Pan and Yang (2010) survey transfer learning taxonomies across feature, instance, and parameter spaces. Ganin et al. (2016) align source and target distributions with domain-adversarial loss. Sun and Saenko (2016) match second-order statistics using CORAL. Tzeng et al. (2017) decouple representation learning and adaptation with discriminative objectives. Baevski et al. (2020) learn speech representations with wav2vec 2.0 pretraining on unlabeled audio at massive scale. Together these lines leave open questions about aligning latent speech features without labeled target transcriptions.

text: Assessing the value of training data is essential for auditing, debugging, and acquisition decisions. Practical methods must scale to modern datasets while retaining stable attributions across retraining. Existing estimators vary widely in their assumptions about model smoothness and loss decomposability. Koh and Liang (2017) use influence functions to estimate the effect of training points on predictions. Ghorbani and Zou (2019) define Data Shapley to attribute utility to individual examples. Wang et al. (2020) accelerate Shapley computations with tractable Monte Carlo schemes. Pruthi et al. (2020) demonstrate opportunistic data poisoning via word substitution. Feldman and Zhang (2020) analyze memorization in overparameterized networks under natural noise. This dispersion complicates fair pricing and selective data removal in compliance scenarios. We present a curvature-regularized influence estimator that remains accurate under mini-batch stochasticity. Our analysis connects robustness guarantees with computational budgets to guide deployment choices.

text: Large language models have transformed code generation yet remain brittle when specifications are partial or ambiguous. Developers seek controllable outputs that respect types, side effects, and security policies. Existing approaches balance prompt engineering with lightweight finetuning but lack consistent measures of adherence. We ground our study in instruction-following models while examining controllability through structured prompts. Brown et al. (2020) introduce GPT-3 with in-context learning for few-shot tasks. Ouyang et al. (2022) align language models with human feedback through instruction tuning. Chen et al. (2021) present Codex for code generation from natural language prompts. Nijkamp et al. (2022) release CodeGen with multilingual training data. Austin et al. (2021) study program synthesis under few-shot prompting with execution-based evaluation and resource constraints. These works leave open the question of enforcing API-level constraints without retraining. We introduce a constraint-aware decoding framework that preserves compilation and test coverage targets.
